points.subsection_title: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights
points.text: TVK's policy is that true social justice means every community receives proportional representation in the appropriate measure. To ensure this, a Caste Survey will be conducted.
points.title: 1 Proportional Representation

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Assembly passes resolution in favour of caste census
evidence.snippet: In June 2024, the Tamil Nadu Assembly passed a resolution urging the Union Government to conduct a caste-based census along with the decennial census.

plan_existence.notes: While Bihar has successfully conducted and released a caste survey, Tamil Nadu has only passed a legislative resolution urging the Centre to do so. The state government has not yet initiated its own independent survey under the Collection of Statistics Act.

legal.comments: The Supreme Court's refusal to stay the Bihar caste survey provides a strong legal precedent for states to conduct 'surveys' even if 'census' is a Union subject.

administrative.comments: Conducting a door-to-door survey for a population of over 72 million is a massive logistical exercise requiring significant manpower and data security infrastructure.

timeline.comments: A state-wide survey can be completed within 18-24 months, well within a 5-year term.

political.comments: There is high political demand for this data among major Dravidian parties and MBC groups like the Vanniyars.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible and legally viable following the Bihar precedent. The primary challenge is the administrative scale and ensuring the data withstands judicial scrutiny for reservation changes.
key_risks: Legal challenges regarding privacy
Data accuracy and manipulation concerns
Political backlash from communities perceiving under-representation

points.subsection_title: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights
points.text: A resolution will be passed in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly demanding the fair and full financial share due to Tamil Nadu from the taxes it contributes to the Union Government. Not stopping with merely passing the resolution, a Special Legal Committee will be constituted to take the fight up to the Supreme Court and legally argue for Tamil Nadu's financial share. Every rupee of revenue that should come to Tamil Nadu will be properly documented, and a report on the Union Government's injustice regarding Tamil Nadu's finances will be published. Furthermore, obtaining the fair financial share due to Tamil Nadu from the Union Government will be ensured.
points.title: 2 Demanding a Fair Share of Finances for Tamil Nadu

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 2 Demanding a Fair Share of Finances for Tamil Nadu
A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu advocates for 50% share in the Central tax devolution
evidence.snippet: Tamil Nadu's Finance Minister stated that the 16th Finance Commission must adopt a framework that incentivises performance and advocated for a 50% share.

plan_existence.notes: The current government already passes resolutions and petitions the Finance Commission for a higher share (50%). However, the specific promise of a 'Special Legal Committee' to litigate this in the Supreme Court is a new procedural escalation.

legal.comments: The Finance Commission is a constitutional body (Article 280), and its recommendations are generally discretionary for the President. Challenging the formula in the Supreme Court has limited precedent, though Kerala's suit on borrowing limits offers a narrow opening.

administrative.comments: Setting up a legal committee and publishing reports is well within state administrative capacity.

timeline.comments: Resolutions and committees can be established immediately upon taking office.

political.comments: Strong public support in Tamil Nadu for 'fiscal justice' and state rights.

feasibility.overall_comments: Administratively simple to initiate, but legally difficult to win. The Supreme Court rarely interferes in the complex fiscal formulas of the Finance Commission.
key_risks: Supreme Court may dismiss the petition as a policy matter
Strained relations with the Union Government
Lengthy litigation with no immediate fiscal gain

points.subsection_title: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights
points.text: The TVK government will stand firm in securing Tamil Nadu's finances from the Union Government. In particular, it will strongly insist that 70 percent of the total GST tax revenue be given to the states. A proper GST compensation amount will also be demanded so that states are relieved from revenue losses.
points.title: 3 Rightful and Fair Share for Tamil Nadu from GST Tax Revenue

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 3 Rightful and Fair Share for Tamil Nadu from GST Tax Revenue
A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights

evidence.title: Opposition-ruled states demand for new GST revenue share rule
evidence.snippet: Chhattisgarh suggested state GST at 70-80 per cent and central GST at 20-30 per cent from the present 50:50.

plan_existence.notes: Currently, GST is split 50:50 (CGST and SGST). While some reports suggest states effectively receive ~70% after devolution, the promise specifically demands a 70% direct share of total GST revenue, which is not the current law.

legal.comments: Requires an amendment to the GST Act and a 3/4th majority in the GST Council, where the Union Government holds a veto-capable 1/3rd vote share.

administrative.comments: Implementation would require a complete overhaul of the GST Network (GSTN) and accounting systems.

timeline.comments: Unlikely to be achieved in 5 years without a massive shift in national political consensus.

political.comments: Requires agreement from the Union and a majority of other states, many of which may not support a formula that favors high-consumption/manufacturing states like Tamil Nadu.

feasibility.overall_comments: Virtually infeasible for a single state to implement. It is a structural national reform that requires the Union Government to voluntarily surrender a massive portion of its revenue.
key_risks: Union Government veto in GST Council
Opposition from less-industrialized states
Potential collapse of the current federal fiscal balance

points.subsection_title: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights
points.text: It is because education (including medical education) is on the Union Government's common list that impositions such as the new education policy and the NEET exam controversy cause educational obstacles. Therefore, appropriate steps will be taken to move education (including medical education) from the Union Government's common list to the state list. In this, appropriate measures will be taken to bring a constitutional amendment in Parliament in coordination with like-minded parties. A legal battle will also be pursued by filing a review petition in the Supreme Court on this issue.
points.title: 4 Appropriate Action to Move Education to the State List!

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 4 Appropriate Action to Move Education to the State List!
A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Govt. seeks to transfer of education back to 'State List'
evidence.snippet: Education was moved from the State List to the Concurrent List by the 42nd Amendment Act of 1976. The TN CM has recently called for its return to the State List.

plan_existence.notes: This is a long-standing political demand in Tamil Nadu. Education is currently Entry 25 of the Concurrent List (List III) of the Seventh Schedule.

legal.comments: Requires a Constitutional Amendment under Article 368, necessitating a two-thirds majority in both Houses of Parliament and ratification by at least half of the State Legislatures.

administrative.comments: If achieved, the state has the infrastructure to manage its own education system, as it did prior to 1976.

timeline.comments: A constitutional amendment of this nature is a multi-year national political process with no guarantee of success.

political.comments: No national party currently supports moving education back to the State List, as it would diminish the Union's power to implement national policies like NEP.

feasibility.overall_comments: A high-level political aspiration with near-zero feasibility for a state-level party to execute unilaterally. It depends entirely on the arithmetic of the Union Parliament.
key_risks: Failure to secure 2/3rd majority in Parliament
Judicial review of any such amendment
Conflict with existing central laws like the NMC Act (for medical education)

points.subsection_title: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights
points.text: TVK strongly opposes the redrawing of parliamentary constituencies on the basis of population. To prevent South Indian states from being severely affected by delimitation, appropriate steps will be taken to bring a constitutional amendment.
points.title: 5 Protecting Tamil Nadu's Rights in Constituency Delimitation

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 5 Protecting Tamil Nadu's Rights in Constituency Delimitation
A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights

evidence.title: Delimitation debate: What is the actual impact of the upcoming exercise on South India representation?
evidence.snippet: The 84th Amendment (2002) extended the freeze on seat reallocation until after 2026. Tamil Nadu Chief Minister MK Stalin has called for political parties to unite against delimitation based on the 2026 Census.

plan_existence.notes: Delimitation is a mandatory constitutional process under Article 82. The current freeze on seats is based on the 1971 census. The promise seeks to amend the Constitution to prevent population-based seat reallocation that might disadvantage states with successful population control.

legal.comments: Constituency delimitation is a Union subject. A state government cannot unilaterally bring a constitutional amendment; it requires a two-thirds majority in both houses of Parliament and ratification by half the states.

administrative.comments: The state can pass resolutions and lobby the Union, but has no direct administrative control over the Delimitation Commission's mandate.

timeline.comments: The delimitation process is tied to the first census after 2026. Influencing a constitutional change within a 5-year term depends entirely on the national political landscape.

political.comments: This is a high-resonance issue in Tamil Nadu with broad consensus across regional parties, though it faces direct opposition from the Union government's current trajectory.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is a political stance on a federal issue rather than a state-executable scheme. Success depends on national-level coalition building and constitutional amendments beyond the state's direct power.
key_risks: Lack of legislative competence at the state level
Opposition from more populous Northern states
Constitutional requirement for a parliamentary supermajority

points.subsection_title: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights
points.text: TVK strongly opposes the Union Government's 'One Nation, One Election' plan, which weakens state autonomy and the federal principle. Therefore, appropriate efforts will be made to have the One Nation One Election draft bill withdrawn.
points.title: 6 Opposition to One Nation One Election

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 6 Opposition to One Nation One Election
A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights

evidence.title: One Nation, One Election - Wikipedia
evidence.snippet: The Constitution (129th Amendment) Bill, 2024 was introduced in Lok Sabha on 17 December 2024 to synchronise all elections. It was referred to a Joint Parliamentary Committee.

plan_existence.notes: The 'One Nation, One Election' is a central government proposal currently in the legislative pipeline. The TVK promise is to oppose this existing central initiative.

legal.comments: The state government can only pass a resolution in the Assembly; the power to withdraw or pass the bill lies solely with the Union Parliament.

administrative.comments: Passing a resolution and making 'efforts' to lobby against the bill is administratively simple for a state government.

timeline.comments: The bill is currently under review by a JPC (as of late 2024/2025); the state's opposition can be registered immediately upon taking office.

political.comments: Strongly aligns with regionalist sentiment in Tamil Nadu regarding state autonomy, though it puts the state in direct conflict with the Union's policy goals.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a reactive policy stance. While the state can formally record its opposition, the final outcome is determined by the Union Government and the Parliament of India.
key_risks: Limited constitutional power of the state to block central legislation
Potential for central-state friction
Dependence on national opposition unity

Tamil Nadu government stance on constituency delimitation 2026 South India seats

points.subsection_title: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights
points.text: The three-language education scheme which the Union Government is attempting to impose through the new education policy will severely harm Tamil Nadu's linguistic heritage and the quality of education. On behalf of TVK, a resolution will be passed in the Legislative Assembly opposing the Union Government's new education policy. A special inter-state committee will be formed to work jointly with other state governments that share this view. It will be urged that all states in India implement a two-language policy with their respective mother tongue and English as the link language.
points.title: 7 Safeguarding the Two-Language Policy

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights

evidence.title: NEP vs Tamil Nadu's SEP: Key differences in exams, language, and admissions
evidence.snippet: Tamil Nadu launched its own State Education Policy (SEP) in August 2025, which rejects the NEP's three-language formula and retains the two-language policy (Tamil and English).

plan_existence.notes: Tamil Nadu has officially followed a two-language policy since 1968. The current government has already established a State Education Policy (2025) to counter the NEP. The TVK promise to pass a resolution and form a committee is an expansion of this existing stance.

fiscal.comments: Passing a resolution and forming an inter-state committee has negligible fiscal impact on the state budget.

legal.comments: Education is on the Concurrent List. While the state can set its own policy, the Union can withhold funds (like Samagra Shiksha) if national guidelines are not met, which is already happening.

administrative.comments: The state has full administrative capacity to pass resolutions and form committees to coordinate with other states.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented immediately within the first few months of a new government.

political.comments: Language policy is a cornerstone of Tamil Nadu politics with near-unanimous public support for the two-language formula.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible as it continues a long-standing state consensus. The primary challenge is the potential loss of central education funding due to non-compliance with the NEP.
key_risks: Withholding of central education grants
Legal challenges to state-specific education policies
Difficulty in achieving consensus among other states for the inter-state committee

points.subsection_title: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights
points.text: On behalf of TVK, continued pressure will be placed on the Union Government to abolish the NEET exam.
points.title: 8 Abolition of NEET Exam

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu Admission to Undergraduate Medical Degree Courses Bill, 2021

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu govt moves SC against President withholding assent to NEET exemption bill
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu government moved the Supreme Court in November 2025 after the President withheld assent to the state's NEET exemption bill.

plan_existence.notes: NEET is a national-level exam. Tamil Nadu has repeatedly passed bills to exempt itself, but these require Presidential assent, which was recently withheld (March 2025). The matter is currently in the Supreme Court.

legal.comments: The Supreme Court has repeatedly upheld NEET's validity. Abolishing it requires an Act of Parliament or a reversal of multiple SC judgments, neither of which the state can do alone.

administrative.comments: The state can continue to pass resolutions and file petitions, but it cannot administratively stop the exam without central or judicial clearance.

timeline.comments: This is a decade-long legal battle with no clear resolution timeline, as it depends on the Union Government and the Judiciary.

political.comments: Extremely high political resonance; almost all major parties in TN support NEET abolition, making it a safe and popular manifesto promise.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is a continuation of an existing political and legal struggle. While politically potent, it remains legally stalled due to the Union's control over medical education standards.
key_risks: Judicial rejection by the Supreme Court
Refusal of Presidential assent for state bills
Conflict with National Medical Commission (NMC) regulations

points.subsection_title: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights
points.text: It will be insisted that the respective state language be used as the language of the court in High Courts.
points.title: 9 State Language in High Courts

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights

evidence.title: Tamil in Madras High Court: A long-pending demand
evidence.snippet: The demand for making Tamil the official language of the Madras High Court has been pending for decades, with the State Assembly passing a resolution in 2006.

plan_existence.notes: While the Tamil Nadu Assembly passed a resolution in 2006 to make Tamil the official language of the High Court, the proposal has been repeatedly stalled due to the requirement of the Chief Justice of India's concurrence and Presidential assent under Article 348(2).

legal.comments: Requires Presidential assent under Article 348(2) and the concurrence of the Supreme Court, which has historically resisted regional languages in High Courts to maintain judicial uniformity.

administrative.comments: Requires significant investment in translation services, legal terminology standardization in Tamil, and training for judicial staff.

timeline.comments: Highly dependent on the Union Government and the Supreme Court's administrative side; unlikely to be resolved within a single state term without central cooperation.

political.comments: Strong local political consensus in Tamil Nadu, but faces potential friction with the Union government's centralized judicial policy.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is legally complex as it hinges on federal and judicial approvals outside the state's direct control. While politically popular, the constitutional bottleneck under Article 348 makes implementation difficult.
key_risks: Rejection by the Supreme Court Collegium
Lack of Presidential assent
Challenges in translating complex legal precedents accurately

points.subsection_title: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights
points.text: On behalf of TVK, continued pressure will be placed on the Union Government to establish a bench of the Supreme Court in Chennai.
points.title: 10 Supreme Court Bench in Chennai

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights

evidence.title: Law Commission Report 229: Need for regional benches of the Supreme Court
evidence.snippet: The Law Commission recommended the setting up of regional benches of the Supreme Court in Delhi, Chennai/Hyderabad, Kolkata, and Mumbai to improve access to justice.

plan_existence.notes: Despite recommendations from the Law Commission and repeated demands from southern states, the Supreme Court Full Court has consistently rejected the proposal for regional benches.

legal.comments: Article 130 of the Constitution allows the CJI to appoint other places as seats of the SC with Presidential approval, but the SC has historically guarded its centralized nature.

administrative.comments: Requires significant physical infrastructure and administrative setup in Chennai, though feasible if the policy is cleared.

timeline.comments: A long-term institutional change that has been debated for decades; implementation within 5 years is highly unlikely given current judicial opposition.

political.comments: Strong regional support across South India; however, it requires the Union Government to override or persuade the judicial establishment.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a demand-based promise targeting the Union Government. The state government has no direct power to implement this, and the Supreme Court's consistent opposition remains the primary hurdle.
key_risks: Judicial resistance to decentralization
Constitutional interpretation of 'Seat of the Supreme Court'
Political inertia at the Union level

points.subsection_title: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights
points.text: TVK will secure Tamil Nadu's rightful share of Cauvery water as per the 2018 Supreme Court verdict. Immediate legal action will be taken against any obstacles created in obtaining Tamil Nadu's share of water. Measures will be taken to further strengthen Tamil Nadu's rights in the Cauvery Water Management Authority. Through continuous and systematic monitoring, we will not yield even a single drop of Tamil Nadu's rightful share of Cauvery water!
points.title: 11 Cauvery River Water Rights

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights

evidence.title: Cauvery Water Management Authority
evidence.snippet: The CWMA was constituted to give effect to the decision of the Cauvery Water Disputes Tribunal as modified by the Supreme Court in 2018.

plan_existence.notes: The legal framework and the 2018 Supreme Court verdict already define Tamil Nadu's share. The promise is essentially to ensure the strict enforcement of this existing legal reality.

legal.comments: The legal rights are already established by the 2018 Supreme Court verdict. The state's role is primarily litigation and representation in the CWMA.

administrative.comments: Tamil Nadu has a robust legal and technical team for monitoring water levels and representing the state in CWMA meetings.

timeline.comments: Water sharing is an annual, seasonal issue; while legal actions are immediate, 'securing' water depends on rainfall and Karnataka's compliance.

political.comments: High risk of inter-state conflict with Karnataka, which often resists water release during distress years, regardless of CWMA orders.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise reiterates the state's existing legal stance. While the legal foundation is strong, the actual delivery of water is subject to monsoon variability and political friction with Karnataka.
key_risks: Non-compliance by Karnataka during drought years
Limited enforcement powers of the CWMA
Climate change affecting river inflow

points.subsection_title: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights
points.text: In line with the Katchatheevu agreement made between the governments of India and Sri Lanka, TVK will sincerely and emphatically pressure the Union Government to take appropriate measures to reclaim and preserve the traditional fishing rights of Indian and Tamil Nadu fishermen in the Gulf of Mannar. It will be strongly urged with the Union Government to completely prevent Tamil Nadu fishermen from being arrested by the Sri Lankan Navy.
points.title: 12 Katchatheevu Fishing Rights

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights

evidence.title: Katchatheevu issue: India-Sri Lanka agreements
evidence.snippet: The 1974 and 1976 agreements settled the maritime boundary between India and Sri Lanka, placing Katchatheevu on the Sri Lankan side.

plan_existence.notes: Traditional fishing rights were restricted following the 1974 and 1976 maritime boundary agreements. Currently, there is no formal mechanism that allows Indian fishermen to fish in Sri Lankan waters around Katchatheevu.

legal.comments: The island was ceded via sovereign international treaties. The Supreme Court of India has previously noted that reclaiming it would require a constitutional amendment or renegotiation of a treaty.

administrative.comments: Requires complex diplomatic negotiation and coordination with the Ministry of External Affairs; the state government has no jurisdiction over international boundaries.

timeline.comments: Renegotiating a 50-year-old international treaty is a generational task, highly unlikely to be achieved in a 5-year term.

political.comments: High emotional resonance in Tamil Nadu, but implementation carries significant diplomatic risks for India-Sri Lanka relations.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a populist promise with extremely low feasibility. Reclaiming Katchatheevu or its fishing rights involves overturning international treaties, which is beyond the constitutional powers of a state government.
key_risks: Diplomatic fallout with Sri Lanka
Legal hurdles in the Supreme Court regarding treaty validity
Increased maritime tension and arrests if unilateral actions are taken

points.subsection_title: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights
points.text: On behalf of TVK, the Union Government will be urged to grant Indian citizenship to Sri Lankan Tamils who have migrated from Sri Lanka and are living in Tamil Nadu. Through this measure, it will be ensured that Sri Lankan Tamils receive all welfare schemes of the state government. Similarly, TVK will pressure the Union Government to take appropriate steps to protect the rights, cultural identity and possessions of Tamils living in Sri Lanka. TVK will continue to make appropriate efforts through the Union Government to eliminate ethnic discrimination prevailing in Sri Lanka.
points.title: 13 Justice for Sri Lankan Tamils

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights

evidence.title: Welfare of Sri Lankan Tamils - Government of Tamil Nadu
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu government provides various welfare schemes, including cash doles, clothing, and subsidized rice to Sri Lankan Tamils living in camps.

evidence.title: Citizenship (Amendment) Act, 2019
evidence.snippet: The CAA 2019 provides a path to citizenship for persecuted minorities from Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Afghanistan, but excludes Sri Lankan Tamils.

plan_existence.notes: While the TN state government provides extensive welfare to refugees in camps, the power to grant citizenship rests solely with the Union Government under the Seventh Schedule. The CAA 2019 currently does not include Sri Lankan Tamils.

legal.comments: Citizenship is a Union subject (Entry 17, List I). A state government has no legal authority to grant citizenship; it can only lobby the central government.

administrative.comments: The state already manages refugee camps and welfare distribution, so administrative machinery for identifying beneficiaries exists.

timeline.comments: Granting citizenship depends entirely on Union Government policy and potential legislative changes at the national level, making the timeline unpredictable.

political.comments: This is a highly popular stance in Tamil Nadu politics, supported by most regional parties, though it faces resistance from the current central administration.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is legally outside the state's jurisdiction as citizenship is a central power. While the state can provide welfare, the core demand for citizenship requires federal intervention.
key_risks: Constitutional conflict with Union Government
Exclusion from current CAA framework
Diplomatic implications with Sri Lanka

points.subsection_title: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights
points.text: TVK will continuously insist on abolishing the position of Governor, which continues as a vestige of colonial British India. Arrangements will be made for the state government itself to appoint Vice-Chancellors of universities.
points.title: 14 Action to Abolish Governor Appointments

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights

evidence.title: TN Assembly passes bills to empower State govt to appoint VCs
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly passed bills to empower the State government to appoint Vice-Chancellors to various universities, bypassing the Governor.

plan_existence.notes: The TN government has already passed legislation to take over VC appointments, but these bills often face delays in gubernatorial assent. Abolishing the Governor's post entirely is a constitutional matter.

legal.comments: Abolishing the post of Governor requires a Constitutional Amendment under Article 368, necessitating a two-thirds majority in Parliament and ratification by half the states.

administrative.comments: The state already has the administrative framework to manage universities; the shift in VC appointment authority is procedurally simple if legally cleared.

timeline.comments: Constitutional abolition of the Governor's post is highly unlikely within a 5-year term given the national political landscape.

political.comments: While popular among regionalist voters, it faces severe opposition from the Union government and requires national consensus.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise to abolish the Governor's post is constitutionally infeasible for a state government. The VC appointment aspect is a continuation of an ongoing legal and political struggle.
key_risks: Judicial strike-down of state-led VC appointments
Constitutional deadlock
Lack of parliamentary support for amendment

points.text: To proclaim and protect the history of Tamils, who possess a unique heritage and pride in world history, Tamil cultural sites will be restored at a cost of Rs. 500 crore. Traditional Tamil cultural arts including silambam, kummi, kolattam, and Thanjavur painting will be revived. International-level cultural exhibitions will be held to make the historical pride and cultural identities of Tamils famous worldwide.
points.title: 1 Budget for Tamil Heritage and Civilization

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 1 Budget for Tamil Heritage and Civilization

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Budget 2024-25: Focus on Culture and Archaeology
evidence.snippet: The state budget regularly allocates funds for the Department of Art and Culture and the restoration of heritage sites.

plan_existence.notes: The state already has departments for archaeology and culture. The specific Rs. 500 crore allocation and the focus on specific arts like Silambam represent an expansion of existing efforts.

fiscal.comments: The proposed Rs. 500 crore is well within the fiscal capacity of the Tamil Nadu state budget, which exceeds Rs. 3 lakh crore annually.

legal.comments: The state has full jurisdiction over 'Libraries, museums and other similar institutions' and 'Ancient and historical monuments' (Entry 12 and 67, List II/III).

administrative.comments: The Department of Art and Culture and the Tamil Nadu Tourism Development Corporation provide a ready mechanism for implementation.

timeline.comments: Restoration projects and cultural festivals can be executed within a 5-year term, though large-scale site restorations may take longer.

political.comments: Promoting Tamil identity and heritage is a core political consensus in the state with high public support.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a highly feasible and fiscally modest promise that aligns with existing state priorities and administrative capabilities.
key_risks: Quality control in heritage restoration
Ensuring funds reach traditional artists directly
Sustainability of international exhibitions

points.text: TVK will support the Archaeology Department in continuing excavations at 10 sites: Keezhadi, Adichanallur, Kodumanal, Mayiladumparai, Sivakalai, Azhagankulam, Arikamedu, Saluvankuppam, Kaveripattinam, and Poompuhar. Efforts will be made to obtain UNESCO World Heritage Site recognition for both Keezhadi and Adichanallur. To help future generations learn our history, school student tours to these sites will be organised at government expense.
points.title: 2 Archaeological Excavations

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu State Department of Archaeology Excavations

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Archaeology Department - Ongoing Excavations
evidence.snippet: The department is currently conducting Phase 9 and 10 of excavations at sites like Keezhadi and Adichanallur.

plan_existence.notes: Excavations at most of the named sites are already ongoing or have completed several phases. UNESCO recognition is a long-term process involving the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI).

fiscal.comments: Archaeological excavations and school tours are relatively low-cost activities that the state already funds annually.

legal.comments: The state has the power to conduct excavations, but UNESCO nominations must be routed through the Central Government (ASI).

administrative.comments: The TN State Department of Archaeology is highly active and has the technical expertise to continue these projects.

timeline.comments: Excavations and tours are ongoing; however, UNESCO World Heritage status typically takes 5-10 years for the nomination process.

political.comments: Archaeological pride is a major political theme in TN; there is no significant opposition to these activities.

feasibility.overall_comments: This promise largely continues existing state government work. The UNESCO goal is ambitious and depends on central cooperation, but the rest is easily achievable.
key_risks: Delays in UNESCO nomination by the Union Government
Preservation of artifacts during large-scale student tours
Technical challenges in underwater sites like Poompuhar

points.text: To proclaim the thousands of years of Tamil history, an international-class 'Tamil Civilization Museum' will be established in Madurai.
points.title: 3 'Tamil Civilization Museum' in Madurai

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Budget 2025-26: State to establish 'Museum of Languages'
evidence.snippet: Tamil Nadu will set up the 'Agaram – Museum of Languages' in Madurai to showcase Tamil's antiquity and evolution.

evidence.title: Keeladi Museum: Stalin inaugurates Sangam-era site near Madurai
evidence.snippet: The Keeladi Museum near Madurai is a world-class archaeological museum that showcases the sophisticated lifestyle of the ancient Tamils.

plan_existence.notes: The Tamil Nadu government has already announced the 'Agaram – Museum of Languages' for Madurai in the 2025-26 budget. Additionally, the world-class Keeladi Museum is located just 12km from Madurai, serving the same purpose of proclaiming Tamil civilization.

fiscal.comments: Funding for a language museum in Madurai was already allocated in the 2025-26 state budget.

legal.comments: No legal hurdles; state has full authority over cultural and heritage projects.

administrative.comments: The Department of Archaeology and the World Tamil Sangam have the capacity to execute this.

timeline.comments: Construction and curation of a world-class museum typically take 2-3 years; feasible within a 5-year term.

political.comments: Tamil identity and heritage projects enjoy unanimous political and public support in the state.

feasibility.overall_comments: This promise is highly feasible as it aligns with existing government projects and budget allocations. The infrastructure for such a museum is already being developed under the 'Agaram' project.
key_risks: Land acquisition delays within Madurai city
Curation quality and international standards maintenance

points.text: Folk Artists Livelihood Protection Scheme: To ensure that registered folk artists get 100 days of employment per year, opportunities to showcase their artistic skills will be created through government functions and events. Additionally, financial assistance of Rs. 15,000 will be provided to help them buy the necessary instruments.
points.title: 4 Folk Artist Welfare Improvement

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu Folk Artistes Welfare Board Schemes
Scheme of Extending Financial Assistance to Folk Artists

evidence.title: Scheme of Extending Financial Assistance to Folk Artists
evidence.snippet: The scheme provides a sum of ₹2,000 to individual folk artists for the purchase of musical instruments and costumes.

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Artists Social Security and Welfare Scheme 2006
evidence.snippet: Established the Tamil Nadu Artists Welfare Board to provide social security and welfare measures to registered artists.

plan_existence.notes: While a welfare board and instrument grants exist, the current grant is only ₹2,000 (occasionally ₹10,000 in special drives). The promise to increase this to ₹15,000 and provide a 100-day employment guarantee is a significant expansion of existing welfare.

legal.comments: The state can amend the Welfare Board rules to include employment guarantees and higher grants.

administrative.comments: Guaranteeing 100 days of employment specifically for 'artistic skills' through government events is extremely difficult to track and implement compared to manual labor schemes like MGNREGS.

timeline.comments: Grant increases can be immediate; the employment scheme would require a 1-2 year pilot phase.

political.comments: Strongly supported by the artist community, though it may face criticism if implementation is uneven.

feasibility.overall_comments: The financial grant is easily feasible, but the 100-day employment guarantee for artists is administratively complex and lacks a clear delivery mechanism for thousands of registered members.
key_risks: Ghost beneficiaries in the Welfare Board registry
Lack of enough government events to fulfill 100-day quotas
Corruption in the selection of artists for events

points.text: Young Artists Encouragement Scheme: To encourage emerging young folk artists, a grant of Rs. 25,000 per year will be given to 1,000 young artists.
points.title: Young Artists Encouragement Scheme

existing_scheme_names: Scheme of Encouragement to Young Artists

evidence.title: Scheme of Encouragement to Young Artists - myScheme
evidence.snippet: The scheme gives an opportunity to talented young artists to perform in cultural programmes... Remuneration in the range of ₹5,000 to ₹10,000 is paid.

plan_existence.notes: The existing scheme provides performance-based remuneration of up to ₹10,000. The manifesto promise proposes a fixed annual grant of ₹25,000 for a specific quota of 1,000 artists, which is a more structured financial support model.

fiscal.comments: The total annual cost is ₹2.5 crore (1,000 artists x ₹25,000), which is a negligible fraction of the state's culture budget.

legal.comments: No legal constraints; falls under state discretionary spending for arts.

administrative.comments: The Directorate of Art and Culture already manages similar schemes; scaling to 1,000 beneficiaries is straightforward.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented within the first year of a new term.

political.comments: Popular youth-centric welfare with no significant opposition.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a highly feasible, low-cost, and high-impact promise that builds upon existing administrative structures.
key_risks: Subjectivity in the selection process for the 1,000 slots
Ensuring equitable distribution across different folk art forms

points.text: Folk arts research and training centres will be established in Coimbatore (karagattam, kummi, oyilattam), Thanjavur (Bharatanatyam, therukoothu, parai), and Tirunelveli (silambam, kambattam), where free training will be provided to interested students.
points.title: 5 Folk Arts Research and Training Centres

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 5 Folk Arts Research and Training Centres

evidence.title: South Zone Cultural Centre, Thanjavur
evidence.snippet: SZCC is one of the seven zonal cultural centres established by the Government of India to preserve and promote traditional arts.

plan_existence.notes: While Thanjavur hosts the Central Government's South Zone Cultural Centre, there are no dedicated state-run 'Folk Arts Research and Training Centres' in Coimbatore or Tirunelveli with the specific art-form focus mentioned in the promise.

fiscal.comments: Setting up three regional centers requires moderate capital expenditure for buildings and recurring costs for trainers.

legal.comments: No legal issues; state has the mandate to promote local arts and education.

administrative.comments: Can be managed by the Department of Art and Culture or affiliated with local universities (e.g., Tamil University, Thanjavur).

timeline.comments: Establishing research-grade centers with faculty and infrastructure will likely take 3-4 years.

political.comments: Promoting regional art forms (like Silambam in Tirunelveli) is politically popular and culturally significant.

feasibility.overall_comments: A well-conceived plan that leverages regional artistic strengths. The inclusion of Bharatanatyam (a classical dance) under a 'folk arts' center in Thanjavur is a slight categorical mismatch but feasible.
key_risks: Difficulty in finding qualified academic researchers for folk traditions
Low student enrollment if not linked to formal certification or jobs

points.text: The following schemes will be implemented to revive, encourage and develop Tamil Nadu's traditional sports: *A Tamil Nadu Rural Olympics series will be held to take Tamil traditional sports to the global stage. *Rs. 5 lakh financial assistance will be given by the government to conduct jallikattu events. Rs. 10 lakh will be given to the family of a bull-tamer who dies during jallikattu. Similarly, Rs. 2 lakh will be given to an injured participant. Further, measures will be taken to promote jallikattu as an international sport. *Rekla racing grounds will be built in districts such as Coimbatore and Salem where rekla races are predominantly held. *Rs. 5 crore will be provided annually to conduct silambam competitions at the international level. One thousand silambam training centres will be established across Tamil Nadu.
points.title: 6 Traditional Sports Development Scheme

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu CM Trophy 2024
evidence.snippet: The CM Trophy includes various traditional sports and modern sports conducted at district and state levels.

evidence.title: Compensation for Jallikattu victims
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu government currently provides ex-gratia payments (typically Rs. 3 lakh) to families of those deceased in Jallikattu events.

plan_existence.notes: The promise expands existing financial aid (increasing Jallikattu death compensation from ~3L to 10L) and formalizes infrastructure like Rekla grounds which are currently informal.

fiscal.comments: The manifesto specifies Rs. 5 crore for Silambam and specific Jallikattu payouts. With hundreds of Jallikattu events annually, the Rs. 5 lakh per event grant would cost several crores, which is manageable within the sports budget.

legal.comments: Jallikattu and Rekla races are protected under the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals (Tamil Nadu Amendment) Act, 2017, upheld by the Supreme Court.

administrative.comments: The Sports Development Authority of Tamil Nadu (SDAT) has the existing framework to conduct state-wide competitions and manage training centers.

timeline.comments: Construction of grounds and increasing pension/compensation amounts can be achieved within the first 1-2 years.

political.comments: Traditional sports are a high-priority cultural issue in Tamil Nadu with broad public support across party lines.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible as it builds on existing cultural infrastructure and increases current compensation rates. The fiscal burden is clearly defined and relatively low compared to major welfare schemes.
key_risks: Animal welfare litigation
Safety management at high-risk events
Ensuring equitable distribution of Silambam centers

points.text: A special scheme will be introduced with the aim of making Thirukkural a functional part of every student's life. Students who fully learn 300 kurals will be given cash prizes and educational scholarships; those who fully learn all 1,330 kurals will be given global cultural experience, state-level recognition and long-term educational support. Further, to build leadership rooted in Tamil heritage values, Thirukkural will be introduced as a special subject in schools and business education institutions.
points.title: 7 Thirukkural Development Scheme

evidence.title: Prizes for students reciting 1330 Kurals
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Development Department provides a cash prize of Rs. 10,000 to students who recite all 1330 couplets of Thirukkural.

plan_existence.notes: Existing schemes provide one-time cash prizes for recitation. This promise expands this into a structured scholarship and introduces it as a 'special subject' in higher and business education.

fiscal.comments: No specific outlay is mentioned for the scholarships or global cultural experience, making fiscal impact assessment difficult.

legal.comments: The state has the authority to modify school curricula and state-funded university syllabi under the State List.

administrative.comments: The School Education Department and Tamil Development Department can implement this through existing school networks.

timeline.comments: Curriculum changes and scholarship rollouts can be completed within 2 academic cycles.

political.comments: Thirukkural is a non-controversial, universally respected text in Tamil Nadu, ensuring zero political opposition.

feasibility.overall_comments: The plan is culturally popular and administratively simple, but the lack of defined scholarship amounts prevents a full fiscal feasibility score.
key_risks: Academic load on students
Standardization of 'leadership' curriculum
Cost of global cultural experience programs

points.text: To honour the sacrifice of the language struggle martyrs, the annual pension given to their families will be increased to Rs. 2,50,000 each. To remember the language struggle martyrs, speech and essay competitions will be held for students.
points.title: 8 Pensions for Language Struggle Martyrs

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Language Martyrs Pension Increase
evidence.snippet: In 2023, the pension for Tamil language martyrs was increased to Rs. 6,000 per month plus a dearness allowance.

plan_existence.notes: The current pension is approximately Rs. 72,000 per year (plus DA). The promise to increase it to Rs. 2,50,000 per year is a nearly 3.5x increase.

fiscal.comments: The number of beneficiaries (families of language martyrs) is very small (estimated under 500). Increasing the pension to Rs. 2.5 lakh per year would cost the state less than Rs. 15-20 crore annually, which is negligible in the state budget.

legal.comments: Executive orders are sufficient to increase pension amounts for this category.

administrative.comments: The Tamil Development and Information Department already has the list of beneficiaries and the payment mechanism.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented immediately via a Government Order (GO).

political.comments: Honoring language martyrs is a core tenet of Dravidian politics; this will be widely welcomed.

feasibility.overall_comments: Extremely feasible and low-cost. It is a high-impact symbolic move with minimal fiscal strain.
key_risks: Verification of legal heirs for older cases

points.text: With the arrival of AI technology, every field has adapted accordingly. However, AI technology is based largely on English. Designing and using this technology in Tamil has become a necessity of the times. Therefore, a dedicated Tamil AI model will be developed in partnership with leading universities. This service will be provided free of charge to all our country's people. It will be provided to foreigners on the basis of a commercial licence.
points.title: 9 Tamil Artificial Intelligence

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu to develop its own LLM
evidence.snippet: The TN government announced in 2024 a project to develop a Tamil Large Language Model (LLM) with an allocation of Rs. 25 crore.

plan_existence.notes: The government has already initiated a Tamil LLM project. The manifesto promise adds a commercial licensing model for foreigners and emphasizes university partnerships.

fiscal.comments: The current government has already allocated Rs. 25 crore for a similar project. Scaling this and maintaining it is well within the state's IT budget.

legal.comments: Intellectual property rights and licensing frameworks for AI are complex but manageable through standard government-university contracts.

administrative.comments: TNeGA (Tamil Nadu e-Governance Agency) and ELCOT have the capacity to oversee this, especially in partnership with institutions like IIT Madras.

timeline.comments: Developing a high-quality, specialized AI model takes significant time (2-4 years) for training, fine-tuning, and deployment.

political.comments: Promoting Tamil in the digital age is a strong political narrative that aligns with 'Tamil Pride'.

feasibility.overall_comments: Technically ambitious but grounded in existing state policy. The commercial licensing aspect for foreigners is a novel revenue-generating idea.
key_risks: Data privacy and bias in AI
High compute costs for model training
Competition from global tech giants' multilingual models

points.text: Sufficient funds will be allocated to create digital archives to protect Tamil Nadu's art, culture and heritage. Under the Tamil Nadu Digital Museum project, efforts will be made to document Tamil Nadu's history, culture and heritage through AR (Augmented Reality), VR (Virtual Reality) and Hologram technology.
points.title: 10 Digital Archives of Tamil Heritage

evidence.title: Virtual Reality museum on Tamil history to come up in Chennai
evidence.snippet: Tamil Virtual Academy is planning to create a Virtual Reality museum to bring life to the past using multimodal visualisation technology at a cost of 2.7 crore.

evidence.title: Kalaignar Memorial - Digital Museum
evidence.snippet: The museum, opened in February 2024, features 3D Holography, Augmented Reality, and VR zones to document the life of M. Karunanidhi.

plan_existence.notes: The state has already inaugurated high-tech digital museums (Kalaignar and Amma memorials) and the Tamil Virtual Academy is currently implementing a VR museum project for Tamil history. The promise expands this to 10 specific archives.

fiscal.comments: Previous similar projects like the TVA VR portal were tendered at approximately 2.7 crore; scaling to 10 archives would require an outlay of roughly 30-50 crore, which is well within the state's discretionary heritage budget.

legal.comments: No legal hurdles; falls under the state's power to promote culture and education.

administrative.comments: The Tamil Virtual Academy and ELCOT have existing experience in digital archiving and VR implementation.

timeline.comments: Digital museums can be developed and launched within 12-24 months per site.

political.comments: Promoting Tamil heritage is a core consensus issue in Tamil Nadu politics with high public reception.

feasibility.overall_comments: The project is highly feasible as the technology and administrative framework are already in place. It represents a logical expansion of existing digital heritage initiatives.
key_risks: High maintenance costs for VR/AR hardware
Content accuracy and curation quality
Technological obsolescence

points.text: TVK's 'Tamil Nadu Responsible Tourism' is presented as a joyful and creative tourism scheme that treats tourism not merely as entertainment but includes eco-tourism, heritage tourism, coastal tourism and cultural tourism. 25 percent of the revenue from this will be spent on improving the livelihoods of the respective local people.
points.title: 1 Tamil Nadu Responsible Tourism

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Tourism Policy 2023
evidence.snippet: The policy focuses on increased revenues for local economy and support to local communities but does not specify a 25% revenue sharing mandate.

plan_existence.notes: While the 2023 Policy emphasizes 'Responsible Tourism' and local community support, the specific mandate to spend 25% of tourism revenue directly on local livelihoods is a new fiscal proposal.

fiscal.comments: Allocating 25% of gross tourism revenue to local livelihoods is a massive fiscal commitment that could deplete funds needed for infrastructure maintenance and marketing.

legal.comments: Requires a clear legal framework to define 'revenue' and 'local people' to avoid misappropriation.

administrative.comments: Extremely difficult to track and distribute revenue at a hyper-local level (village/town) fairly without leakages.

timeline.comments: Policy can be drafted quickly, but the distribution mechanism would take years to stabilize.

political.comments: Highly popular with local communities in tourist hubs like Ooty or Kanyakumari, but may face pushback from state finance departments.

feasibility.overall_comments: The 25% revenue sharing promise is fiscally aggressive and administratively complex. While it aligns with global sustainable tourism goals, the specific percentage is likely a populist figure that would be hard to implement without a dedicated local government tourism tax.
key_risks: Fiscal deficit in the tourism department
Corruption in local distribution
Ambiguity in defining 'local' beneficiaries

points.text: The following initiatives will be implemented to attract tourists by centering Tamil culture: Folk art performances will be held every evening at 10 major tourist sites. Rural tourism centres providing a village life experience will be created. Night markets selling handicrafts made by local artisans will be set up near all cultural tourism sites. A Tamil Cultural Festival will be held during tourist seasons including the summer holidays.
points.title: 2 Archaeological Excavations

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu's artisans development scheme to cover 25 trades
evidence.snippet: The Kalaignar Kaivinai Thittam provides support to artisans across 25 trades including pottery and weaving.

evidence.title: Namma Ooru Thiruvizha to promote folk arts
evidence.snippet: The state government organizes folk art performances to support traditional artists.

plan_existence.notes: The state already organizes 'Namma Ooru Thiruvizha' for folk arts and has launched the 'Kalaignar Kaivinai Thittam' for artisans. Night markets and permanent rural tourism centers are mentioned as objectives in the 2023 policy but are not yet ubiquitous.

fiscal.comments: Organizing festivals and setting up markets are relatively low-cost compared to major infrastructure; can be funded via existing tourism and MSME budgets.

legal.comments: No legal barriers; aligns with existing state mandates for cultural promotion.

administrative.comments: The Department of Tourism and the Art and Culture Department have the machinery to execute these events.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented within the first 2 years of a term.

political.comments: Strong support from the rural electorate and the artist community.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a highly feasible and practical set of initiatives that builds on existing successful programs like Namma Ooru Thiruvizha. It focuses on soft infrastructure and livelihood support.
key_risks: Seasonal nature of tourism
Ensuring consistent quality of folk performances
Competition from commercial markets

Tamil Nadu Responsible Tourism policy 25 percent revenue local people
Tamil Nadu night markets handicrafts local artisans scheme
TTDC Arupadai Veedu Tourism Scheme Shaivite Vaishnavite circuits

points.text: A Tamil Nadu Spiritual Tourism scheme will be developed along four major spiritual routes: *The Arupadai Veedu Tourism Scheme covering the spiritual sites of Palani, Thirupparamkunram, Thiruchendur, Swamimalai, Thiruthani and Pazhamudircholai. *The Shaivite Temples Tourism Scheme covering Chidambaram, Tiruvannamalai, Kumbakonam and Thiruvaiyaru. *The Vaishnavite Holy Sites Tourism Scheme covering Srirangam, Thirukoshtiyur and Alwar Thirunagari. *The Inter-faith Harmony Tourism Scheme covering holy sites of all our religions, including Nagore Dargah, Velankanni and Kanyakumari.
points.title: 3 Spiritual Tourism Scheme

evidence.title: 4 DAYS ARUPADAI VEEDU TOUR - TTDC
evidence.snippet: TTDC already offers a 4-day Arupadai Veedu tour covering the six abodes of Lord Murugan.

evidence.title: TTDC Spiritual Tour Packages
evidence.snippet: Official TTDC site lists packages for Navagraha temples, Divya Desams, and major Shaivite sites.

plan_existence.notes: The Tamil Nadu Tourism Development Corporation (TTDC) already operates specific tour packages for Arupadai Veedu, Shaivite, and Vaishnavite circuits. The 'Inter-faith' route is a common travel circuit but could be more formally branded.

fiscal.comments: Most of the infrastructure (temples, roads, hotels) already exists. The cost is primarily for branding, marketing, and specialized transport services, which are self-sustaining through ticket sales.

legal.comments: No legal issues; the state already manages these sites through the HR&CE department and TTDC.

administrative.comments: TTDC has decades of experience running these exact spiritual circuits.

timeline.comments: Can be 'launched' as a new scheme immediately as the routes are already active.

political.comments: Spiritual tourism is a major revenue generator and has broad public support across all religious groups.

feasibility.overall_comments: This promise is essentially a rebranding of existing TTDC services. It is 100% feasible because it is already being done, though it could benefit from better integration and 'circuit' branding.
key_risks: Overcrowding at major sites
Maintaining inter-faith harmony in sensitive areas
Infrastructure pressure during peak festival seasons

points.text: Rest houses for pilgrims will be built at Tiruvannamalai, Madurai, Rameswaram, Palani, Velankanni and Nagore — major spiritual worship centres for all religions.
points.title: 4 Rest Houses for Pilgrims

evidence.title: HOTEL AALAYAM - Tiruvannamalai - TTDC
evidence.snippet: TTDC operates Hotel Aalayam and Hotel Tamil Nadu in major pilgrim destinations like Tiruvannamalai and Rameswaram.

plan_existence.notes: The Tamil Nadu Tourism Development Corporation (TTDC) and the HR&CE Department already maintain guest houses and Yatri Niwas in these spiritual hubs. This promise suggests an expansion of low-cost or dedicated pilgrim-specific rest houses.

legal.comments: State government has full authority to build and manage public accommodation on state-owned or temple-owned land.

administrative.comments: TTDC and HR&CE have established mechanisms for constructing and managing such facilities.

timeline.comments: Construction of multiple rest houses can be completed within a 5-year term.

political.comments: Highly popular across all religious demographics as it targets major multi-faith spiritual centres.

feasibility.overall_comments: A standard infrastructure expansion project that is highly feasible and aligns with existing state tourism and religious endowment goals.
key_risks: Land acquisition in crowded temple towns
Maintenance and hygiene standards
Competition with private budget hotels

points.text: At cultural sites such as Keezhadi, Adichanallur, Kodumanal and Gangaikondacholapuram, the Tamil urban civilisation experience will be recreated for tourists through AR (Augmented Reality) and VR (Virtual Reality).
points.title: 5 Tamil Cultural Tourism Scheme

evidence.title: Experience 6th century BC at new Keezhadi museum
evidence.snippet: Visitors can take a virtual tour of the excavations undertaken in Keeladi and enjoy facilities for playing traditional Tamil sports on touchscreens.

plan_existence.notes: The Keezhadi museum already incorporates VR/AR elements. This promise expands the 'digital experience' model to other major archaeological sites in the state.

legal.comments: No legal hurdles; the state archaeological department has jurisdiction over these sites.

administrative.comments: Requires specialized tech vendors, but the successful implementation at Keezhadi serves as a proven template.

timeline.comments: Digital recreations can be developed and deployed within 2-3 years.

political.comments: Strongly aligns with Tamil identity politics and cultural pride, likely to receive widespread support.

feasibility.overall_comments: Technologically modern and culturally significant; builds on existing successful museum models in the state.
key_risks: High maintenance costs for VR hardware
Accuracy of historical recreations
Internet connectivity in rural excavation sites

points.text: Tax concessions will be given to private owners who convert Chettinad houses, Nilgiri bungalows and similar heritage properties reflecting Tamil culture and tradition into guest houses. Additionally, the government will provide a 50 percent subsidy towards the cost of restoring such heritage buildings.
points.title: 6 Tamil Heritage Stays

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Tourism Policy 2023
evidence.snippet: The policy provides for capital subsidy up to 25% (max 1.5 crore) for tourism projects including heritage hotels.

plan_existence.notes: The current 2023 policy offers a 25% capital subsidy. The promise to increase this to 50% and add specific tax concessions for heritage properties is a significant enhancement of the existing framework.

fiscal.comments: A 50% restoration subsidy is exceptionally high for private property and would place a significant burden on the state exchequer given the high cost of heritage restoration.

legal.comments: Tax concessions on GST require GST Council approval; state-level concessions are limited to SGST reimbursement or property tax.

administrative.comments: Verifying 'heritage' status and monitoring restoration quality requires a specialized heritage committee.

timeline.comments: Policy can be notified quickly, though actual restoration projects take years.

political.comments: Appeals to elite property owners and promotes cultural preservation, but may be criticized as a subsidy for the wealthy.

feasibility.overall_comments: Ambitious fiscal incentive that doubles existing subsidies; implementation depends heavily on the state's ability to fund such high-percentage grants.
key_risks: Misuse of funds for non-heritage renovations
Fiscal sustainability of 50% subsidy
Legal challenges regarding selective tax concessions

points.text: A coastal cruise tourism route will be created along Tamil Nadu's long coastline from Vedaranyam to Rameswaram-Mandapam. The respective local fishermen themselves will serve as tour guides in their areas and as mariners taking tourists out to sea. Through this, their revenue loss during the fishing ban period will be prevented. Rameswaram-Dhanushkodi will be declared as Tamil Nadu's first coastal tourism district. Efforts will be made to start a boat service between Rameswaram and Talaimannar in Sri Lanka.
points.title: 7 Tamil Nadu Coastal Cruise Tourism

evidence.title: India looks to launch second ferry service to Sri Lanka
evidence.snippet: Efforts are being made to expand the ferry initiative to the Rameswaram-Talaimannar route, with a DPR of 150 crore submitted to the Union Shipping Ministry.

plan_existence.notes: While the Rameswaram-Talaimannar ferry is an active bilateral discussion (Central Govt), the specific 'Coastal Cruise Route' using fishermen as guides and the declaration of a 'Coastal Tourism District' are new state-level proposals.

fiscal.comments: Requires significant investment in jetties and terminals; ferry terminal cost is estimated at 150 Cr by IIT Madras.

legal.comments: International boat services and maritime routes require mandatory clearance from the Ministry of External Affairs and Ministry of Shipping (GoI).

administrative.comments: Training fishermen as certified mariners and guides requires a large-scale skill development program.

timeline.comments: Domestic cruise routes are feasible in 5 years; international ferry depends on bilateral diplomatic speed.

political.comments: Strongly supports fishermen's welfare during ban periods, which is a key electoral issue in coastal TN.

feasibility.overall_comments: A complex multi-jurisdictional project that combines livelihood support with high-end tourism; success depends on Central Government cooperation.
key_risks: Diplomatic delays with Sri Lanka
Safety and liability issues with fishermen-led tours
Environmental impact on sensitive coastal zones

points.text: 15 trekking trails will be identified at Ooty, Coonoor, Gudalur, Kotagiri and Mudumalai, and the Nilgiri Mountain Adventure Tourism Scheme will be implemented. A Nilgiris Mountain Biking Festival will be held every year in an eco-friendly manner. In a government–private partnership, paragliding stations will be set up at three locations in Ooty. Facilities for kayaking on the Moyar river will be arranged.
points.title: 8 Nilgiri Mountain Adventure Tourism

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Tourism Policy 2023
evidence.snippet: The policy identifies 12 priority tourism segments including Adventure and Eco-tourism, aiming to attract investments of ₹20,000 crore.

evidence.title: 5 Things to Do in Ooty for the Adventure Lover
evidence.snippet: Trekking, hang gliding, and mountain biking already exist in Ooty as activities, but not under a unified 'Nilgiri Mountain Adventure Tourism Scheme'.

plan_existence.notes: While adventure activities like trekking and paragliding already occur in the Nilgiris, there is no specific 'Nilgiri Mountain Adventure Tourism Scheme' or a formalized annual Mountain Biking Festival run by the state government. The promise seeks to institutionalize these activities.

fiscal.comments: The use of Public-Private Partnerships (PPP) for paragliding stations significantly reduces the direct fiscal burden on the state. Identifying trekking trails is a low-cost administrative task.

legal.comments: Activities in Mudumalai and trekking trails in the Nilgiris require strict clearances from the Forest Department and compliance with the Wildlife Protection Act, 1972, and Eco-Sensitive Zone (ESZ) regulations.

administrative.comments: The Tamil Nadu Tourism Development Corporation (TTDC) has experience in managing hill station tourism; however, coordinating with the Forest Department for 15 trails requires high inter-departmental synergy.

timeline.comments: Identifying trails and launching a festival can be achieved within 1-2 years. PPP paragliding stations may take 3 years for bidding and setup.

political.comments: High public and local business support as it boosts the local economy in a major tourism hub.

feasibility.overall_comments: The plan is highly feasible as it builds on existing natural assets and utilizes PPP models for infrastructure. The primary challenge is navigating environmental and forest conservation laws in the sensitive Western Ghats region.
key_risks: Environmental degradation due to increased footfall in eco-sensitive zones
Strict Forest Department regulations delaying trail approvals
Safety risks associated with adventure sports requiring high-standard regulation

points.text: Tamil Nadu's first caravan tourism policy will be created. Under this, beautiful landscapes across 20 tourism zones throughout Tamil Nadu will be selected, and caravan stops with drinking water, internet, electricity connections and toilet facilities will be set up. Entrepreneurs and self-help groups coming forward to set up these caravan stops will be given Rs. 2 lakh financial assistance.
points.title: 9 Caravan Tourism

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Tourism Policy 2023 - Focus Segments
evidence.snippet: Caravan tourism is listed as one of the 12 priority themes in the 2023 policy, but a dedicated standalone 'Caravan Tourism Policy' with specific SHG grants is not yet active.

plan_existence.notes: The 2023 Tourism Policy recognizes Caravan tourism as a priority segment, but the promise to create the 'first caravan tourism policy' and provide specific ₹2 lakh grants to SHGs represents a new, targeted implementation phase.

fiscal.comments: A ₹2 lakh grant per stop is a modest fiscal commitment. Even with 100 stops across 20 zones, the total outlay would be only ₹2 crore, which is well within the tourism department's budget.

legal.comments: No major legal hurdles; requires standard land use permissions and transport department permits for caravans.

administrative.comments: Setting up basic amenities (water, internet, toilets) is straightforward. Managing SHG disbursements is a routine task for the TNCDW (Tamil Nadu Corporation for Development of Women).

timeline.comments: Policy creation and initial stop setup can be completed within 12-18 months.

political.comments: Strong political appeal as it promotes self-employment and modernizes the tourism sector.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a highly practical and low-cost promise that leverages existing SHG networks to create a new tourism niche. It aligns perfectly with current national and state tourism trends.
key_risks: Maintenance and hygiene of caravan stops
Security concerns at remote caravan locations
Low initial adoption rate by tourists

points.text: To taste the traditional cuisines of Tamil Nadu, the Chettinad Food Tourism Trail (Karaikudi, Kanadukathan, Devakottai), Kongu Food Tourism Trail (Coimbatore, Erode, Salem) and Tirunelveli Food Tourism Trail will be created. An international food festival will be held to showcase our state's proud food culture.
points.title: 10 Food Tourism Trails

evidence.title: Embark on a food trail across Tamil Nadu
evidence.snippet: While food trails are popular among private travel groups and food critics, there is no official government-sanctioned 'Food Tourism Trail' scheme.

plan_existence.notes: The concept of food trails exists informally and through private tourism. The promise to create official government-branded trails and an international festival is a new initiative to formalize culinary tourism.

fiscal.comments: Marketing and branding trails are low-cost. An international food festival would require a significant one-time annual outlay (approx. ₹5-10 crore), likely offset by sponsorships.

legal.comments: No legal impediments; primarily involves marketing and event management.

administrative.comments: Requires coordination between the Tourism Department and local municipal bodies to ensure hygiene and infrastructure at designated food stops.

timeline.comments: Trails can be mapped and marketed within a year; the festival can be an annual fixture starting from year one.

political.comments: Promotes regional pride and local businesses, making it politically popular across different districts.

feasibility.overall_comments: A very feasible and culturally resonant promise. It effectively uses existing culinary reputation to drive economic growth without requiring massive infrastructure investment.
key_risks: Maintaining food safety and hygiene standards at all trail locations
Commercialization potentially diluting traditional culinary practices
Ensuring equitable benefit to small local vendors vs large hotels

points.text: River-based tourism will be developed along three major river routes: the Cauvery river route from Hogenakkal Falls to Srirangam island, the Madurai Vaigai route, and the Tamirabarani river route from Papanasam to Tirunelveli.
points.title: 11 River-based Tourism

evidence.title: Rs 374 crore allocated for major riverfront development projects
evidence.snippet: In February 2026, the TN government allocated ₹374 crore for riverfront development in Tiruchirappalli, Madurai, Erode, Coimbatore, and Tirunelveli covering Cauvery, Vaigai, and Thamirabarani.

plan_existence.notes: The promise directly aligns with the existing Riverfront Development Project announced in the 2026 Interim Budget. It specifies the exact routes (e.g., Hogenakkal to Srirangam) which adds a layer of tourism-specific planning to the existing infrastructure/sewage treatment focus.

fiscal.comments: The ₹374 crore already allocated in the 2026 budget provides a strong foundation. Expanding this to full-scale tourism routes will require additional funding for jetties and safety equipment.

legal.comments: Requires environmental clearances from the State Environmental Impact Assessment Authority (SEIAA) and compliance with River Zone regulations.

administrative.comments: Requires coordination between Public Works Department (PWD), Water Resources Department (WRD), and Tourism Department.

timeline.comments: Riverfront development is time-consuming due to seasonal water flow variations and complex civil works; 5 years is a tight but possible window for completion.

political.comments: High political value as it rejuvenates iconic rivers central to Tamil culture and identity.

feasibility.overall_comments: This promise is highly credible as it is already backed by a budget allocation. The focus on specific routes like Hogenakkal to Srirangam makes it a logical extension of urban riverfront development into a broader tourism circuit.
key_risks: Seasonal water scarcity affecting boat-based tourism
Pollution and sewage management challenges in urban stretches
High maintenance costs for riverfront infrastructure

points.text: Twelve-week training courses covering hospitality, food preparation, tour guiding, water sports safety management and art skill development will be introduced. Employment will be guaranteed to those who complete these courses successfully.
points.title: 12 Tourism Skill Development Scheme

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu Tourism Skill Development Drive

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Tourism Launches Major Skill Development Drive
evidence.snippet: Tamil Nadu Tourism has launched a large-scale skill development initiative in collaboration with KITTS to enhance the capabilities of its tourism workforce.

evidence.title: Naan Mudhalvan - TNSDC
evidence.snippet: A flagship skill development program by the TN government aiming to upskill 10 lakh youth annually across various sectors including hospitality.

plan_existence.notes: While skill development in tourism exists through TNSDC and the 2023 Tourism Policy, the specific 12-week duration and the 'guaranteed employment' clause represent a more formal and binding expansion of current placement-linked training.

legal.comments: State has full authority to implement vocational training and employment schemes under the concurrent list.

administrative.comments: TNSDC and the Department of Tourism already have the infrastructure to roll out specialized courses; however, guaranteeing employment requires private sector MoUs.

timeline.comments: 12-week courses can be designed and launched within the first year of a term.

political.comments: Job creation and skill development are universally popular and face no significant political opposition.

feasibility.overall_comments: The plan is highly feasible as it leverages existing skill development frameworks (TNSDC). The only challenge is the 'guaranteed employment' promise, which typically depends on market demand rather than government mandate.
key_risks: Saturation of the tourism job market
Quality control of 12-week short-term training
Difficulty in enforcing employment guarantees with private hospitality partners

points.text: A cinema tourism trail covering famous shooting locations throughout Tamil Nadu will be created. Arrangements will be made for visitors to learn about the connections between these places and their respective films through QR codes.
points.title: 13 Cinema Tourism

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Tourism Policy 2023
evidence.snippet: The policy identifies 'Film Tourism' as a niche sector but does not specify a dedicated QR-code based trail across the state.

plan_existence.notes: While the 2023 Tourism Policy recognizes film tourism, a structured 'Cinema Tourism Trail' with QR-coded information at shooting locations is a new, specific implementation idea.

fiscal.comments: Low-cost initiative involving signage and digital content creation; well within state budget capacity.

legal.comments: May require permissions from private property owners where films were shot, but generally no major legal hurdles.

administrative.comments: TTDC can easily manage the mapping of locations and digital content hosting.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented within 1-2 years through a phased rollout at major hubs like Ooty, Chennai, and Madurai.

political.comments: Capitalizes on the massive popularity of Tamil cinema; likely to be very well received by the public.

feasibility.overall_comments: A highly practical and low-cost way to boost tourism by leveraging the state's strong cinematic heritage. It is a modern, tech-savvy approach to destination marketing.
key_risks: Maintenance of QR code signage at remote locations
Intellectual property issues with film clips/stills
Vandalism of physical markers

points.text: To enhance safety features for tourists, infrastructure and basic facilities at tourist sites will be improved. In particular, qualified operators, coastal lifeguards, tourist police and 24-hour help centres will be provided.
points.title: 14 Tourism Safety

evidence.title: Marina Lifeguard unit inaugurated in Chennai
evidence.snippet: The Marina Lifeguard unit was inaugurated in 2022 with the aim of preventing accidental drowning of tourists.

evidence.title: Tourist Police in Tamil Nadu
evidence.snippet: Tamil Nadu has a dedicated Tourist Police wing operating at major heritage and transit points since the early 2000s.

plan_existence.notes: Tourist police and beach lifeguards already exist in major hubs like Chennai and Mahabalipuram. The promise suggests a statewide expansion and infrastructure upgrade of these existing services.

fiscal.comments: Requires recurring expenditure for salaries and equipment, but manageable within the Home and Tourism department budgets.

legal.comments: No legal constraints; falls under the state's mandate for law and order and public safety.

administrative.comments: Requires coordination between the Police Department and Tourism Department to scale up specialized units.

timeline.comments: Expansion of existing units can be achieved incrementally over a 5-year term.

political.comments: Safety is a non-controversial priority that enhances the state's image for international and domestic travelers.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a logical expansion of existing safety infrastructure. The focus on 24-hour help centers and coastal lifeguards addresses specific gaps in current tourist safety protocols.
key_risks: Inter-departmental coordination delays
Staffing shortages in specialized police units
High maintenance costs for coastal rescue equipment
