points.text: Creating a poverty-free Tamil Nadu is TVK's primary objective. Poor families in Tamil Nadu will be identified, and it will be ensured that all government welfare schemes reach them. Further, appropriate employment opportunities will be created for them. Opportunities for self-employment will also be created. Through these measures, their minimum annual income will be ensured.
points.title: 1 Poverty-Free Tamil Nadu

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu State Rural Livelihood Mission (TNSRLM)

evidence.title: CM's Thayumanavar Scheme to tackle poverty
evidence.snippet: Launched in 2024, this scheme aims to rescue 5 lakh of the poorest families from poverty through integrated government assistance.

plan_existence.notes: The state already has a dedicated mission (Thayumanavar Scheme) to identify and uplift the poorest 5 lakh families. The promise to 'ensure minimum annual income' adds a potential Universal Basic Income (UBI) or income-supplement component to existing welfare.

legal.comments: State has broad powers to implement welfare and income support schemes.

administrative.comments: Identifying the 'poorest' is complex and prone to inclusion/exclusion errors, though the PIP (Participatory Identification of Poor) process is already in use.

timeline.comments: A 5-year window is sufficient for identification and initial rollout, but 'eradicating' poverty is a long-term structural challenge.

political.comments: Poverty alleviation is the core of Dravidian politics and carries massive electoral weight.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is a broad vision that aligns with existing state missions. The 'minimum annual income' guarantee is the most significant addition, but its feasibility cannot be assessed without a specific monetary target or budget outlay.
key_risks: Extreme fiscal burden if income guarantee is high
Inaccurate identification of beneficiaries
Dependency on welfare over sustainable job creation

points.text: Eligible households will be given 200 units of free electricity every month.
points.title: 2 Free Electricity

evidence.title: TANGEDCO - Tariff for Domestic Consumers
evidence.snippet: The Government of Tamil Nadu provides 100 units of free electricity to all domestic consumers every two months (billing cycle).

plan_existence.notes: Tamil Nadu currently provides 100 units of free electricity to domestic consumers. This promise proposes doubling that limit to 200 units per month.

fiscal.comments: TANGEDCO's accumulated losses exceed ₹1.5 lakh crore. Doubling the free unit quota from 100 to 200 would significantly increase the state's subsidy burden, which was already over ₹10,000 crore annually for the existing 100-unit scheme.

legal.comments: The state government has the statutory power to provide subsidies under the Electricity Act, 2003, provided it pays the subsidy amount in advance to the utility.

administrative.comments: The billing infrastructure for free units is already in place via TANGEDCO; adjusting the software for 200 units is a simple administrative task.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented immediately via a Government Order (GO) and tariff notification.

political.comments: Highly popular measure with significant voter appeal, though it may face criticism regarding the financial health of the state utility.

feasibility.overall_comments: Technically and administratively simple to implement as it expands an existing mechanism, but fiscally dangerous given the massive debt of the state power utility.
key_risks: Increased fiscal deficit
Further degradation of TANGEDCO's financial health
Potential for reduced investment in power infrastructure

points.text: 100 percent clean drinking water supply will be ensured to all homes in Tamil Nadu.
points.title: 3 Drinking Water for All

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu Combined Water Supply Schemes

evidence.title: Jal Jeevan Mission - Tamil Nadu Dashboard
evidence.snippet: Tamil Nadu has achieved over 80% coverage of Functional Household Tap Connections (FHTC) as of 2024 under the Jal Jeevan Mission.

plan_existence.notes: The goal of 100% coverage is the stated objective of the ongoing Jal Jeevan Mission (Central-State partnership).

fiscal.comments: Funding is largely secured through the Jal Jeevan Mission (50:50 center-state) and state-funded TWAD board projects. Significant capital is already allocated.

legal.comments: No legal hurdles; providing water is a primary state responsibility under the Directive Principles.

administrative.comments: Reaching the last 10-15% in remote or hilly areas is administratively and engineering-wise challenging.

timeline.comments: Achievable within a 5-year term given that the state is already at high coverage levels.

political.comments: Universal water supply is a non-controversial, high-priority political goal.

feasibility.overall_comments: A highly feasible continuation of existing national and state goals, with the main challenge being the 'last mile' connectivity in difficult terrains.
key_risks: Depleting groundwater levels
Maintenance of existing infrastructure
Water quality monitoring in rural areas

points.text: Five lakh new houses will be built so that poor and underprivileged people in rural areas can own homes.
points.title: 4 Housing for All

evidence.title: Kalaignar Kanavu Illam Scheme - TN Budget 2024
evidence.snippet: The TN government launched 'Kalaignar Kanavu Illam' to build 8 lakh concrete houses in rural areas to replace huts by 2030.

plan_existence.notes: The promise of 5 lakh houses fits within or complements the existing state target of 8 lakh houses under the Kalaignar Kanavu Illam scheme.

fiscal.comments: At an average cost of ₹3.5 lakh per house, 5 lakh houses would require ₹17,500 crore. This is a significant but manageable outlay if spread over 5 years.

legal.comments: No legal impediments; housing schemes are standard welfare activities.

administrative.comments: The Rural Development and Panchayat Raj Department has established mechanisms for beneficiary identification and fund disbursement.

timeline.comments: Building 1 lakh houses per year is consistent with previous performance in Tamil Nadu.

political.comments: Housing is a high-impact political promise with strong rural resonance.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible as it aligns with existing state machinery and targets, provided the fiscal allocation is maintained.
key_risks: Rising cost of construction materials
Beneficiary selection bias
Quality control of construction

points.text: Government pattas will be issued to all those living on government poramboke lands (provided there are no objections). MGR patta documents currently held by people living in hill areas including Kalrayan will be properly computerised.
points.title: 5 Regularising Land Pattas

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Revenue Department - Land Records
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nilam portal provides computerized access to land records (Patta/Chitta). Regularisation of poramboke land is an ongoing process subject to classification.

plan_existence.notes: While general computerization exists, specific focus on 'MGR pattas' in hill areas like Kalrayan (Kallakurichi/Salem districts) addresses localized administrative gaps in land titling.

fiscal.comments: Administrative costs for computerization and survey are relatively low compared to infrastructure projects.

legal.comments: Regularising poramboke land often faces legal hurdles, especially if the land is classified as water bodies, grazing land, or forest land (prohibited by Madras High Court rulings).

administrative.comments: Surveying hill areas and resolving decades-old land disputes requires significant revenue department manpower and modern GIS tools.

timeline.comments: Computerization is feasible in 5 years, but legal regularisation of disputed poramboke lands often takes much longer due to litigation.

political.comments: Land ownership is a sensitive and powerful political issue for tribal and rural poor populations.

feasibility.overall_comments: The computerization aspect is highly feasible, but the actual issuance of pattas for poramboke land is constrained by strict judicial oversight on land classification.
key_risks: Judicial stay on poramboke regularisation
Conflict with Forest Conservation Act in hill areas
Inaccurate historical records for MGR-era pattas

points.text: Priority will be given to the elderly, persons with disabilities and marginalised people by delivering ration items to their homes. To prevent anaemia and vitamin A and B-12 deficiencies among the public, iron and folic acid-enriched rice, vitamin A and vitamin D-enriched cooking oil, and fortified wheat flour will be distributed through ration shops to everyone. To prevent short-weighing, poor quality, adulteration and smuggling of ration items, all ration items other than rice will be distributed in packets, ensuring people get quality goods at fair weights. Strict quality checks will be conducted at every stage of the public distribution system. One ration shop will be established for every 500 ration cards. Sufficient weighers will be appointed in every rural ration shop.
points.title: 6 Public Distribution System Reform

existing_scheme_names: Centrally Sponsored Pilot Scheme on Fortification of Rice

evidence.title: CM Stalin to launch doorstep delivery of PDS commodities for elderly and disabled
evidence.snippet: Tamil Nadu Chief Minister M.K. Stalin will launch a new welfare initiative on August 12, providing doorstep delivery of essential commodities to senior citizens above 70 years of age and persons with disabilities across the state.

plan_existence.notes: Tamil Nadu launched the 'Thayumanavar Thittam' in August 2025 for doorstep delivery to elderly (65+) and PwDs. Fortified rice is already being distributed in several districts under a central mandate. The promise expands this to all commodities and mandates packet distribution.

legal.comments: PDS management and welfare delivery are within the state's executive and legislative powers.

administrative.comments: Thayumanavar Thittam provides a working model for doorstep delivery. Packet distribution for all items and increasing shop density are the main logistical hurdles.

timeline.comments: Can be fully implemented within a 5-year term given the existing pilot success.

political.comments: Highly popular welfare measure with minimal opposition risk.

feasibility.overall_comments: The plan is highly feasible as it builds on the existing 'Thayumanavar Thittam' and central fortification mandates. The primary challenge lies in the logistical scale-up of packet distribution and ensuring quality control during home delivery.
key_risks: Logistical failure in doorstep delivery
Cost of fortification and packaging
Leakage during home delivery
Resistance from existing ration shop staff

points.subsection_title: B. Welfare of Adi Dravidars and Tribal Communities
points.text: The funds allocated in the budget for the welfare of Adi Dravidar-Tribal and marginalised communities will be used entirely for their development. This fund will never be used for other development schemes.
points.title: 1 Social Justice Budget

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: B. Welfare of Adi Dravidars and Tribal Communities

existing_scheme_names: Development Action Plan for Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes Bill, 2024

evidence.title: TN govt passes Bill to monitor SC/ST sub plan funds
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu government on February 22, passed a new Bill that will govern how the special funds meant for Dalit and Adivasi welfare—the Scheduled Caste Sub Plan (SCSP) and Scheduled Tribe Sub Plan (TSP) funds—are spent in the state.

plan_existence.notes: The state passed a monitoring bill in 2024, but it has been criticized for allowing 'non-divisible infrastructure' spending from SC/ST funds. This promise aims to close those loopholes and ensure zero diversion.

legal.comments: Requires legislative amendment to the 2024 Act to explicitly forbid diversion and tighten accounting rules.

administrative.comments: Requires robust accounting and real-time tracking to prevent fund reappropriation across departments.

timeline.comments: Legislative changes can be enacted quickly within the first year of the term.

political.comments: Strong support from SC/ST groups; may face internal departmental resistance to ring-fencing funds.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a necessary legislative refinement to address gaps in the 2024 SC/ST monitoring bill. It is politically strong and administratively possible with better accounting transparency.
key_risks: Bureaucratic resistance to fund ring-fencing
Potential for unspent funds if specific projects aren't identified
Difficulty in defining 'marginalised' for non-SC/ST groups

points.subsection_title: B. Welfare of Adi Dravidars and Tribal Communities
points.text: Adi Dravidar and Tribal students pursuing professional education, medicine, law and management will be given a scholarship of Rs. 40,000 per year.
points.title: 2 Adi Dravidar Scholarship

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: B. Welfare of Adi Dravidars and Tribal Communities

evidence.title: Post-matric scholarships for Adi Dravidar students
evidence.snippet: Post-matric scholarships are available for Adi Dravidar, Tribal, and Adi Dravidar Christian students for further studies after SSLC. The scholarship covers tuition fee and maintenance allowance.

plan_existence.notes: Existing Post-Matric Scholarships provide maintenance allowances, but they are generally lower (approx. Rs. 8,000-12,000) than the proposed Rs. 40,000. This represents a significant increase in the financial support per student.

legal.comments: The state has full authority to set and increase scholarship amounts for its own welfare schemes.

administrative.comments: DBT systems are already in place for scholarship disbursement in Tamil Nadu.

timeline.comments: Can be rolled out in the first academic cycle of the new term.

political.comments: Highly popular with youth and marginalized communities; low political risk.

feasibility.overall_comments: A substantial increase in student support that is administratively simple to deliver via DBT. However, the lack of enrollment data prevents a full fiscal assessment.
key_risks: Fiscal sustainability if student numbers spike
Delays in DBT disbursement
Verification of professional course eligibility

points.subsection_title: B. Welfare of Adi Dravidars and Tribal Communities
points.text: Under the Scheduled Caste Entrepreneur Scheme, first-generation entrepreneurs from Adi Dravidar and Tribal communities will be given Rs. 5 lakh as investment capital.
points.title: 3 Adi Dravidar Entrepreneur Scheme

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: B. Welfare of Adi Dravidars and Tribal Communities

existing_scheme_names: Chief Minister Adi Dravidar and Tribal Socio-Economic Development Scheme (CM-ARISE)

evidence.title: Annal Ambedkar Business Champions Scheme (AABCS) - myScheme
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu government provides a 35% capital subsidy (up to Rs 1.5 crore) and 6% interest subvention for SC/ST entrepreneurs.

evidence.title: CM-ARISE Scheme - TAHDCO
evidence.snippet: Provides financial assistance to SC/ST individuals with a 35% subsidy or Rs. 3.50 lakhs, whichever is lower, for starting business activities.

plan_existence.notes: The Government of Tamil Nadu already operates several schemes for SC/ST entrepreneurs. The CM-ARISE scheme specifically offers a subsidy of up to Rs. 3.5 lakh. The manifesto promise of Rs. 5 lakh as investment capital represents a 42% increase over the current CM-ARISE subsidy cap and specifically targets first-generation entrepreneurs.

fiscal.comments: The current CM-ARISE scheme already has a budget for 3.5L subsidies. Increasing this to 5L for first-generation entrepreneurs is a moderate fiscal expansion. While specific outlays for this new cap aren't cited, the state already allocates hundreds of crores to TAHDCO and MSME subsidies (e.g., AABCS).

legal.comments: Welfare schemes targeting SC/ST communities are constitutionally valid under Articles 15(4) and 16(4). No legal impediments exist for state-funded grants or subsidies for these groups.

administrative.comments: The administrative machinery (TAHDCO and the MSME Department) is already fully functional and experienced in processing applications, verifying community status, and disbursing entrepreneurship subsidies.

timeline.comments: This can be implemented within the first year of a term by amending the existing CM-ARISE or AABCS guidelines to include the higher 5L cap for first-generation applicants.

political.comments: SC/ST welfare is a core political priority in Tamil Nadu. There is likely to be zero opposition to increasing entrepreneurship support for these communities.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is highly feasible as it is an incremental improvement on existing, well-established state schemes. It leverages existing delivery mechanisms (TAHDCO) and addresses a specific gap (first-generation entrepreneurs) with a manageable increase in financial assistance.
key_risks: Verification of 'first-generation' status to prevent misuse
Potential duplication with the existing AABCS scheme which offers higher total subsidies
Ensuring bank credit linkage if the 5L is a subsidy rather than a direct grant

points.subsection_title: B. Welfare of Adi Dravidars and Tribal Communities
points.text: Loans taken by Adi Dravidar people from the Tamil Nadu Adi Dravidar Housing and Development Corporation will be fully waived.
points.title: 4 TAHDCO Loan Waiver

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: B. Welfare of Adi Dravidars and Tribal Communities

evidence.title: TAHDCO - Tamil Nadu Adi Dravidar Housing & Development Corporation
evidence.snippet: TAHDCO implements various economic development schemes providing subsidies for bank loans to SC/ST individuals.

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu CM Waives Rs 6000 Crore Cooperative Bank Loans
evidence.snippet: The state government has previously waived cooperative bank gold loans and SHG loans, but no specific waiver for TAHDCO loans is currently active.

plan_existence.notes: While the Tamil Nadu government has a history of waiving cooperative and SHG loans, there is no current active scheme specifically for waiving loans issued or facilitated by TAHDCO. TAHDCO primarily provides subsidies for bank-linked loans, so a waiver would require the state to settle outstanding dues with commercial or cooperative banks.

legal.comments: The state government has full authority to waive loans issued by its own corporations or to provide grants to settle bank loans taken by specific communities.

administrative.comments: TAHDCO maintains records of beneficiaries; however, since many loans are bank-linked, coordination with multiple financial institutions would be required to execute a full waiver.

timeline.comments: A loan waiver can be implemented via a government order (GO) and budgetary allocation within the first year of a term.

political.comments: Highly popular among the target demographic; follows the precedent of previous successful loan waivers in the state.

feasibility.overall_comments: Feasible as a one-time welfare measure, provided the state allocates sufficient funds to cover the outstanding principal and interest. The primary challenge is the lack of public data on the total outstanding loan volume specifically under the TAHDCO portfolio.
key_risks: Fiscal burden on the state exchequer
Moral hazard affecting future repayment behavior
Complexity in waiving bank-linked loans vs direct corporation loans

points.subsection_title: B. Welfare of Adi Dravidars and Tribal Communities
points.text: Permanent housing and pattas will be provided to all Adi Dravidar and Tribal people in Tamil Nadu.
points.title: 5 Adi Dravidar and Tribal Housing Scheme

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 5 Adi Dravidar and Tribal Housing Scheme
B. Welfare of Adi Dravidars and Tribal Communities

existing_scheme_names: Iyothee Thass Pandithar Habitation Development Scheme

evidence.title: T.N. Budget 2024 | 'Tholkudi' to upgrade basic amenities in tribal habitations
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu government announced the 'Tholkudi' scheme with an outlay of ₹1,000 crore to provide permanent houses and amenities in tribal settlements.

evidence.title: Free housing pattas distributed to 3.09 lakh beneficiaries
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu government distributed free housing pattas to about 3.09 lakh people of the Adi Dravidar and tribal communities.

plan_existence.notes: Multiple schemes already exist to provide housing and land titles (pattas) to SC/ST communities. The manifesto promise aims for universal coverage ('all'), which represents a significant scaling up of existing departmental targets.

legal.comments: Land acquisition and patta distribution are within state powers, though forest land rights for tribals involve the Forest Rights Act (Central).

administrative.comments: Identifying land, resolving ownership disputes, and constructing houses for the entire SC/ST population is a massive administrative undertaking prone to delays.

timeline.comments: Providing permanent housing to 'all' is unlikely to be completed within a single 5-year term given the current housing deficit.

political.comments: Strong political appeal as it addresses core issues of dignity and asset ownership for marginalized groups.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is an ambitious expansion of existing successful schemes. While the legal framework exists, the administrative and fiscal scale required for universal coverage makes it a long-term goal rather than a short-term deliverable.
key_risks: Scarcity of available government land for patta distribution
High cost of construction materials and labor
Legal hurdles in regularizing settlements in forest or restricted areas

points.subsection_title: B. Welfare of Adi Dravidars and Tribal Communities
points.text: Atrocities against Adi Dravidar and Tribal people will be fully prevented. A dedicated police wing will be set up under the supervision of the Superintendent of Police in each district.
points.title: 6 SC/ST Atrocity Prevention

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: B. Welfare of Adi Dravidars and Tribal Communities

existing_scheme_names: Social Justice and Human Rights (SJ&HR) Wing

evidence.title: Social Justice and Human Rights unit acts as the SC/ST Protection Cell
evidence.snippet: The SJ&HR wing, formed in 1972, has units in each of Tamil Nadu's 38 districts headed by a Deputy Superintendent of Police (DSP) to monitor SC/ST Act cases.

plan_existence.notes: A dedicated wing (SJ&HR) already exists with district-level units. However, these are currently headed by DSPs. The promise to put them under the 'supervision of the Superintendent of Police' in each district suggests either a new dedicated SP post per district or a higher level of direct accountability than the current structure.

fiscal.comments: The cost of restructuring or adding supervisory roles within the existing police force is relatively low compared to welfare schemes.

legal.comments: Police is a state subject. The state can reorganize its police wings and create new supervisory roles without central approval.

administrative.comments: Requires internal police department restructuring. The mechanism for district-level units already exists, making the transition feasible.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented within 1-2 years through administrative orders and personnel transfers.

political.comments: Addresses a critical demand from Dalit activists regarding the perceived ineffectiveness of current lower-rank investigations.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible as it builds upon an existing institutional framework. Elevating the rank of supervision could improve the quality of investigations and the rate of charge-sheeting in atrocity cases.
key_risks: Potential resistance within the police hierarchy to rank-based restructuring
Risk of the new wing becoming a symbolic change without actual increase in field-level personnel
Need for specialized training for the dedicated wing to be effective

points.text: The TVK government will work with dedication for the social and economic advancement of all people. Therefore, we will urge the Union Government to implement the recommendations of the Sachar Committee. Through this, we will ensure proper representation for minorities in education and employment.
points.title: 1 Sachar Committee Recommendations Will Be Implemented

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 1 Sachar Committee Recommendations Will Be Implemented

existing_scheme_names: Prime Minister's New 15-Point Programme for Minorities

evidence.title: Implementation of Recommendations of Sachar Committee
evidence.snippet: The Central Government accepted 72 out of 76 recommendations of the Sachar Committee in 2007, with implementation spread across various ministries.

plan_existence.notes: The Sachar Committee recommendations were primarily for the Union Government. While some have been implemented (like scholarship schemes), many structural recommendations regarding representation remain unfulfilled. The promise is to 'urge' the Union and implement state-level measures.

legal.comments: Implementing reservation-related recommendations at the state level may face legal challenges regarding the 50% cap, though Tamil Nadu already has a 69% quota system.

administrative.comments: State-level implementation of education and employment representation is feasible, but 'urging the Union' is a diplomatic/political action with no guaranteed administrative outcome.

timeline.comments: Full implementation depends on Union Government cooperation, which is unlikely to be resolved within a 5-year state term.

political.comments: Strongly appeals to the Muslim minority; however, it may face opposition from groups concerned about changes to existing reservation quotas.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is primarily a political advocacy stance. While the state can implement some socio-economic measures, the core recommendations require Union action, making the feasibility of 'full implementation' low for a state government.
key_risks: Dependence on Union Government's political will
Legal challenges to any new sub-quotas or representation models
Potential for communal polarization during implementation

points.text: Schemes for minority education will be implemented within a planned time frame. Skill development centres will be set up in every district. Colleges for minority women will be opened through the Wakf Board. The process for obtaining the financial assistance needed to start education and businesses will be simplified.
points.title: 2 Minority Education and Entrepreneurship Contribution

evidence.title: A BRIEF NOTE ON TAMCO
evidence.snippet: TAMCO provides loans at low interest to Minorities for Self-Employment, including Term Loans up to Rs. 30 lakh and Education Loans.

evidence.title: M.S.S. Wakf Board College - Wikipedia
evidence.snippet: Muqayyath Sha Sirguro Wakf Board College in Madurai is a Muslim minority institution and the only college in India run by the Wakf Board.

plan_existence.notes: Tamil Nadu already has a dedicated corporation (TAMCO) for minority loans and a nodal agency (TNSDC) for skill development. One Wakf Board college exists in Madurai; the promise to open more specifically for women in every district is an expansion.

fiscal.comments: Setting up colleges in every district requires significant capital expenditure. TAMCO already manages substantial loan portfolios (e.g., Rs. 6.72 crore in Coimbatore alone), suggesting existing fiscal channels can be scaled.

legal.comments: The state has the authority to establish educational institutions and manage the Wakf Board under the Wakf Act, 1995.

administrative.comments: Existing infrastructure like TNSDC and the Minorities Welfare Department provides a strong foundation for implementation.

timeline.comments: Opening colleges in every district is a multi-year infrastructure project that may exceed a single 5-year term for full statewide coverage.

political.comments: Minority welfare is a core political pillar in Tamil Nadu with broad support across major Dravidian parties.

feasibility.overall_comments: The plan is highly feasible as it builds on existing institutions like TAMCO and the Wakf Board. The primary challenge is the fiscal and logistical scale of opening new colleges in every district.
key_risks: High capital cost for new colleges
Ensuring quality of training in decentralized skill centers
Potential delays in land acquisition for Wakf institutions

points.text: The TVK government will create legal and policy frameworks to protect minority-run public educational and charitable medical institutions.
points.title: 3 Minority Institutions Will Be Protected

existing_scheme_names: Article 30 of the Constitution of India

evidence.title: CM Stalin grants lifetime recognition to minority schools in TN
evidence.snippet: In February 2024, the TN government announced lifetime recognition for minority educational institutions and permanent minority status certificates.

evidence.title: Madras High Court Verdict Strengthens Minority Institutions' Autonomy
evidence.snippet: A July 2025 ruling reaffirmed the constitutional rights of minority-run institutions under Article 30 to appoint staff without government interference.

plan_existence.notes: Protection of minority institutions is a constitutional right (Article 30). The current Tamil Nadu government has already implemented 'lifetime recognition' and simplified staff appointment rules for these institutions.

fiscal.comments: Creating legal and policy frameworks is a regulatory task with minimal direct fiscal impact.

legal.comments: Strongly supported by Article 30 of the Constitution, which grants minorities the right to establish and administer educational institutions.

administrative.comments: The State Minorities Commission and the Department of Minorities Welfare are already equipped to handle these policy frameworks.

timeline.comments: Policy changes and legal protections can be enacted through Government Orders (GOs) within months.

political.comments: Protecting minority institutions is a standard policy position in Tamil Nadu politics.

feasibility.overall_comments: This promise is highly feasible as it largely codifies existing constitutional protections and recent administrative improvements already in place in the state.
key_risks: Legal challenges regarding the definition of 'minority status'
Balancing institutional autonomy with state regulatory standards

points.text: The TVK government will wage a legal battle in the Supreme Court to permanently strike down the new Waqf Amendment Act.
points.title: 4 Legal Battle Against the Waqf Amendment Act

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 4 Legal Battle Against the Waqf Amendment Act

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu Assembly Resolution against Waqf Bill 2024

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu CM MK Stalin vows legal battle in SC over Waqf bill
evidence.snippet: In April 2025, CM Stalin announced that Tamil Nadu will initiate legal action in the Supreme Court against the Waqf (Amendment) Bill 2024.

plan_existence.notes: The Tamil Nadu Assembly passed a resolution against the Waqf Amendment Bill in March 2025. The current government has already committed to a legal challenge in the Supreme Court.

fiscal.comments: The cost of litigation for a state government is negligible within the overall budget.

legal.comments: While the state has the right to challenge federal laws, the outcome depends entirely on the Supreme Court's interpretation of the Constitution's basic structure and religious freedoms.

administrative.comments: The state's legal department and Advocate General's office are fully capable of managing this.

timeline.comments: Supreme Court battles over major federal legislation often take many years, potentially exceeding a 5-year term.

political.comments: This is a high-visibility political stance that aligns with the state's secular political identity.

feasibility.overall_comments: Waging a legal battle is entirely feasible as an action, though the 'permanent strike down' of the Act is not within the government's direct control but the judiciary's.
key_risks: Uncertain judicial outcome
Potential friction with the Central Government
Lengthy litigation process

points.text: The Haj subsidy for Muslims undertaking the Haj pilgrimage will be increased. Subsidies will also be given to Christians undertaking the Jerusalem pilgrimage, and government subsidies will be provided for pilgrimages of all religions.
points.title: 5 Pilgrimage Subsidy for People of All Religions

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 5 Pilgrimage Subsidy for People of All Religions

evidence.title: TN govt grants Rs 25,000 subsidy to 10 Hajj pilgrims
evidence.snippet: In May 2025, the TN government launched a scheme providing Rs 25,000 each as subsidy to 5,650 first-time Haj pilgrims at a cost of Rs 14.12 crore.

evidence.title: Minorities Welfare - Jerusalem Pilgrimage
evidence.snippet: The state provides Rs. 37,000 (general) and Rs. 60,000 (nuns/sisters) for Jerusalem pilgrimage.

plan_existence.notes: Tamil Nadu already provides subsidies for Haj (reintroduced by state after Center scrapped it), Jerusalem, Mansarovar, and Muktinath. The promise to 'increase' and 'provide for all' is an expansion of these existing schemes.

fiscal.comments: The current Haj subsidy costs approximately Rs. 14.12 crore for 5,650 pilgrims. Expanding this to 'all religions' and increasing amounts would require a significant but manageable budget increase (estimated < Rs. 100 crore).

legal.comments: While the Supreme Court (2012) directed the phasing out of the Central Haj subsidy, state-funded subsidies for diverse religious pilgrimages have generally been upheld if they are non-discriminatory.

administrative.comments: The Tamil Nadu State Hajj Committee and the Minorities Welfare Department already have the mechanisms to process these subsidies.

timeline.comments: Subsidy increases can be implemented immediately through the annual state budget.

political.comments: Providing subsidies for 'all religions' is a politically safe and popular move that avoids 'appeasement' critiques.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible as it builds on established state programs. The fiscal burden is relatively low compared to major welfare schemes.
key_risks: Potential legal challenges based on secularism principles
Managing the selection process for a high volume of applicants across all religions

points.text: Adequate land will be identified and allocated for minority burial grounds (kabarstans) and cemeteries.
points.title: 6 Land for Burial Grounds and Graveyards

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 6 Land for Burial Grounds and Graveyards

existing_scheme_names: Scheme for construction of compound wall / barbed wire fencing around Burial Ground

evidence.title: Allocate two-three acres of land in every district for cemeteries, kabristans: TN state minorities commission
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu state minorities commission recommended that the state govt allocate two to three acres of land in every district for cemeteries and kabristan due to space shortages.

evidence.title: T. N. government has sanctioned ₹10 crore for upkeep of burial grounds
evidence.snippet: The State Government had allotted ₹10 crore during this fiscal for repairing the compound walls of the burial grounds of Christians and Muslims.

plan_existence.notes: While the Tamil Nadu Burial Ground Rules 1999 mandate local bodies to provide burial spaces, specific land for religious minorities is often managed via the Waqf Board or through ad-hoc district-level allocations. The State Minorities Commission formally recommended a dedicated allocation of 2-3 acres per district in December 2025 to address acute space shortages.

fiscal.comments: The state recently allocated ₹10 crore for upkeep, but new land acquisition costs, especially in urban areas like Chennai or Coimbatore, could be significant if government 'poramboke' land is unavailable.

legal.comments: The right to a decent burial is a recognized fundamental right under Article 21 of the Constitution. The Waqf Act 1995 and state burial rules provide a clear legal framework for such allocations.

administrative.comments: District Collectors have the authority to identify and allot government land. The primary challenge is the physical scarcity of suitable land in densely populated urban centers.

timeline.comments: Identification and transfer of land parcels can be executed within a 5-year term through executive orders.

political.comments: The move is politically favorable among minority groups but may face localized resistance from residents near proposed sites or during land acquisition in sensitive areas.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a highly feasible and necessary expansion of existing social welfare duties. It directly addresses a formal recommendation from the State Minorities Commission and fulfills a constitutional obligation, though urban land scarcity remains a practical hurdle.
key_risks: Scarcity of government-owned land in urban districts
High cost of private land acquisition
Local community opposition to new burial sites
Potential litigation over land use changes

points.text: The senior citizen pension will be raised to Rs. 3,000.
points.title: 1 Senior Citizen Pension

existing_scheme_names: Indira Gandhi National Old Age Pension Scheme (IGNOAPS)

evidence.title: Social Security Schemes | Kanniyakumari District, Government of TamilNadu
evidence.snippet: Indira Gandhi National Old Age Pension provides Rs. 1,000 per month to senior citizens 60 years and above who are destitute and belong to BPL families.

evidence.title: Vijay unveils TVK manifesto with welfare push
evidence.snippet: Senior citizens, widows and persons with disabilities are to receive Rs 3,000 per month. Pension coverage for 15 lakh beneficiaries has also been promised.

plan_existence.notes: The current base pension in Tamil Nadu is Rs. 1,000 per month. The promise seeks to triple this amount and expand coverage to an additional 15 lakh beneficiaries.

fiscal.comments: Increasing the pension from Rs. 1,000 to Rs. 3,000 for the existing ~37.8 lakh beneficiaries plus 15 lakh new ones would cost approximately Rs. 19,000 crore annually, creating an additional fiscal burden of ~Rs. 14,500 crore per year.

legal.comments: Social security is a concurrent subject; the state has full legal authority to enhance its own contribution to pension schemes.

administrative.comments: Tamil Nadu has a robust Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) system already handling over 37 lakh pensioners, making the rollout administratively simple.

timeline.comments: The increase can be implemented immediately through a Government Order (GO) and budgetary allocation in the first fiscal year.

political.comments: High populist appeal; senior citizens are a reliable voting bloc, and similar increases have been successful political tools in other states.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is administratively and legally straightforward but carries a heavy fiscal price tag that will strain the state's debt-to-GDP ratio. It represents a significant expansion of the existing social safety net.
key_risks: High recurring fiscal deficit
Inflationary pressure on state budget
Potential for exclusion errors during the 15-lakh expansion

points.text: Every year, arrangements will be made to take 2.5 lakh senior citizens on pilgrimages to places of their choice. This will include AC train travel, comfortable accommodation, and good food.
points.title: 2 Free Pilgrimage

evidence.title: Govt starts free spiritual tour to Amman temples
evidence.snippet: Tamil Nadu govt has launched free Aadi month spiritual tours for senior citizens... Last year, 25 lakh was allocated and 1,003 pilgrims benefited. This year, the allocation has been doubled to 50 lakh.

evidence.title: HR&CE Department Invites Applications for Free Rameswaram–Kashi Spiritual Journey
evidence.snippet: This year, 600 devotees will be taken on a special spiritual journey from Rameswaram to Kashi with a government grant of ₹1.5 crore.

plan_existence.notes: Tamil Nadu currently operates several small-scale pilgrimage schemes through the HR&CE department, covering approximately 4,000 to 5,000 senior citizens annually with a total budget under ₹10 crore. The TVK promise to cover 2.5 lakh people represents a nearly 50-fold expansion in scale and introduces 'places of choice' and AC travel, which are not standard in current state-wide bus-based tours.

fiscal.comments: Based on the current Rameswaram-Kashi scheme cost of ₹25,000 per person (₹1.5 cr for 600 people), covering 2.5 lakh citizens would cost approximately ₹625 crore annually. While significant, this is manageable within Tamil Nadu's ₹3 lakh crore+ budget, though it represents a massive jump from the current sub-₹10 crore allocation for such tours.

legal.comments: State governments have full authority to fund and organize welfare and tourism schemes for senior citizens. No constitutional or statutory barriers exist.

administrative.comments: Managing logistics for 2.5 lakh people annually (approx. 21,000 per month) with 'places of their choice' is extremely complex. Existing schemes use fixed circuits and batches; individualized destinations would require a massive dedicated travel desk and IRCTC coordination.

timeline.comments: The scheme can be rolled out in phases over a 5-year term, starting with fixed circuits and expanding to the promised scale.

political.comments: Highly popular with the elderly and their families. Similar schemes in Delhi and Madhya Pradesh have seen high demand and positive public reception.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is a massive expansion of existing small-scale spiritual tours. While fiscally possible, the primary challenge lies in the administrative logistics of handling 2.5 lakh people annually and the 'choice of destination' clause, which complicates standardized travel arrangements.
key_risks: Logistical failure in managing large-scale elderly travel
High per-capita cost if AC travel and premium accommodation are provided
Potential for corruption in vendor selection for food and stay
Medical emergencies during transit for a large elderly population

points.text: One thousand mobile senior citizens clinics will be started across Tamil Nadu. Each mobile clinic will have a doctor, a nurse, and a physiotherapist. They will visit every village panchayat in Tamil Nadu and perform free medical check-ups. Medicines for chronic illnesses will be provided.
points.title: 3 Vettri Mobile Senior Citizens Clinic

evidence.title: Makkalai Thedi Maruthuvam 2024 - TNPSC Current Affairs
evidence.snippet: The Tamilnadu State government's flagship health scheme, Makkalai Thedi Maruthuvam (MTM) provides comprehensive home-based healthcare services for hypertension and diabetes. It benefits people aged over 45 and provides physiotherapy treatment.

evidence.title: Comparative Assessment of Various Models of Mobile Medical Units
evidence.snippet: Mobile Medical Units have been provided in all 385 blocks under NHM in Tamil Nadu and are functioning since February 2009. Each unit covers 25 to 30 remote villages.

plan_existence.notes: Tamil Nadu already operates 385 Mobile Medical Units (one per block) and the 'Makkalai Thedi Maruthuvam' scheme, which delivers chronic illness medicines and physiotherapy to the elderly at their doorsteps. This promise proposes expanding the fleet to 1,000 units and specifically branding them for senior citizens.

fiscal.comments: Based on NHM guidelines, the capital cost for 1,000 units is approximately ₹250-280 crore, with annual recurring costs of ~₹240 crore. Given Tamil Nadu's health budget of over ₹20,000 crore (2024-25), the total 5-year outlay of ~₹1,450 crore is fiscally manageable.

legal.comments: Health is a State Subject under the Seventh Schedule of the Indian Constitution; the state government has full authority to launch and fund such clinics.

administrative.comments: Recruiting 1,000 doctors and 1,000 physiotherapists for rural mobile duty is a significant challenge. While TN has high medical seat capacity, rural retention and the logistics of managing a 1,000-vehicle fleet require robust administrative machinery.

timeline.comments: Procurement of vehicles and phased recruitment of staff can be realistically achieved within a 5-year term.

political.comments: Welfare schemes for senior citizens are politically popular and face minimal opposition, though it may be viewed as a rebranding of the existing MTM scheme.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is a logical expansion of existing doorstep healthcare infrastructure in Tamil Nadu. While fiscally and legally sound, its success depends on the state's ability to hire and retain specialized medical staff for rural mobile service, which has historically been difficult.
key_risks: Shortage of qualified doctors and physiotherapists willing to work in rural mobile units
High maintenance and fuel costs for a large fleet of 1,000 vehicles
Potential duplication of services with the existing Makkalai Thedi Maruthuvam scheme
Logistical challenges in ensuring regular visits to all 12,525 village panchayats

points.text: A financial grant of Rs. 10,000 will be provided for senior citizens to buy essential items such as walking sticks, spectacles, wheelchairs, hearing aids and dentures.
points.title: 4 Senior Citizens Equipment Grant

existing_scheme_names: Free Supply of Spectacles and Hearing Aids

evidence.title: Rashtriya Vayoshri Yojana (RVY) | Department of Social Justice & Empowerment
evidence.snippet: RVY provides physical aids and assisted-living devices like walking sticks, wheelchairs, hearing aids, and dentures free of cost to BPL senior citizens.

evidence.title: TN credits special grant of ₹2,000 each to 37.79 lakh old age pension beneficiaries
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu government credited a special grant of ₹2,000 to elderly citizens receiving Old Age Pension in March 2026.

plan_existence.notes: While the central RVY scheme provides the actual equipment (walking sticks, dentures, etc.) in camp mode, this promise proposes a direct financial grant of ₹10,000, which is a shift from in-kind to cash-based assistance.

fiscal.comments: With over 1.04 crore senior citizens in Tamil Nadu (2021 census data), a ₹10,000 grant for even 20% of the population would cost over ₹2,000 crore. This is a significant fiscal burden compared to the current ₹2,000 special grants.

legal.comments: No legal barriers; falls under the state's mandate for social security and welfare of the elderly.

administrative.comments: Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) mechanisms are well-established in Tamil Nadu through the Social Welfare department.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented immediately via budget allocation and existing pension databases.

political.comments: Highly popular move for the significant elderly voter base; aligns with existing welfare trends.

feasibility.overall_comments: The scheme is highly feasible administratively but carries a high fiscal cost if implemented at scale. It effectively upgrades the existing central in-kind scheme to a more flexible cash grant.
key_risks: High fiscal outflow
Potential for misuse of funds for non-medical items
Verification of medical need for equipment

points.text: Homes for senior citizens will be set up in every district with qualified caregivers, medical staff, recreational facilities and all other amenities they need.
points.title: 5 Senior Citizens' Homes

evidence.title: Madras HC directs Tamil Nadu government to establish old age homes in all districts
evidence.snippet: In September 2024, the Madras High Court directed the state to establish old age homes in every district as mandated by the 2007 Act.

plan_existence.notes: The Maintenance and Welfare of Parents and Senior Citizens Act, 2007 already mandates at least one home per district. Currently, most are run by NGOs with state grants; this promise implies direct state-run facilities.

fiscal.comments: Setting up 38 district-level homes with medical staff requires significant capital expenditure and recurring operational costs for salaries and maintenance.

legal.comments: This promise fulfills a statutory obligation under the 2007 Act and follows recent judicial directives.

administrative.comments: Requires hiring specialized medical and caregiving staff, which may face recruitment delays in rural districts.

timeline.comments: Construction and staffing of 38 facilities can be completed within a 5-year term if prioritized.

political.comments: Strong public support due to the rising trend of nuclear families and elderly abandonment.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a necessary implementation of an existing legal mandate that has been delayed. It is highly feasible and legally required, though it requires sustained funding for staffing.
key_risks: Staffing shortages for specialized care
Maintenance quality in government-run facilities
Land acquisition in urban districts

points.text: A Senior Citizens Protection Wing will be set up in every District Superintendent of Police's office. A dedicated Nodal Officer will be appointed to resolve issues such as property disputes, harassment and abandonment affecting senior citizens.

evidence.title: Nodal officer in city police stations to help senior citizens
evidence.snippet: In each police station in the city, a police office will act as a nodal officer to look after any kind of problem faced by the senior citizens.

evidence.title: Police to launch dedicated helpline for women in distress and senior citizens
evidence.snippet: The police will soon come out with a dedicated helpline: 'Ladies First' and 'Hello Seniors' to redress grievances of women and senior citizens in the district.

plan_existence.notes: While some districts like Trichy and Cuddalore have implemented nodal officers or helplines for seniors, there is no standardized, statewide 'Senior Citizens Protection Wing' established in every District Superintendent of Police office with a specific mandate for property disputes.

fiscal.comments: Setting up a wing within existing SP offices and appointing nodal officers from existing personnel has negligible fiscal impact.

legal.comments: Property disputes are primarily civil matters; police intervention is legally restricted unless there is a criminal element like harassment or trespass. Clear SOPs would be needed to avoid jurisdictional overreach.

administrative.comments: The Tamil Nadu police already has specialized wings (e.g., AWPS); replicating this structure for seniors is administratively feasible using existing infrastructure.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented via executive order within the first year of a term.

political.comments: High public support as it addresses the safety of a vulnerable and growing demographic.

feasibility.overall_comments: The plan is highly feasible as it leverages existing police infrastructure. The main challenge is ensuring the wing does not overstep into civil judicial territory regarding property disputes.
key_risks: Jurisdictional conflict with civil courts over property disputes
Potential for corruption or bias in mediation
Inadequate training of nodal officers in geriatric sensitivity

plan_existence.notes: The Thayumanavar Scheme already provides doorstep delivery of PDS commodities for seniors (65+). The promise to include 'ration card renewal' and 'issuing certificates' represents an expansion of this existing logistics framework to include revenue and civil supply services.

fiscal.comments: Expanding from commodity delivery to service delivery involves additional costs for mobile biometric devices and personnel training, though the logistics backbone is already being established under the Thayumanavar scheme.

legal.comments: No legal barriers; the state has the authority to determine the mode of service delivery for its citizens.

administrative.comments: Implementation of the existing Thayumanavar scheme has seen challenges, with only 40-45% of targeted beneficiaries reached in early phases. Scaling this to include complex certificate issuance requires robust digital integration.

timeline.comments: Phased rollout is possible within 2-3 years as it builds on existing PDS delivery routes.

political.comments: Very popular 'pro-people' initiative that reduces the burden on the elderly.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a logical expansion of the current Thayumanavar scheme. Success depends on fixing the implementation gaps identified in the current doorstep ration delivery system.

Biometric authentication failures at the doorstep
Data privacy concerns during certificate processing

evidence.snippet: The scheme aims to provide a pension of ₹1,000/- to differently-abled persons with disability 40% and above.

evidence.title: Revise monthly pension to Rs 6K, PwDs urge government
evidence.snippet: Till 2022, it was Rs 1,000... the government in 2022 increased it by Rs 500 in both categories [to Rs 1,500]. There are 8.52 lakh people receiving monthly assistance.

plan_existence.notes: The current pension is Rs. 1,500 for most categories. The promise to increase it to Rs. 3,000 is a 100% hike. Beneficiary groups have recently protested for an increase to Rs. 6,000, making this a moderate middle-ground promise.

fiscal.comments: With ~8.52 lakh beneficiaries, a Rs. 1,500 increase per person would cost approximately Rs. 1,533 crore annually. This is a significant recurring expenditure for the state budget.

legal.comments: The state has full authority to increase social security pensions.

administrative.comments: The Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) mechanism is already in place; only the amount needs to be updated in the system.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented immediately via a Government Order (GO).

political.comments: High political value as it addresses a long-standing demand from a vocal advocacy group.

feasibility.overall_comments: Technically simple to implement but carries a high fiscal burden. It is a direct response to ongoing protests by PwD rights groups.
key_risks: Fiscal strain on the state exchequer

points.subsection_title: E. Welfare of Persons with Disabilities
points.text: A dedicated special-needs teacher will be appointed in every government school. Similarly, dedicated schools for special children will also be opened.
points.title: 2 Education for Special Children

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: E. Welfare of Persons with Disabilities

existing_scheme_names: Inclusive Education for the Disabled in Secondary Stage (IEDSS)

evidence.title: Madras HC directs TRB to fix qualification for special education teachers
evidence.snippet: The court noted that 38% of posts have been lying vacant in nearly 22 government schools since March 2022... directed the state government to ensure special teachers are recruited in all schools.

evidence.title: Madras HC notifies TN govt of plea filed by 723 special educators
evidence.snippet: Nearly 1.30 lakh children with special needs are studying in various government schools... only 1,800 special educators are in service, mostly on contract.

plan_existence.notes: Tamil Nadu currently has 22 government special schools and roughly 1,800 special educators (mostly on contract). The promise to have a special teacher in *every* government school and open more dedicated schools is a massive expansion of the current infrastructure, which is currently struggling with high vacancy rates.

fiscal.comments: Appointing a special teacher in every government school (over 37,000 schools) would require a massive salary budget. Even at a modest salary, the annual cost would exceed several hundred crores.

legal.comments: Mandated by the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 and supported by recent Madras High Court directives.

administrative.comments: There is a severe shortage of qualified special educators in the state. Recruiting and training tens of thousands of teachers is a major administrative hurdle.

timeline.comments: Opening new schools and recruiting teachers at this scale would take the full 5-year term or longer.

political.comments: Strongly supported by parents of special-needs children and education advocates.

feasibility.overall_comments: While legally required and socially necessary, the scale of the promise (one teacher per school) is extremely ambitious and likely to face significant recruitment and fiscal constraints.
key_risks: Shortage of qualified RCI-certified special educators
High recurring salary expenditure
Infrastructure gaps in existing schools for inclusive education

evidence.title: 4% Reservation for PwDs in Tamil Nadu Government Jobs
evidence.snippet: The Government of Tamil Nadu has issued orders (G.O. Ms. No. 21) providing 4% reservation for persons with disabilities in all state government establishments.

plan_existence.notes: The 4% reservation is a statutory requirement under the Central Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016, which the TN government has already adopted.

fiscal.comments: Implementing reservation in existing vacancies does not require additional budgetary outlay beyond standard salary provisions.

legal.comments: It is a legal obligation under the RPwD Act 2016; failure to implement would be a violation of federal law.

administrative.comments: The challenge lies in identifying suitable posts and clearing backlogs, which requires coordination across all government departments.

timeline.comments: Backlog recruitment can be completed within a 5-year term through special recruitment drives.

political.comments: Strong political will usually exists for social justice measures, though it may face minor friction from general category aspirants.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a commitment to enforce an existing law. The feasibility is high because the legal framework is already in place, though administrative execution often lags.
key_risks: Administrative apathy in identifying posts
Legal challenges regarding roster points
Slow recruitment processes

administrative.comments: The Transgender Welfare Board is already established; scaling up existing SHG and training programs is administratively feasible.

points.text: A grant of Rs. 30,000 per year will be given to every weaver family that owns a loom (powerloom/handloom).
points.title: 1 Weavers Grant

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Handlooms and Textiles Department Schemes
evidence.snippet: Current schemes include free electricity, insurance, and old age pensions, but no direct annual cash grant of Rs. 30,000 for loom ownership exists.

plan_existence.notes: While there are welfare schemes like the 'Savings and Security Scheme' and 'Old Age Pension', a direct unconditional annual grant of Rs. 30,000 per weaver family is a new proposal.

fiscal.comments: With several lakh weaver families in TN (approx. 2-3 lakh handloom and many more powerloom), an annual grant of 30k would cost several thousand crores, posing a significant fiscal burden.

legal.comments: No legal barriers to providing direct income support to a specific occupational group.

administrative.comments: Requires rigorous verification of loom ownership and active status to prevent ghost beneficiaries.

timeline.comments: Could be phased in over 5 years, starting with handloom weavers.

political.comments: Highly popular in textile hubs like Erode, Salem, and Kancheepuram; strong vote-bank incentive.

feasibility.overall_comments: Politically attractive but fiscally demanding. The success depends on the state's ability to fund a large-scale direct benefit transfer (DBT) without cutting other textile subsidies.
key_risks: High fiscal deficit impact
Difficulty in verifying 'active' loom ownership
Potential for corruption in beneficiary selection

points.text: Weavers will be given a 50 percent subsidy for purchasing raw materials such as yarn, dye and chemicals required for the weaving trade.
points.title: 2 Weaving Raw Material Purchase Subsidy

evidence.title: Schemes - Department of Handlooms - Government of Tamil Nadu
evidence.snippet: The Government of Tamil Nadu is implementing the Rebate Subsidy Scheme to promote the sales of handloom cloth... The Mill Gate Price Scheme (Central) provides yarn at mill gate prices.

evidence.title: Vijay promises 50% subsidy on raw materials for weavers
evidence.snippet: TVK president Vijay announced a 50% subsidy on raw materials such as yarn, dyes, and chemicals for all weavers.

plan_existence.notes: While the Central government provides yarn at mill gate prices and the State provides a 20% rebate on sales, a direct 50% purchase subsidy for raw materials (yarn, dyes, chemicals) is a significant expansion of existing support levels.

fiscal.comments: Yarn and chemical prices are highly volatile. A 50% subsidy on all raw materials for the entire weaving sector (approx. 1.7 lakh handloom households and 1.6 lakh powerloom units) would require a massive annual outlay, likely exceeding several hundred crores.

legal.comments: State governments have the authority to provide subsidies for industrial raw materials under the 'Industries' entry in the State List.

administrative.comments: Implementing this for co-operative weavers is feasible through existing societies, but reaching independent/private weavers would require a new verification and direct benefit transfer (DBT) mechanism.

timeline.comments: Can be rolled out within 1-2 years by expanding the existing procurement and distribution network of Co-optex and the Handloom Department.

political.comments: Highly popular in the 'Kongu' belt (Erode, Tiruppur, Salem) where the weaving community is a significant vote bank.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is administratively viable but fiscally demanding. The 50% subsidy level is unprecedented compared to current 15-20% support structures, posing a risk of budget overruns if raw material prices spike.
key_risks: High fiscal burden due to yarn price volatility
Potential for leakage in the distribution of subsidized chemicals/dyes
Exclusion of non-cooperative weavers from the subsidy net

plan_existence.notes: The promise increases handloom free units from 300 to 500 and powerloom units from 1000 to 1500. The 75% solar subsidy for small powerloom units is a new specific target, as current solar schemes (PM Surya Ghar) are primarily residential.

fiscal.comments: TANGEDCO is already under severe financial stress with high debt. Increasing free units by 50-66% for over 2 lakh consumers will significantly increase the state's subsidy burden, which was already Rs. 563 crore for powerlooms in 2023-24.

legal.comments: Electricity is a concurrent subject; the state has full authority to provide subsidies through budgetary allocations to the state utility.

administrative.comments: Extremely easy to implement as the billing infrastructure and beneficiary database already exist within TANGEDCO.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented immediately via a Government Order (GO) and tariff revision notification.

political.comments: Free electricity is a potent political tool in Tamil Nadu; increasing the quota is a standard and popular election promise.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible administratively and politically, but fiscally irresponsible given the current state of the power utility. The solar subsidy is a constructive long-term move to reduce the recurring electricity subsidy burden.
key_risks: Increased financial deficit for TANGEDCO
Potential for misuse of free power for commercial purposes beyond the 12-loom limit
High upfront cost for the 75% solar subsidy

Dependence on the timely release of funds by the state treasury

points.text: Integrated weavers' townships will be developed in the districts of Kanchipuram, Arani, Salem and Erode, which excel in the weaving trade. Solar panels with 3 kW capacity will be installed for the electricity needs of these townships.
points.title: 6 Integrated Weavers' Townships

evidence.title: Vijay Promises Old Pension Scheme Reinstatement and Rs 15000 crore MSME Fund At Tiruppur Rally
evidence.snippet: TVK leader Vijay said that if his party comes to power... Integrated weavers' townships will be developed in the districts of Kanchipuram, Arani, Salem and Erode.

plan_existence.notes: While Tamil Nadu has several textile parks and handloom clusters (e.g., under PM MITRA or state-specific cluster development), there is no existing 'Integrated Weavers' Township' scheme specifically providing residential-cum-workspaces with 3 kW solar panels in these four specific locations.

fiscal.comments: Developing four integrated townships involves massive capital expenditure for land acquisition and infrastructure. A 3 kW solar system costs approximately ₹1.5-2 lakh per unit; providing this for thousands of weaver households would require hundreds of crores in subsidy alone.

legal.comments: Land acquisition for industrial/residential townships is within state powers, though it often faces local litigation.

administrative.comments: Requires coordination between the Department of Handlooms, TANGEDCO for solar integration, and housing boards. TN has experience with 'Samathuvapuram' and industrial parks, but integrated occupational townships are more complex.

timeline.comments: Land acquisition, infrastructure development, and solar installation for four major townships are unlikely to be completed within a single 5-year term.

political.comments: Highly popular in the 'Textile Valley' of TN (Salem-Erode-Kanchipuram belt), which holds significant electoral weight.

feasibility.overall_comments: The plan is a high-cost infrastructure project that addresses the specific needs of the weaving community but faces significant fiscal and timeline hurdles. The integration of solar power is a modern, sustainable touch but adds to the upfront cost.
key_risks: High cost of land acquisition
Fiscal burden of 100% solar infrastructure
Delays in construction and utility provisioning
Potential displacement issues during land consolidation

evidence.title: National Handloom Development Programme (NHDP): Scholarship Component
evidence.snippet: The scholarship provides a maximum of ₹2,00,000 per annum for up to 2 children of handloom weavers/workers for pursuing diploma, undergraduate, or postgraduate courses at recognized government-funded textile institutions.

plan_existence.notes: The Central Government already provides a substantial scholarship (up to ₹2 lakh/year) for weavers' children in textile institutions. The manifesto promise appears to be a state-level expansion or a specific focus on Design and Textile Engineering fields.

fiscal.comments: Scholarships for a niche group (children of weavers in specific engineering/design streams) are fiscally very manageable compared to broader welfare schemes.

legal.comments: No legal hurdles; states have full authority to provide educational grants.

administrative.comments: Can be easily administered through the existing Department of Handlooms and Textiles using DBT.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented within the first year of a new government.

political.comments: Positive reception as it promotes social mobility for traditional artisan families.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a highly feasible and low-cost promise that complements existing central schemes. It targets skill upgradation and professionalization of the next generation of the weaving community.
key_risks: Duplication of Central NHDP benefits
Low awareness among eligible students
Limited number of seats in specialized textile/design institutes

points.text: A dedicated e-commerce brand will be created to sell Tamil Nadu weavers' products directly at the international level. Vettri Thari showrooms will be set up in every major city.
points.title: 8 Vettri Thari

existing_scheme_names: Cooptex (Tamil Nadu Handloom Weavers' Co-operative Society)

evidence.title: Vijay Promises Vetri Thari retail outlets in major cities
evidence.snippet: To facilitate the direct sale of weavers' products in international markets, a state-owned global e-commerce brand will be established. 'Vetri Thari' (Victory Loom) retail outlets will be set up in major cities.

plan_existence.notes: While Cooptex exists and has an e-commerce presence, 'Vettri Thari' is proposed as a new, distinct brand with a specific focus on international markets and a new chain of showrooms.

fiscal.comments: Setting up showrooms in 'every major city' and building a global e-commerce brand requires significant marketing and real estate investment. However, it can be structured as a revenue-generating PSU.

legal.comments: No legal barriers to establishing a state-owned brand or retail chain.

administrative.comments: Managing a global e-commerce supply chain and high-end retail outlets requires professional expertise that state departments often lack, potentially requiring a private-public partnership (PPP) model.

timeline.comments: E-commerce platforms can be launched quickly; physical showrooms in all major cities would take 2-3 years.

political.comments: Strong appeal as it promises 'direct' sales, bypassing middlemen and increasing weaver margins.

feasibility.overall_comments: A modern approach to traditional industry. The success depends on branding and logistics rather than just policy. It risks competing with the existing Cooptex brand unless clearly differentiated.
key_risks: Competition with existing Cooptex brand
High operational costs of physical showrooms
Logistical challenges in international shipping and returns
Quality control across diverse weaver clusters

points.text: A 100 percent subsidy will be provided to set up solar panels for loom sheds owned by individuals.
points.title: 9 Solar Panel Subsidy

existing_scheme_names: PM Surya Ghar: Muft Bijli Yojana
Chief Minister's Solar Rooftop Capital Incentive Scheme

evidence.title: Solar Energy Subsidy in Tamil Nadu: Amount, Eligibility
evidence.snippet: TN Govt subsidy for solar panels is also known as Chief Minister's Solar Rooftop Capital Incentive Scheme... offers a subsidy of Rs. 20,000 per kilowatt. Central subsidy under PM Surya Ghar offers up to Rs. 78,000.

plan_existence.notes: Current schemes provide partial subsidies (approx. 40-70% depending on capacity). A 100% subsidy specifically for individual loom sheds is a significant expansion and does not currently exist.

fiscal.comments: A 100% subsidy is extremely expensive. With over 2 lakh weavers in TN, even if 10% (20,000) apply for a 3 kW system at ₹1.6 lakh each, the cost would be ₹320 crore. Scaling this to all individual sheds would run into thousands of crores.

legal.comments: State has the authority to provide 100% subsidies for renewable energy adoption.

administrative.comments: TEDA and TANGEDCO have existing frameworks for solar installation and net metering, making the delivery mechanism feasible.

timeline.comments: Solar installations are quick; a large-scale rollout could be achieved in 3-4 years.

political.comments: Extremely popular as it eliminates electricity costs for weavers, a major pain point in the sector.

feasibility.overall_comments: While administratively and politically sound, the 100% subsidy model is fiscally aggressive. It effectively shifts the entire capital risk to the state, which may be unsustainable if demand is high.
key_risks: Massive fiscal drain on the state exchequer
Quality of solar components under 100% subsidy tenders
Maintenance and repair responsibility after installation
Potential for misuse or resale of subsidized equipment

points.text: The Tamil Nadu Guaranteed Pension Scheme brought in by the DMK government will be abolished, and in line with government employees' demands, the full Old Pension Scheme will be compassionately reviewed and assessed.
points.title: 1 Implementing the Old Pension Scheme

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu's new pension scheme broadly follows the guidelines of Old Pension Scheme
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu Assured Pension Scheme (TAPS), launched in January 2026, is a hybrid model providing 50% of last drawn pay but retaining the 10% employee contribution.

evidence.title: CM Stalin launches Tamil Nadu Assured Pension Scheme for govt staff
evidence.snippet: The TAPS scheme guarantees 50 per cent of the last drawn salary as assured pension and will cost the state an initial Rs 13,000 crore.

plan_existence.notes: The DMK government implemented the Tamil Nadu Assured Pension Scheme (TAPS) on January 1, 2026, as a hybrid between the Old Pension Scheme (OPS) and the National Pension System (NPS). This promise seeks to abolish TAPS and restore the non-contributory 'full' OPS.

fiscal.comments: Restoring full OPS would eliminate the 10% employee contribution and require the state to bear the entire pension liability. TAPS already costs the state Rs 11,000 crore annually; a full OPS would significantly exceed this, straining a state already facing high debt-to-GSDP ratios.

legal.comments: While states have the authority to define pension rules for their employees, the legal transition from a contributory fund (CPS/TAPS) back to a non-contributory one involves complex accounting of accumulated corpuses (over Rs 84,500 crore as of 2025).

administrative.comments: The administrative machinery for pension disbursement exists; the primary challenge is the financial restructuring of the pension fund.

timeline.comments: A 'review and assessment' can be completed quickly, but full implementation and fund migration would take several years.

political.comments: Restoring OPS is the single most significant demand of government employee unions (JACTTO-GEO) and carries immense electoral weight.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is fiscally high-risk but politically potent. It directly challenges the hybrid TAPS model introduced in early 2026, aiming to satisfy the long-standing demand for a non-contributory pension.
key_risks: Extreme fiscal burden on state exchequer
Legal complexity in handling the existing CPS corpus
Potential reduction in capital expenditure to fund pension liabilities

points.text: A Government Employees Welfare Board will be established with an allocation of Rs. 5,000 crore to protect the rights and ensure the welfare of government employees — including government staff, police personnel, school teachers and college professors.
points.title: 2 Government Employees Welfare Board

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu CM Stalin launches Rs 5000 crore project for women safety
evidence.snippet: While a Rs 5,000 crore project for women (TNWESafe) was launched in Jan 2026, no dedicated Government Employees Welfare Board with this allocation exists.

plan_existence.notes: Tamil Nadu has numerous welfare boards for unorganized workers, but permanent government employees are typically governed by service rules and departmental benefits rather than a separate welfare board with a dedicated multi-crore fund.

fiscal.comments: An allocation of Rs 5,000 crore is a substantial one-time outlay (roughly 1.5% of the state's annual budget). Without a defined revenue source or specific spending plan, the fiscal feasibility is low.

legal.comments: There are no legal barriers to creating a welfare board; the state has broad powers to establish such bodies via executive order or legislation.

administrative.comments: Tamil Nadu has extensive experience managing welfare boards (e.g., for construction workers), making the administrative setup feasible.

timeline.comments: A board can be established and funds allocated within the first year of a new government term.

political.comments: Likely to be well-received by the influential government employee vote bank, though it may face criticism for prioritizing well-protected public servants over unorganized workers.

feasibility.overall_comments: A novel administrative proposal that is highly feasible to implement but carries a significant price tag of Rs 5,000 crore for a group already covered by robust service benefits.
key_risks: Duplication of existing departmental benefits
High fiscal cost without clear utility for permanent staff
Potential for the fund to remain underutilized or diverted

points.text: Temporary staff appointed on a contract basis in government roles such as teachers and nurses will be made permanent staff after 5 years of service.
points.title: 3 Permanency for Temporary Staff

evidence.title: TN regularises 831 contract nurses | Chennai News - Times of India
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu government issued permanent appointment orders to 831 contract nurses in January 2026, completing a phase of regularisation.

evidence.title: TVK Election Manifesto Promises Regularisation for Contract Employees
evidence.snippet: The TVK manifesto (April 2026) specifically promises that all contract employees with five years of experience will have their services regularised.

plan_existence.notes: Regularisation currently happens in a phased, ad-hoc manner based on protests or specific vacancies. This promise proposes a systematic, automatic rule for permanency after 5 years of service.

fiscal.comments: Regularizing thousands of contract workers (especially in health and education) involves a massive jump in the wage bill, as they move from consolidated pay to time-scale pay with full benefits.

legal.comments: The Supreme Court (Umadevi judgment) restricts 'back-door' regularisation of temporary staff if they were not recruited through a transparent, competitive process. Automatic permanency after 5 years may face legal challenges.

administrative.comments: Requires creating thousands of new sanctioned permanent posts, which is a slow administrative process involving the Finance Department.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented in phases over a 5-year term, as seen with recent nurse regularisations.

political.comments: Extremely popular among the thousands of temporary workers and their families; a major campaign issue for 2026.

feasibility.overall_comments: A high-impact welfare promise that faces significant legal hurdles regarding recruitment norms and a heavy long-term fiscal burden on the state budget.
key_risks: Legal challenges based on Supreme Court recruitment precedents
Massive increase in the state's recurring salary expenditure
Potential friction with candidates awaiting regular recruitment via TNPSC/TRB
