points.text: It will be ensured that promotions and transfers of government employees take place fairly and transparently.
points.title: 4 Transparency in Promotions and Transfers

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu State and Subordinate Services Rules

evidence.title: Personnel and Administrative Reforms Department - Panel Preparation
evidence.snippet: Existing rules mandate annual preparation of promotion panels and specify criteria for recruitment by transfer and promotion.

evidence.title: Transfer and Posting Guidelines | CAG Tamil Nadu
evidence.snippet: Guidelines exist for periodic transfers and the functioning of Transfer and Posting Boards to ensure administrative needs are met.

plan_existence.notes: While rules exist, the promise addresses the common grievance of political interference and lack of transparency in the actual execution of these rules.

fiscal.comments: Improving transparency in administrative processes has negligible direct fiscal cost and may even save money by reducing litigation.

legal.comments: Fully within the state's power; requires only the enforcement of existing service rules or minor amendments to administrative guidelines.

administrative.comments: Feasible through the expansion of online 'counseling' systems (currently used for teachers) to all departments.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented immediately through executive orders and digital portals.

political.comments: Highly popular with employees but may face quiet resistance from political intermediaries who benefit from the current discretionary system.

feasibility.overall_comments: A standard but essential governance promise that is highly feasible and carries no fiscal risk, though its success depends entirely on political will to reduce discretionary power.
key_risks: Resistance from middle-level bureaucracy and political brokers
Technical challenges in digitizing complex seniority lists across all departments

points.text: Once announced by the Union Government, the TVK government will immediately implement the 8th Pay Commission in the state to benefit government employees.
points.title: 5 Immediate Implementation of the 8th Pay Commission

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 5 Immediate Implementation of the 8th Pay Commission

evidence.title: 8th Pay Commission 2026: Fitment Factor, Pay Matrix, Salary Structure and Hike
evidence.snippet: The 8th Pay Commission was formally constituted by the Government of India via Gazette Notification on November 3, 2025, with an expected implementation date of January 1, 2026.

plan_existence.notes: While the Central Government has initiated the 8th Pay Commission process, the Tamil Nadu state government has not yet implemented it. States typically wait for the final Central notification before forming their own state-level official committees to adopt the revised scales.

fiscal.comments: Implementing a new pay commission typically increases the state's salary and pension bill by 15-20%. Given Tamil Nadu's high outstanding debt (over ₹7.5 lakh crore), this will significantly strain the fiscal deficit.

legal.comments: State governments have full legal authority to revise the pay scales of their employees.

administrative.comments: The administrative machinery for pay revision (Treasury and Accounts department) is well-established, though calculating arrears for lakhs of employees takes time.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented immediately following the Central Government's notification and the State's own committee report.

political.comments: High political appeal as it targets a massive and influential voting bloc of government employees and their families.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is a standard administrative progression followed by most states. The primary challenge is the massive recurring fiscal burden, but it is politically non-negotiable for any incoming government.
key_risks: Severe fiscal strain on state budget
Potential delay in arrear payments
Inflationary pressure due to increased liquidity

points.text: A Police Welfare and Work Reform Act will be brought in and weekly holidays on a rotation basis will be provided. The laundry allowance for police personnel will be raised from Rs. 500 to Rs. 1,000. Police welfare hospitals will be set up in districts including Madurai, Coimbatore, Trichy, Tirunelveli, Salem and Vellore.
points.title: 1 Police Welfare Scheme

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu Police Weekly Off System

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu govt issues order granting weekly off for police personnel
evidence.snippet: A Government Order allowing weekly off for police personnel was issued in November 2021, applicable to personnel from Grade-II level constables to head constables.

plan_existence.notes: Weekly offs already exist via executive order (DGP circular/GO), but the promise to codify this into an 'Act' is new. Laundry allowance currently stands at ₹500; the promise doubles it. Dedicated police hospitals currently exist primarily in Chennai (Egmore).

fiscal.comments: Doubling the laundry allowance for ~1.2 lakh personnel costs roughly ₹72 crore annually. Building six specialized hospitals requires significant capital expenditure (estimated ₹300-500 crore).

legal.comments: The state has the power to enact a Police Reform Act and manage its own police force welfare.

administrative.comments: Implementing weekly offs on a 'rotation basis' is administratively challenging due to existing vacancies in the police force (often 15-20% shortage).

timeline.comments: Legislative changes and allowance hikes can be immediate; hospital construction will take 3-4 years.

political.comments: Police welfare is a popular theme to ensure loyalty and improve morale in the force.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise builds on existing schemes but adds a legislative layer and infrastructure. The main hurdle is the manpower shortage which makes 'compulsory' weekly offs difficult to sustain during emergencies or festivals.
key_risks: Manpower shortage hindering weekly off implementation
High capital cost for multi-district hospitals
Maintenance and staffing of new medical facilities

points.text: The basic monthly pay of police personnel will be increased from the current Rs. 18,200 to Rs. 25,000. Additionally, a monthly Duty Allowance of Rs. 1,000 will be given to police personnel working in divisions with heavy workloads.
points.title: 3 Dignified Pay and Welfare for Police Personnel

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 3 Dignified Pay and Welfare for Police Personnel

evidence.title: TNUSRB Police Constable Salary 2026
evidence.snippet: The starting basic pay for a Grade II Police Constable in Tamil Nadu is currently ₹18,200 (Pay Level 3).

evidence.title: Fifth Police Commission recommends pay hike for constables
evidence.snippet: The commission recommended that the pay scale of police constables may be fixed at ₹21,700-69,100, up from the current ₹18,200.

plan_existence.notes: The promise to raise basic pay to ₹25,000 exceeds the Fifth Police Commission's recommendation of ₹21,700. The 'Duty Allowance' of ₹1,000 is a specific new monthly addition for high-workload divisions.

fiscal.comments: Raising basic pay by ~37% (from 18.2k to 25k) for the constabulary (~1 lakh+ personnel) would cost the state several thousand crores annually in recurring expenditure, including the ripple effect on DA and HRA.

legal.comments: The state has full authority to set pay scales for its police force.

administrative.comments: Defining 'divisions with heavy workloads' for the ₹1,000 allowance may lead to internal disputes or demands for universal application.

timeline.comments: Pay revisions can be implemented in the first budget of the new government.

political.comments: Very popular among the police force, but may trigger similar demands from other equivalent government cadres (e.g., fire service, prison staff).

feasibility.overall_comments: While fiscally expensive, this addresses a long-standing grievance regarding the low entry-level pay of the police compared to other states. It is a significant but standard welfare intervention.
key_risks: Inter-departmental pay parity demands
Significant recurring fiscal burden
Subjectivity in defining 'heavy workload' divisions

points.text: A transparent and accountable police administration free from political interference in transfers and postings will be ensured. All decisions will be taken solely on the basis of administrative needs, performance metrics and professional integrity.
points.title: 4 Transparent and Accountable Police Administration

evidence.title: Section 8 in The Tamil Nadu Police (Reform) Act, 2013
evidence.snippet: There shall be a Police Establishment Board... to consider and recommend promotion, transfer and posting of the officers.

plan_existence.notes: The Tamil Nadu Police (Reforms) Act, 2013 already mandates the creation of a Police Establishment Board to handle transfers and postings to reduce political interference. However, civil society groups have criticized the act for being weak and not fully insulating the police from political control.

legal.comments: Police is a state subject under the Seventh Schedule of the Constitution; the state has full authority to legislate on its administration.

administrative.comments: While the mechanism (Police Establishment Board) exists, ensuring zero political interference requires significant shift in institutional culture and oversight.

timeline.comments: Procedural changes to transfer protocols can be implemented within a 5-year term.

political.comments: Political parties often rely on control over police transfers for local influence, making genuine implementation politically difficult.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise duplicates existing statutory requirements under the 2013 Act. The challenge is not the lack of a plan, but the lack of political will to implement it as intended by the Supreme Court's Prakash Singh directives.
key_risks: Political resistance to losing control over postings
Bureaucratic inertia
Weak statutory teeth in the existing 2013 Act

points.text: The practice of posting police personnel as orderlies in the homes of VIPs in Tamil Nadu against established rules will be stopped.
points.title: 5 Abolition of the Orderly System

existing_scheme_names: District-level committees for eradicating orderly system (2026)

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu government constitutes district-level committees to abolish police orderly system
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu government, on February 5, 2026, informed the Madras High Court of having issued a Government Order (G.O.) on January 21, 2026 constituting district-level committees headed by the Collectors for eradicating orderly system.

plan_existence.notes: The orderly system was officially abolished in 1979, but the practice persisted. Following strict orders from the Madras High Court in 2022-2025, the state government recently (Jan 2026) formed committees to ensure total eradication.

fiscal.comments: Abolishing the system actually saves money or reallocates existing salaries to active duty, as personnel are moved from private homes to public service.

legal.comments: The Madras High Court has repeatedly ordered the abolition of this system, calling it a 'colonial slavery system'.

administrative.comments: The recent formation of district-level committees provides a clear administrative mechanism for enforcement.

timeline.comments: Can be fully achieved within months if the newly formed committees act decisively.

political.comments: May face quiet resistance from senior IPS officers and VIPs who benefit from the system.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a highly feasible and legally mandated promise that aligns with recent judicial directives. The primary hurdle is the internal resistance within the police hierarchy.
key_risks: Non-compliance by senior officials
Personnel being used unofficially without formal records
Lack of reporting by subordinates due to fear of reprisal

points.text: The deployment of women police personnel in bandobust duty will be stopped. It will be ensured that they are posted as front-line police personnel.

existing_scheme_names: 9-point Navaratna welfare scheme for women police (2023)

plan_existence.notes: While the 2023 'Navaratna' scheme improved working conditions (roll-call timings, hostels, crèches), it did not ban bandobust duty for women. A total exemption from bandobust duty is a new and significant policy shift.

administrative.comments: Exempting a significant portion of the force (over 35,000 women) from bandobust duty could create severe manpower shortages during large festivals, protests, or VIP visits.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented immediately via an executive order from the DGP/Home Department.

political.comments: Likely to be very popular among women police personnel and their families, though it may face internal criticism regarding gender-neutral policing.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible in terms of cost and timeline, but poses significant administrative challenges for law and order management due to the sudden reduction in available personnel for field duties.
key_risks: Manpower shortage for large-scale public events
Potential impact on career progression/field experience for women officers
Increased workload on male counterparts leading to resentment

points.text: A Tamil Nadu Teachers' Protection Act will be brought in, and teachers working on a temporary basis who have completed 5 years of service will be made permanent teachers. Similarly, during the period they serve as temporary teachers, it will be ensured that they receive 'equal pay for equal work' on par with permanent teachers.
points.title: 1 Equal Work! Equal Pay!

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu teachers protesting pay disparity, seeking permanent jobs arrested in Chennai
evidence.snippet: Teachers have been protesting for over a week, placing demands such as equal pay for equal work and making temporary teachers permanent.

evidence.title: Part-time teachers to be regularised via entrance exam: CM Stalin
evidence.snippet: Chief Minister MK Stalin announced in January 2026 that part-time teachers will be regularised following a mandatory entrance exam with weightage for service.

plan_existence.notes: While the current government announced a regularization process in early 2026 involving an entrance exam, this promise proposes a specific 'Teachers' Protection Act' and automatic regularization after 5 years without mentioning an exam. The 'equal pay for equal work' demand is a long-standing grievance currently under committee review.

fiscal.comments: Regularizing over 14,000 temporary teachers into permanent pay scales (Level 10 or higher) would incur a massive recurring expenditure, likely exceeding several hundred crores annually.

legal.comments: Courts have frequently struck down regularization that bypasses standard recruitment rules (Articles 14 and 16). The Madras High Court recently ruled that 'equal pay' applies only under identical service conditions.

administrative.comments: Establishing a new legislative framework (Protection Act) is feasible, but verifying service records and integrating diverse temporary cadres into a unified pay structure is complex.

timeline.comments: Legislative action and initial regularization orders can be passed within a 5-year term, though legal challenges may delay implementation.

political.comments: Extremely popular among the teaching community and unions, addressing a core grievance that has seen repeated protests.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise addresses a critical social justice issue for teachers but faces significant legal hurdles regarding recruitment norms and a heavy fiscal burden. The proposed 'Protection Act' is a novel legislative approach to provide job security.
key_risks: Legal challenges in High Court/Supreme Court regarding recruitment rules
High fiscal deficit impact due to permanent salary and pension liabilities
Inter-se seniority disputes between regularized and directly recruited teachers

points.subsection_title: K. Welfare of Anganwadi and ASHA Workers
points.text: All Anganwadi workers will be made 'Grade D' government employees and paid Rs. 18,000 per month on that basis. Similarly, it will be ensured that Anganwadi helpers and ASHA workers who conduct public-health awareness work also receive a monthly salary of Rs. 10,000.
points.title: 1 Pay Rise

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: K. Welfare of Anganwadi and ASHA Workers

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu ICDS Pay Scales
evidence.snippet: Anganwadi workers in TN are currently paid under a Special Time Scale of Pay, which is not equivalent to regular Grade D government employment.

plan_existence.notes: Anganwadi workers currently receive a 'Special Time Scale' honorarium. Making them 'Grade D' employees would be a fundamental shift in their employment status from voluntary/contractual to permanent government service.

fiscal.comments: Tamil Nadu has approximately 54,439 Anganwadi workers and 43,000 helpers. Increasing salaries to 18,000 and 10,000 respectively, plus Grade D benefits (pension, DA), would create a massive recurring expenditure exceeding several thousand crores annually.

legal.comments: The government has the power to create posts, but changing the status of 'honorary' workers to 'permanent' workers has complex service law implications.

administrative.comments: Requires restructuring the entire ICDS and NHM workforce management in the state.

timeline.comments: Pay hikes can be implemented via G.O. within a single fiscal year, though permanent status takes longer.

political.comments: Extremely popular among the large workforce of women in rural and urban areas.

feasibility.overall_comments: While politically lucrative, the fiscal burden of permanent Grade D status for nearly 1 lakh workers is the primary hurdle.
key_risks: Extreme fiscal strain on the state budget
Demands for similar status from other contractual workers
Legal challenges regarding recruitment seniority

points.subsection_title: K. Welfare of Anganwadi and ASHA Workers
points.text: It will be ensured that Anganwadi and ASHA workers who have completed 20 years of service receive Rs. 15 lakh as retirement benefits.
points.title: 2 Retirement Benefits

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: K. Welfare of Anganwadi and ASHA Workers

existing_scheme_names: Lumpsum Retirement Benefit for Anganwadi Workers

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Budget 2023-24 Welfare Announcements
evidence.snippet: The retirement benefit for Anganwadi workers was recently increased to Rs. 1,00,000 and for helpers to Rs. 50,000.

plan_existence.notes: The promise of Rs. 15 lakh represents a 1500% increase over the current benefit of Rs. 1 lakh.

fiscal.comments: Providing 15 lakh per retiring worker is fiscally unsustainable given the current benefit is only 1 lakh. With thousands retiring annually, the one-time payout cost would be astronomical.

legal.comments: The state government has full authority to determine retirement benefits for its quasi-government staff.

administrative.comments: Existing mechanisms for disbursing retirement benefits can be used; only the quantum changes.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented immediately via executive order, subject to fund availability.

political.comments: High political appeal for long-serving grassroots workers.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is a massive expansion of an existing benefit. While administratively simple, it is fiscally reckless without a dedicated corpus fund.
key_risks: Budgetary deficit
Inflationary pressure on state liabilities
Inequity compared to other government servants with similar service years

points.text: The inhumane practice of human beings manually removing human waste will be completely abolished. Modern machines will be used for this work. Strict non-bailable legal action will be taken against private firms or contractors that violate this.
points.title: 1 Ending Manual Handling of Human Waste

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 1 Ending Manual Handling of Human Waste

existing_scheme_names: Prohibition of Employment as Manual Scavengers and their Rehabilitation Act, 2013

evidence.title: The Prohibition of Employment as Manual Scavengers and their Rehabilitation Act, 2013
evidence.snippet: Prohibits the engagement or employment of anyone for manual scavenging and provides for strict penalties.

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu's use of Bandicoot Robots
evidence.snippet: The Greater Chennai Corporation and other municipalities have already begun deploying robotic scavengers to eliminate manual entry.

plan_existence.notes: Manual scavenging is already illegal under central law. The promise focuses on stricter enforcement and 100% mechanization, which is the current stated goal of the TN government.

fiscal.comments: Funding for machines like 'Bandicoot' is already part of various central and state schemes (AMRUT, Swachh Bharat). Scaling this statewide is financially manageable.

legal.comments: Strengthens existing federal law; non-bailable clauses for contractors are already supported by the 2013 Act and SC judgments.

administrative.comments: Monitoring private contractors and apartments in urban areas remains a significant enforcement challenge.

timeline.comments: Full mechanization can be achieved in 5 years with aggressive procurement.

political.comments: High social justice priority; aligns with the ideologies of major Dravidian parties.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a continuation of existing legal and technological trends. It is highly feasible and necessary, focusing on enforcement rather than new policy creation.
key_risks: Corruption in machine procurement
Difficulty in monitoring illegal manual cleaning in private residential complexes
Need for training workers to operate machines

points.text: Workers who were previously engaged in manually removing human waste will be trained to operate the modern machines mentioned above and appointed as operators. This will prevent their loss of employment and ensure their dignified livelihood.
points.title: 2 Training for Sanitation Workers

existing_scheme_names: NAMASTE (National Action for Mechanised Sanitation Ecosystem)
Self Employment Scheme for Rehabilitation of Manual Scavengers (SRMS)

evidence.snippet: The scheme focuses on the mechanization of sewer and septic tank cleaning... providing alternative livelihood opportunities, skill development, and training in modern cleaning technologies.

plan_existence.notes: The promise aligns with the existing NAMASTE (Central) and SWDS (State) schemes which already focus on mechanization and training for manual scavengers. The promise emphasizes the transition of former manual scavengers into machine operators to prevent job loss.

fiscal.comments: The NAMASTE scheme has a national outlay of Rs 350-360 crore. Tamil Nadu's SWDS is already operational with allocated funds. Costs for training are relatively low compared to machine procurement.

legal.comments: Mandated by the Prohibition of Employment as Manual Scavengers and their Rehabilitation Act, 2013.

administrative.comments: Existing infrastructure through Urban Local Bodies (ULBs) and the Tamil Nadu Adi Dravidar Housing and Development Corporation (TAHDCO) is already in place.

timeline.comments: Training programs can be rolled out within 1-2 years as part of ongoing mechanization drives.

political.comments: High political consensus on eradicating manual scavenging and rehabilitating workers.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a highly feasible continuation of existing state and central policies. The administrative framework is already active, and the fiscal burden is shared with the central government.
key_risks: Identification of all eligible workers
Quality and relevance of technical training
Availability of modern machinery in all local bodies

administrative.comments: Current utilization of the pension is reported to be very low due to lack of awareness and application hurdles; administrative efficiency must improve significantly.

Administrative bottlenecks in the Welfare Board

points.text: To protect the environment, a 50 percent subsidy (up to Rs. 75,000) will be given for newly purchased electric autos. A 50 percent subsidy (up to Rs. 30,000) will be given for converting existing autos to CNG (gas) operation.

points.text: More than 15 lakh gig workers in Tamil Nadu work independently without any job protection or medical insurance. Considering their welfare, a Tamil Nadu Gig Workers Act will be enacted, ensuring their job security and social status.
points.title: 1 Tamil Nadu Gig Workers Act

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu Platform-based Gig Workers Welfare Board

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu rolls out free accident insurance for gig workers
evidence.snippet: The state established the Platform-Based Gig Workers Welfare Board in December 2023 and launched insurance schemes, but a dedicated 'Act' is still pending.

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu State Planning Commission calls for legal protection for gig workers
evidence.snippet: A March 2026 study by the SPC recommends a new legal framework exclusively for platform-based gig workers.

plan_existence.notes: Tamil Nadu has a Welfare Board (est. 2023) and has extended the Manual Workers Act to gig workers, but it does not yet have a standalone 'Gig Workers Act' like Rajasthan's 2023 legislation.

legal.comments: Defining the legal status of gig workers (employee vs. contractor) is a complex legal issue currently being debated in courts and under the Central Code on Social Security.

administrative.comments: Enforcement is difficult as aggregators often do not share real-time workforce data with state governments, making registration and benefit delivery a challenge.

timeline.comments: Legislative drafting and passing can be done within 1-2 years, following the models of Rajasthan or Karnataka.

political.comments: Strong support from labor unions; potential friction with major tech platforms (Swiggy, Zomato, Uber) over welfare cess and compliance.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is a logical progression of existing state efforts. While the legislative path is clear, the actual enforcement of 'job security' in a gig model remains a significant global challenge.
key_risks: Legal conflict with Central Government's labor codes
Aggregator pushback or reduction in operations due to increased costs
Difficulty in tracking and registering a highly transient workforce

fiscal.comments: The current premium for Rs. 5 lakh accident insurance is approximately Rs. 105 per worker annually. administrative.comments: The mechanism for registration and insurance disbursement is already in place through the Labour Department, though worker enrollment rates have historically been low.

timeline.comments: Insurance policies can be tendered and implemented within a single fiscal year.

political.comments: Gig worker welfare is a high-priority area for all major parties to capture the youth and urban vote.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible as it builds on an existing administrative structure. The primary challenge remains increasing the registration of gig workers on the state portal to ensure coverage.

Dependence on platform companies for data sharing

points.text: Interest-free loans will be given to gig workers employed with firms such as Swiggy and Zomato to buy e-bikes.
points.title: 3 Interest-Free Loans to Buy E-bikes

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu E-Scooter Subsidy for Gig Workers

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu to offer ₹20,000 for gig workers to buy e-scooters
evidence.snippet: Tamil Nadu finance minister announced in the 2025-26 budget that the government will offer ₹20,000 to 2,000 gig workers to aid them in buying electric scooters.

plan_existence.notes: The current state scheme provides a one-time subsidy of Rs. 20,000 for a limited number of workers (2,000). The promise shifts the model to interest-free loans, which could potentially cover a larger number of workers but requires a different financial mechanism (interest subvention).

fiscal.comments: Providing interest-free loans for thousands of workers requires significant capital or high interest-subvention costs. If the state provides the principal, the capital requirement is high; if it subvents interest, the recurring cost is manageable but depends on bank tie-ups.

legal.comments: State governments frequently provide interest-free or subsidized loans through cooperative banks or specialized welfare boards.

administrative.comments: Managing loan recoveries from a floating workforce like gig workers is administratively difficult for banks, often leading to high NPA risks unless tied to platform payouts.

timeline.comments: A loan scheme can be launched within 12-18 months once tie-ups with cooperative banks are finalized.

political.comments: Promoting green mobility while supporting low-income workers is politically popular and aligns with climate goals.

feasibility.overall_comments: Feasible as an evolution of the current subsidy model. The shift to loans allows for wider reach than fixed-budget subsidies, provided the recovery mechanism is robust.
key_risks: High default rates among informal workers
Administrative burden of loan processing
Limited charging infrastructure affecting vehicle utility

points.text: Under the Street Vending Regulation and Livelihood Protection Act, special zones for street vending will be set up in every urban local body. Investment loans of up to Rs. 50,000 will be provided interest-free through cooperative banks. Street vendors will only be relocated after alternative locations have been confirmed.
points.title: 1 Act for Regulation of Street Vending and Protection of Livelihoods

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 1 Act for Regulation of Street Vending and Protection of Livelihoods

existing_scheme_names: Street Vendors (Protection of Livelihood and Regulation of Street Vending) Act, 2014

evidence.title: PM SVANidhi Scheme: Loans for Street Vendors
evidence.snippet: Provides collateral-free working capital loans up to ₹50,000 with a 7% interest subsidy for street vendors.

plan_existence.notes: The central PM SVANidhi scheme already provides loans up to Rs. 50,000. However, it is not 'interest-free' (it offers a 7% subsidy). The promise to make it interest-free through cooperative banks implies the state will pay the remaining interest. The creation of vending zones is a statutory requirement under the 2014 Act already being implemented.

fiscal.comments: The cost of covering the remaining interest (beyond the 7% central subsidy) is minimal for the state, likely amounting to 2-4% of the loan value.

legal.comments: The Street Vending Act 2014 already mandates the creation of vending zones and protection from arbitrary eviction.

administrative.comments: Setting up vending zones in 'every' urban local body has faced significant delays due to urban space constraints and local resistance.

timeline.comments: While loans can be disbursed quickly, the physical creation of vending zones and confirmation of alternative locations for all vendors is a multi-year infrastructure task.

political.comments: Street vendors are a significant urban vote bank, but vending zones often face opposition from resident welfare associations (RWAs).

feasibility.overall_comments: The loan component is highly feasible as an add-on to PM SVANidhi. The regulatory component (vending zones) is a continuation of existing legal mandates that face practical implementation hurdles.
key_risks: Urban space scarcity for vending zones
Conflict with traffic and pedestrian regulations
Slow identification and certification of vendors by ULBs

points.subsection_title: P. Welfare of Journalists and Media Workers
points.text: Life insurance of Rs. 15 lakh will be provided to all accredited journalists. Families of retired journalists with an annual income of less than Rs. 5 lakh will be given a monthly pension of Rs. 15,000. Steps will be taken to accredit local journalists working at the taluk level as accredited journalists.
points.title: Social Security

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: P. Welfare of Journalists and Media Workers

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu CM Stalin announces pension hike for journalists
evidence.snippet: CM Stalin ordered an increase in the pension for journalists from ₹12,000 to ₹15,000 and the family pension from ₹6,000 to ₹7,500 in March 2026.

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu government enhances solatium to kin of journalists
evidence.snippet: The solatium for the kin of journalists who died after completing 20 years of service has been increased from ₹5 lakh to ₹10 lakh in December 2024.

plan_existence.notes: The pension amount of Rs. 15,000 was already implemented in March 2026. The promise of Rs. 15 lakh insurance is an increase from the current Rs. 10 lakh. The expansion of accreditation to taluk-level journalists is a new administrative proposal.

fiscal.comments: The pension is already at the promised level. Increasing the death benefit from 10L to 15L for a relatively small pool of accredited journalists (approx. 3,000-4,000) has a negligible fiscal impact.

legal.comments: The state has full authority to set accreditation rules and welfare benefits for journalists.

administrative.comments: Expanding accreditation to the taluk level requires a more decentralized verification process but is administratively straightforward for the I&PR department.

timeline.comments: These changes can be implemented via Government Orders (GO) within months.

political.comments: Journalist welfare schemes generally receive positive media coverage and face no political opposition.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible as the most expensive part (pension) is already implemented. The remaining points are incremental improvements to existing welfare structures.
key_risks: Verification of 'active' status for taluk-level journalists
Potential for misuse of accreditation cards

points.subsection_title: Q. Welfare of Lawyers and Legal Advisors
points.text: Lawyers with 20 years of practice experience and an annual income of less than Rs. 5 lakh will be given a pension of Rs. 3,000 per month. Life insurance will be provided to all registered lawyers.
points.title: 1 Tamil Nadu Lawyers' Welfare Fund

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: Q. Welfare of Lawyers and Legal Advisors

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Advocates' Welfare Fund - Bar Council of Tamil Nadu and Puducherry
evidence.snippet: The fund provides a death benefit of Rs. 10 lakhs to the nominees of deceased advocates. There is also a stipend of Rs. 3,000 per month for junior advocates for two years.

plan_existence.notes: While a death benefit and a junior advocate stipend (Rs. 3,000) exist, there is currently no monthly pension scheme for senior lawyers with 20 years of experience. This promise expands the welfare net to include a monthly pension for low-income veteran lawyers.

legal.comments: The state has the authority to create and fund welfare schemes for professionals through the Bar Council and state-funded welfare boards.

administrative.comments: The Bar Council of Tamil Nadu and Puducherry already has the infrastructure to verify years of practice and registration, making delivery feasible.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented within a single budget cycle by amending the Advocates' Welfare Fund rules.

political.comments: Lawyers are a politically influential group; such welfare measures generally face no opposition.

feasibility.overall_comments: The proposal is highly feasible as it builds on existing administrative structures. The primary challenge would be the recurring fiscal commitment, though the income ceiling of Rs. 5 lakh limits the beneficiary pool.
key_risks: Verification of actual annual income
Potential demand for higher pension amounts over time
Funding sustainability of the Welfare Fund

points.subsection_title: Q. Welfare of Lawyers and Legal Advisors
points.text: To honour legal advisors who provide free legal advice and legal aid to rural people, a monthly honorarium of Rs. 5,000 will be given.
points.title: 2 Honorarium for Legal Advisors

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: Q. Welfare of Lawyers and Legal Advisors

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu State Legal Services Authority (TNSLSA) Panel Lawyers

evidence.title: TNSLSA - Payment of Honorarium
evidence.snippet: Panel lawyers and para-legal volunteers are paid honorariums based on cases handled or sittings attended, rather than a fixed monthly salary.

plan_existence.notes: Existing schemes pay per case or per session. A fixed monthly honorarium of Rs. 5,000 for rural legal aid is a shift from a transaction-based model to a fixed-income model for legal advisors.

legal.comments: State governments can provide honorariums for social services under existing legal aid frameworks.

administrative.comments: Requires a robust mechanism to verify 'free' services provided to rural people to prevent misuse of the honorarium.

timeline.comments: Setting up the verification and disbursement system through District Legal Services Authorities (DLSA) would take 1-2 years.

political.comments: Generally popular as it improves rural access to justice, though it may be viewed as a small amount for professional services.

feasibility.overall_comments: The plan is administratively viable through the existing TNSLSA network but requires strict auditing to ensure the 'free' legal aid is actually reaching the rural poor.
key_risks: Difficulty in verifying 'free' services provided
Ghost beneficiaries
Low incentive for high-quality legal advice at this price point

points.text: Registered construction workers will be given accident insurance worth Rs. 5 lakh. Restructuring the Construction Workers' Welfare Board: The deficiencies in the welfare board set up for construction workers will be removed, and it will be restructured to function fully.
points.title: Social Security

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu Construction Workers Welfare Board (TNCWWB) Benefits

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Construction Workers Welfare Board - Welfare Schemes
evidence.snippet: The board already provides Rs. 5,00,000 for accidental death and varying amounts for disability and other injuries.

plan_existence.notes: The Rs. 5 lakh accident insurance (for death) is already an active policy of the TNCWWB. The promise to 'restructure' the board addresses long-standing administrative delays and corruption complaints.

fiscal.comments: The 5 lakh benefit is already budgeted and active. Restructuring costs are administrative and typically low.

legal.comments: The state has full authority to restructure its own welfare boards.

administrative.comments: Restructuring a large, often bureaucratic board with millions of members is complex and prone to internal resistance.

timeline.comments: Administrative restructuring can be initiated immediately, though full efficiency gains may take years.

political.comments: Highly popular among the large construction labor vote bank; restructuring is a common demand of trade unions.

feasibility.overall_comments: The insurance component is already in place, making that part redundant. The restructuring is the core of the promise and is feasible but requires significant political will to overcome bureaucratic inertia.
key_risks: Bureaucratic resistance to restructuring
Ensuring digital transparency in a traditionally manual board
Maintaining the 5 lakh payout speed

points.subsection_title: S. Welfare of Palmyra Climbers and Salt Pan Workers
points.text: The palmyra tree, which is Tamil Nadu's state tree and one of the markers of Tamil life, is dwindling. To revive it, the following schemes will be implemented: *The palmyra tree will be declared a protected tree. Further, a Tamil Nadu Palmyra Development Corporation will be established and Rs. 200 crore allocated to it. Using that fund, padaneer processing, palmyra jaggery production and the export of value-added palmyra products will be planned. *During periods when palmyra products are not produced, workers engaged in palmyra-based trades will be given a monthly livelihood support of Rs. 3,000.
points.title: 1 Palmyra Tree Protection

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: S. Welfare of Palmyra Climbers and Salt Pan Workers

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu Palm Products Development Board

evidence.title: TN Govt bans felling of palmyra trees - The Hindu
evidence.snippet: In 2021, the Tamil Nadu government made it mandatory to get permission from the District Collector to cut palmyra trees.

plan_existence.notes: The tree is already protected by law. A 'Board' exists; upgrading it to a 'Corporation' with a specific Rs. 200 crore fund and introducing a monthly livelihood support of Rs. 3,000 are the new elements.

fiscal.comments: The Rs. 200 crore allocation is explicitly stated and is a manageable amount within the state's agriculture/MSME budget.

legal.comments: The state has the power to create corporations and declare trees as protected species.

administrative.comments: The existing Board can be converted into a Corporation. Livelihood support can be distributed via the existing welfare board for palm tree workers.

timeline.comments: Setting up the corporation and processing units can be achieved in 2-3 years.

political.comments: Strong cultural resonance as the Palmyra is the state tree; supports a traditional and marginalized community.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a well-defined, culturally significant, and fiscally grounded promise. The transition from a Board to a Corporation with a dedicated fund for value-addition is a logical step for economic revival of the sector.
key_risks: Market volatility for value-added products
Ensuring the Rs. 3,000 reaches actual workers during off-season
Competition from other sweeteners/products

points.subsection_title: S. Welfare of Palmyra Climbers and Salt Pan Workers
points.text: The monsoon relief amount given to salt pan workers will be raised to Rs. 7,000.

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 2 Monsoon Relief for Salt Pan Workers
S. Welfare of Palmyra Climbers and Salt Pan Workers

existing_scheme_names: Monsoon Relief Assistance Scheme for Salt Pan Workers

evidence.title: Two years on, TN salt pan workers' welfare board remains only on paper
evidence.snippet: In 2022, the state government launched the monsoon relief assistance scheme providing a sum of Rs 5,000 each to 16,562 families working in salt pans across the state.

plan_existence.notes: The Tamil Nadu government currently provides Rs 5,000 as monsoon relief to registered salt pan workers. This promise proposes a 40% increase in the relief amount to Rs 7,000.

fiscal.comments: Based on current beneficiary data of ~16,562 families, the total outlay at Rs 7,000 per family would be approximately Rs 11.6 crore. This is a negligible fraction of the state budget and highly feasible.

legal.comments: No legal hurdles; the state already has the executive power to disburse welfare funds through the Manual Workers Welfare Board.

administrative.comments: The mechanism for DBT (Direct Benefit Transfer) is already in place, though registration of all workers in the welfare board remains a challenge.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented immediately in the first monsoon season of the government's term.

political.comments: Strongly positive reception in coastal districts like Thoothukudi where salt production is a major livelihood.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a highly feasible and low-cost welfare promise that builds on an existing successful scheme with a clear target group.
key_risks: Exclusion of unregistered workers from the relief net
Delays in the formal constitution of a dedicated Salt Pan Workers Welfare Board

points.text: For every woman head of a family, Rs. 2,500 will be credited to her bank account every month until she turns 60. Through this, women heads of families will move away from depending on others for their basic expenses, and their economic independence will be protected.
points.title: 1 Honoured Women Scheme

evidence.title: Kalaignar Magalir Urimai Thogai: Income ceiling likely to be increased
evidence.snippet: The KMUT scheme currently benefits around 1,16,50,000 women... providing them with financial assistance of ₹1000 per month.

plan_existence.notes: The current KMUT scheme provides Rs 1,000 per month to eligible women. This promise proposes increasing the amount to Rs 2,500 (a 150% increase) and adds an age cap of 60 years.

fiscal.comments: The current KMUT scheme costs ~Rs 13,722 crore annually for 1.16 crore women at Rs 1,000/month. Increasing this to Rs 2,500 would cost approximately Rs 34,300 crore per year, representing nearly 10% of the state's total revenue receipts, which is extremely taxing for a debt-stressed state.

legal.comments: No legal barriers; cash transfers are a standard policy tool.

administrative.comments: The administrative infrastructure for KMUT (DBT, eligibility verification) is already robust and can handle the increased amount.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented in phases, but the full fiscal impact would be felt immediately upon rollout.

political.comments: Extremely high political appeal as it significantly outbids the current government's flagship scheme.

feasibility.overall_comments: While administratively simple and politically potent, the fiscal sustainability of a Rs 34,000+ crore annual commitment is highly questionable given Tamil Nadu's current fiscal deficit.
key_risks: Severe fiscal strain leading to cuts in capital expenditure
Inflationary pressure due to massive liquidity injection
Political backlash if eligibility criteria are tightened to manage costs

points.text: Currently, free travel for women is implemented only on certain government buses. Changing this, the Vettri Payanam scheme — free travel for women — will be implemented on all state-run town, city and inter-district government buses across the state.
points.title: 3 Vettri Payanam (Victory Journey)

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Vidiyal Payanam Thittam
evidence.snippet: The scheme provides free travel for women in ordinary town buses operated by State Transport Undertakings.

evidence.title: 80 new BS6 buses to operate under Vidiyal Payanam scheme

plan_existence.notes: The current 'Vidiyal Payanam' scheme is restricted to 'ordinary' (white board) town buses. The promise expands this to all state-run buses, including Express, Deluxe, and inter-district services, which are currently excluded.

fiscal.comments: Expanding free travel to inter-district and deluxe buses will exponentially increase the subsidy burden on State Transport Undertakings (STUs), which already face significant operational losses.

legal.comments: State governments have full authority over state-run transport corporations and fare structures.

administrative.comments: The mechanism for 'zero-ticket' issuance is already functional across the state; expanding it to other bus types is administratively straightforward.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented immediately via a government order (GO).

political.comments: Extremely popular welfare measure with high public reception, though it may face criticism regarding the financial health of STUs.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible administratively as the infrastructure exists, but the fiscal sustainability is a major concern given the existing debt of Tamil Nadu's transport corporations.
key_risks: Increased fiscal deficit of STUs
Potential reduction in bus maintenance quality due to fund diversion
Overcrowding in long-distance buses

key_risks: Fiscal deficit expansion
Inflationary pressure on state budget
Verification of 'destitute' status to prevent leakage

points.text: Women's self-help groups will be given interest-free loans of up to Rs. 5 lakh. Women's self-help groups that aim to become micro and small entrepreneurs will be given Rs. 5 lakh as a non-repayable investment grant. Women's self-help groups that perform exceptionally well for 5 consecutive years will be given an incentive of Rs. 1 lakh. An e-commerce platform will be created to market products made by women's self-help groups internationally.
points.title: 8 Singappengal Development Scheme

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu State Rural Livelihood Mission (TNSRLM)

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Corporation for Development of Women
evidence.snippet: TNCDW implements the Mahalir Thittam and provides credit linkage to SHGs. Mathi Bazaar is an existing e-commerce portal for SHG products.

plan_existence.notes: While SHG credit linkages and the Mathi Bazaar e-commerce platform exist, the specific 'interest-free' nature up to 5L and the 'non-repayable grant' of 5L for MSE conversion are significant upgrades over current interest subvention and revolving fund models.

fiscal.comments: Non-repayable grants of Rs. 5 lakh for SHGs transitioning to MSEs is a high-cost capital subsidy. Interest-free loans require the state to bear the entire interest burden, which is costly given the volume of SHG lending in TN (over Rs. 30,000 crore annually).

legal.comments: No legal barriers; the state can provide subsidies and grants through executive policy.

administrative.comments: TNCDW (Mahalir Thittam) has a strong grassroots network to implement this, though vetting 'exceptionally well-performing' groups for the 1L incentive requires clear metrics.

timeline.comments: Phased implementation over 5 years is realistic for the grant and incentive components.

political.comments: SHGs are a powerful political constituency in Tamil Nadu; this scheme would be very popular.

feasibility.overall_comments: Strong administrative foundation exists, but the fiscal commitment for non-repayable grants and full interest waivers is substantial and requires careful budgetary planning.
key_risks: High fiscal burden of interest subvention
Potential for 'ghost' SHGs to claim grants
Market competition for e-commerce platform

points.text: Food items, linen and school supplies required by government agencies and government service organisations are currently purchased from many private firms. Changing this, 40 percent of such procurement will be made from women's self-help groups and the micro and small enterprises run by them. Through this, stable income for thousands of women entrepreneurs will be ensured.
points.title: 9 Magalir Mahasakthi

existing_scheme_names: Public Procurement Policy for Micro and Small Enterprises (MSEs) Order, 2012

evidence.title: Public Procurement Policy for MSEs - Ministry of MSME
evidence.snippet: Central Government Ministries/CPSEs must procure 25% from MSEs, with a sub-target of 3% from women-owned MSEs.

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu MSME Policy 2021
evidence.snippet: The policy encourages procurement from local MSEs but does not mandate a 40% quota specifically for women-run enterprises.

plan_existence.notes: The current national mandate for women-owned MSEs is only 3%. A 40% reservation for women SHGs/MSEs in state procurement would be a pioneering and drastic increase in the quota.

fiscal.comments: This is largely budget-neutral as it redirects existing procurement spending rather than requiring new funds, though SHG products might occasionally have different price points than bulk private contractors.

legal.comments: May face legal challenges regarding 'right to equality' in trade if the reservation is deemed excessive (40% for one specific gender/group), though Article 15(3) allows special provisions for women.

administrative.comments: Extremely difficult to implement. SHGs often lack the scale, quality certifications (ISO, etc.), and supply chain consistency to meet 40% of all government needs for linen, school supplies, and food items.

timeline.comments: Achieving 40% capacity in SHGs would take the full 5-year term or longer to build the necessary industrial capacity.

political.comments: Will be welcomed by women's groups but strongly opposed by existing private contractors and industrial lobbies.

feasibility.overall_comments: A bold economic move that shifts wealth to women, but the 40% target is likely too high for the current production capacity of SHGs, risking supply shortages for essential government services.
key_risks: Supply chain failure
Quality control issues
Legal challenges to procurement quotas
Corruption in SHG certification

points.text: Special industrial estates with infrastructure that fully meets women's needs will be set up in 10 cities: Chennai, Coimbatore, Madurai, Trichy, Salem, Vellore, Tiruppur, Thanjavur, Tuticorin and Tirunelveli.
points.title: 10 Special Industrial Estates

evidence.title: TANSIDCO - Industrial Estates for Women
evidence.snippet: TANSIDCO has already established exclusive industrial estates for women in places like Thirumullaivoyal (Chennai) and Kappalur (Madurai).

plan_existence.notes: The concept of women-only industrial estates exists in TN. This promise proposes a systematic expansion to 10 specific major cities, ensuring standardized infrastructure (creches, security, etc.) across the state's urban hubs.

fiscal.comments: Setting up 10 industrial estates requires significant capital for land acquisition and infrastructure. However, these are often self-financing in the long run through lease/sale of plots.

legal.comments: Standard land acquisition laws apply. No major legal hurdles specifically for women-centric estates.

administrative.comments: TANSIDCO and SIPCOT have decades of experience in developing and managing industrial estates.

timeline.comments: Land acquisition and basic infrastructure development for 10 estates can be completed within a 5-year term.

political.comments: Strongly aligns with the state's goal of increasing Women's Labour Force Participation Rate (LFPR).

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible and builds on an existing successful model. The focus on 10 specific cities provides a clear roadmap for urban industrial growth tailored to women.
key_risks: Land acquisition delays
Under-utilization of plots in smaller cities
High cost of urban land in cities like Chennai/Coimbatore

points.text: To enhance women's safety, 500 surveillance teams named after Rani Velu Nachiyar will be set up across Tamil Nadu. Composed entirely of women, these teams will carry out surveillance work in plain clothes with body-worn cameras.
points.title: 1 Rani Velu Nachiyar Force

evidence.title: Chennai Police's 'Amma Patrol' to check crimes against women
evidence.snippet: The Greater Chennai Police launched 'Amma Patrol' teams to prevent crimes against women and children, using specially equipped vehicles.

plan_existence.notes: While 'Amma Patrol' and 'Pink Patrol' exist in various TN cities, they typically use marked vehicles. A dedicated 500-team force specifically in plain clothes with body cameras under the name 'Rani Velu Nachiyar Force' is a new organizational proposal.

legal.comments: State government has full authority over 'Police' and 'Public Order' under the Seventh Schedule of the Constitution.

administrative.comments: The Tamil Nadu Police department has the infrastructure to create specialized wings; however, recruiting and training 500 dedicated all-women teams requires significant human resource planning.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented within a 5-year term through phased recruitment and procurement of body-worn cameras.

political.comments: Naming the force after a local historical icon (Rani Velu Nachiyar) and focusing on women's safety is likely to receive high public and political support.

feasibility.overall_comments: The plan is highly feasible as it builds on existing police structures. The use of plainclothes officers and body cameras is a standard modern policing tactic for surveillance.
key_risks: High recurring cost of personnel
Privacy concerns regarding plainclothes surveillance
Maintenance of body-worn camera data

points.text: The Kaavalan app currently in use for women's safety will be upgraded, and a 'Smart Panic Button' will be installed in all buses, share autos and similar vehicles running across Tamil Nadu. This will be connected to AI technology and monitored 24 hours. CCTV cameras integrated with AI technology will be installed at all major public places. All unsafe areas with low footfall will be identified, and appropriate safety features will be put in place in those areas.
points.title: 2 Integrated Surveillance Centre Powered by AI

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 2 Integrated Surveillance Centre Powered by AI

existing_scheme_names: Nirbhaya Fund - Panic Buttons in Buses

evidence.title: Panic buttons, CCTV cameras in 500 Chennai buses soon
evidence.snippet: Under the Nirbhaya Fund, the MTC has started installing panic buttons and CCTV cameras in buses, connected to a central monitoring system.

evidence.title: Kaavalan-SOS - Tamil Nadu Police App
evidence.snippet: The Kaavalan SOS app is an existing mobile application developed by the TN Police for citizens to seek immediate help.

plan_existence.notes: The Kaavalan app and panic buttons in MTC buses already exist. The promise expands this to 'all' buses and share autos statewide and integrates AI technology for monitoring, which is currently limited to specific pilot projects like the Chennai Safe City Project.

legal.comments: Privacy laws regarding AI-based facial recognition and continuous surveillance are evolving in India; compliance with the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 will be necessary.

administrative.comments: Equipping thousands of share autos and private buses with smart panic buttons and maintaining a 24/7 AI monitoring center is a massive technical and logistical challenge.

timeline.comments: Statewide rollout across all public transport and major public places is unlikely to be completed in 5 years given the current pace of Safe City projects.

political.comments: Strong public demand for tech-driven safety measures; minimal political opposition expected.

feasibility.overall_comments: Technically ambitious but administratively difficult. While it builds on existing infrastructure (Kaavalan, Nirbhaya), the scale of 'all' vehicles and 'all' public places is extremely high.
key_risks: High cost of AI infrastructure
Technical failure of panic buttons in informal transport (share autos)
Data privacy and surveillance overreach

points.text: Special fast-track courts will be set up to investigate crimes against women and deliver justice quickly.
points.title: 3 Special Fast-Track Courts for Women

evidence.title: Fast Track Special Courts (FTSCs) for expeditious disposal of cases
evidence.snippet: The Department of Justice implements a Centrally Sponsored Scheme for setting up FTSCs for rape and POCSO Act cases.

plan_existence.notes: Tamil Nadu already has Mahila Courts and Fast Track Special Courts (FTSCs) for crimes against women. This promise would involve increasing the number of such courts or expanding their jurisdiction beyond current central schemes.

legal.comments: Setting up courts requires mandatory consultation and concurrence with the Madras High Court. The state cannot unilaterally create judicial posts.

administrative.comments: The primary bottleneck is the shortage of judicial officers and support staff, which requires a lengthy recruitment process through the TNPSC or High Court.

timeline.comments: Physical infrastructure can be built quickly, but staffing usually takes 2-3 years.

political.comments: High political will as it addresses public outcry over delayed justice in crimes against women.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is a continuation/expansion of existing central and state policies. Its success depends entirely on the cooperation of the Judiciary and the speed of judge appointments.
key_risks: Judicial vacancy rates
Coordination with the High Court
Potential for fast-tracking to compromise trial quality

points.text: Hostels with proper safety and modern amenities will be set up in major cities across Tamil Nadu for working women and students. The maximum monthly charge for these will be only Rs. 2,500.
points.title: 4 Hostels for Working Women and Students

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 4 Hostels for Working Women and Students

existing_scheme_names: Thozhi (Tamil Nadu Working Women's Hostels Corporation)

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Working Women's Hostels Corporation (Thozhi)
evidence.snippet: The TN government provides safe and affordable accommodation for working women through 'Thozhi' hostels in various districts.

plan_existence.notes: The 'Thozhi' scheme already provides hostels. However, current monthly charges in Thozhi hostels often exceed Rs. 2,500 (ranging from 3,000 to 7,000+ depending on the city and room type). The promise introduces a strict price cap of Rs. 2,500.

fiscal.comments: A cap of Rs. 2,500 for 'modern amenities' in major cities like Chennai is significantly below market rates and likely below the operational cost per bed, necessitating heavy state subsidies.

legal.comments: The state has the authority to set tariffs for government-run or subsidized hostels.

administrative.comments: The TNWWHC (Thozhi) already has the administrative framework to manage these hostels; scaling up is feasible.

timeline.comments: New hostels can be constructed or existing buildings repurposed within 5 years.

political.comments: Extremely popular among the growing demographic of migrating female workers and students.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible administratively as it builds on the 'Thozhi' model, but the Rs. 2,500 price cap will require substantial and permanent fiscal support from the state budget to maintain 'modern' standards.
key_risks: Fiscal burden of subsidies
Maintenance of quality standards under low pricing
Overcrowding and long waitlists

points.text: Pregnant women will be given Rs. 25,000 in financial aid to help them buy nutritious food, carry out monthly medical check-ups and meet essential needs. 'Thaai Care' women's service and help centres will be set up across the state. Pregnant women can stay at these centres and benefit from their services as their delivery date approaches. At these centres, pregnant women will be provided with free food, medical aid and continuous medical monitoring.
points.title: 1 Grant for Pregnant Women

existing_scheme_names: Dr. Muthulakshmi Reddy Maternity Benefit Scheme (MRMBS)

evidence.title: Dr. Muthulakshmi Reddy Maternity Benefit Scheme | Vikaspedia
evidence.snippet: Currently Rs. 18,000/- will be provided under Dr Muthulakshmi Reddy Maternity Benefit Scheme out of which Rs. 14,000/- is cash assistance and 2 nutrition kits worth Rs. 4,000/-.

evidence.title: Over 10,000 women benefit from Dr. Muthulakshmi Reddy Maternity Benefit Scheme in Tiruchi
evidence.snippet: Under the scheme, ₹18,000 is given to pregnant women for nutritional needs. The amount includes ₹14,000 in cash given in three instalments.

plan_existence.notes: The existing MRMBS provides Rs. 18,000. The promise proposes an increase to Rs. 25,000. Additionally, 'Thaai Care' centres resemble existing 'Birth Waiting Rooms' in hilly areas, but the promise suggests a state-wide expansion of such residential facilities.

fiscal.comments: Increasing the grant from Rs. 18,000 to Rs. 25,000 for approximately 9-10 lakh annual births in TN would require an additional outlay of ~Rs. 630-700 crore per year.

legal.comments: Maternity benefits are within state legislative competence and align with Directive Principles.

administrative.comments: The PICME portal and existing health infrastructure (PHCs/VHN network) are robust enough to handle the cash transfer and monitoring.

timeline.comments: Cash transfer increases can be implemented immediately; building 'Thaai Care' centres across the state would take 2-4 years.

political.comments: Highly popular welfare measure with no significant political opposition.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is a logical expansion of a highly successful existing scheme. The fiscal burden is manageable given the state's focus on health, though the residential 'Thaai Care' centres will require significant capital expenditure.
key_risks: Fiscal burden of increased cash transfers
Land acquisition for new care centres
Staffing requirements for 24/7 monitoring at centres

points.text: Free sanitary napkins will be provided to women at ration shops, schools and colleges. Sanitary napkin vending machines will be installed at important locations in every town. Arrangements will be made in all government school toilets to properly dispose of used sanitary napkins.
points.title: 2 Free Sanitary Napkins

evidence.title: TN government cites Rs 4,000 crore cost, says no plans to supply sanitary napkins via ration shops
evidence.snippet: The government informed the Madras High Court that distributing sanitary napkins through ration shops would cost around Rs 4,000 crore and there is no such proposal.

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu government to provide free sanitary napkins to women in urban areas
evidence.snippet: The State government provides free sanitary napkins to government school girls and women inpatients at government medical institutions.

plan_existence.notes: Free napkins are currently provided to rural girls (10-19) and urban slum women. The promise expands this to ration shops and colleges, which the current government recently deemed fiscally unviable.

fiscal.comments: The TN government officially estimated a cost of Rs. 4,000 crore for distribution via ration shops, which is a massive recurring expenditure.

legal.comments: No legal hurdles; supported by Supreme Court directives on menstrual hygiene in schools.

administrative.comments: Logistics of supplying napkins to 35,000+ ration shops and maintaining vending machines is complex.

timeline.comments: Vending machine installation and school disposal systems can be completed within 2-3 years.

political.comments: High political appeal as it addresses 'period poverty' and dignity.

feasibility.overall_comments: While socially progressive, the fiscal cost is the primary barrier. The state has already expressed reluctance due to the Rs. 4,000 crore price tag, making this a high-cost promise.
key_risks: Extreme fiscal burden
Supply chain leakage in ration shops
Maintenance of vending machines

points.text: Free HPV vaccines will be given to all girls between the ages of 9 and 14. Additionally, free cervical cancer screening will be done for all women above 30 at primary health centres across Tamil Nadu.
points.title: 3 Free Cervical Cancer Screening and Vaccination

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 3 Free Cervical Cancer Screening and Vaccination

evidence.title: T.N. to launch HPV vaccination programme in four districts
evidence.snippet: Tamil Nadu is set to roll out the HPV vaccination programme... starting with four districts: Ariyalur, Perambalur, Tiruvannamalai, and Dharmapuri.

evidence.title: Cervical cancer prevention: Tamil Nadu to roll out HPV vaccination pilot
evidence.snippet: The pilot aims at benefiting 27,196 girls aged 14... based on findings, we plan to scale up the programme across the state.

plan_existence.notes: HPV vaccination is currently in a pilot phase in 4 districts. Screening for women >30 is already an established program in PHCs across the state.

fiscal.comments: The state has already allocated Rs. 36 crore for the pilot. Full rollout for ~3.38 lakh girls is estimated to be manageable within the health budget.

legal.comments: Standard public health intervention with no legal barriers.

administrative.comments: TN has one of India's best immunization and PHC networks, capable of universal rollout.

timeline.comments: Can be achieved within 1-2 years as the pilot is already active.

political.comments: Strong public support for cancer prevention; aligns with national health goals.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a highly feasible and necessary expansion of an existing pilot. The infrastructure and political will are already in place.
key_risks: Vaccine hesitancy/misinformation
Ensuring two-dose completion
Cold chain logistics for state-wide scale

points.text: Free solar stoves will be provided to tribal and hill-dwelling women to reduce the hardships of using firewood stoves.
points.title: 4 Solar Stoves for Tribal Women:

evidence.title: Get Your Free Solar Stove at Home — Government Launches New Scheme
evidence.snippet: The Government of India has launched a new scheme... in collaboration with Indian Oil Corporation (IOC), which will provide solar stoves.

plan_existence.notes: There is no specific Tamil Nadu state scheme for free solar stoves for tribal women. The Central government has a pilot for 'Surya Nutan' indoor solar cookers, but it is not yet a mass distribution welfare scheme in TN.

fiscal.comments: Tribal population in TN is relatively small (~1.1% of total), making the total cost of distribution manageable even at high unit prices (~Rs. 15,000/stove).

legal.comments: Falls under Tribal Sub-Plan (TSP) and environmental welfare.

administrative.comments: Reaching remote hill-dwelling tribal settlements for installation and maintenance is a significant logistical challenge.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented within 2-3 years through the Tribal Welfare Department.

political.comments: Positive reception among tribal communities; aligns with green energy goals.

feasibility.overall_comments: A niche but highly impactful promise for a vulnerable population. The small scale makes it fiscally viable, though maintenance in remote areas will be the main hurdle.
key_risks: Logistical difficulty in remote terrain
Cultural adoption of solar cooking vs. traditional methods
Maintenance and repair in isolated regions

points.text: Unemployed graduates waiting after registering at the employment exchange will be given a monthly grant of Rs. 4,000. Young people who have completed 12th standard or ITI/diploma courses and are waiting for jobs will be given a monthly grant of Rs. 2,500. This grant will be paid for the first two years from the date of registration. This grant will help in their skill development.
points.title: 1 Youth Vettri Fund

existing_scheme_names: Unemployment Assistance Scheme for Educated Youth

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Unemployment Assistance Scheme
evidence.snippet: The scheme provides monthly assistance to unemployed youth registered with employment exchanges for 5 years. Current rates are Rs. 600 for graduates and Rs. 400 for HSC holders.

plan_existence.notes: The promise significantly increases the monthly grant from the current Rs. 200-600 range to Rs. 2,500-4,000. It also drastically reduces the waiting period from the current 5-year requirement to immediate eligibility upon registration for a 2-year duration.

fiscal.comments: With millions of youth registered in TN employment exchanges, increasing the payout by 6-7 times and removing the 5-year waiting period will create a massive recurring expenditure. No specific budget outlay is provided in the text.

legal.comments: The state has full authority to define and fund welfare schemes for its residents.

administrative.comments: The existing employment exchange infrastructure can be used, but verifying 'unemployed' status to prevent leakage at this higher pay scale will be challenging.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented via a Government Order (GO) within the first year of a new term.

political.comments: High populist appeal among the youth demographic, which is a key voting bloc.

feasibility.overall_comments: While fiscally demanding, the scheme is a direct expansion of an existing welfare pillar. The primary challenge is the fiscal sustainability of a 700% increase in benefit levels.
key_risks: Fiscal deficit increase
Verification of unemployment status
Incentivizing long-term unemployment

points.text: A special law will be enacted to ensure that appointments to government jobs are made fairly and transparently. Through this law, all procedures from the announcement of vacancies to the appointments will be completed within one year.
points.title: 2 Transparency in Employment

evidence.title: Bill passed to let TNPSC handle university staff hiring
evidence.snippet: The state Assembly passed a Bill in 2025 to establish a more uniform and transparent system for recruiting non-teaching staff via TNPSC.

plan_existence.notes: While various recruitment boards (TNPSC, TRB, MRB) exist and have their own procedures, there is no overarching 'Transparency Law' that mandates a strict 1-year completion timeline for all government recruitments.

fiscal.comments: Enacting a law has minimal direct fiscal cost, though speeding up recruitment may increase administrative overhead.

legal.comments: The state can legislate on state public services (Entry 41, State List), but the law must not conflict with existing constitutional protections for recruitment boards.

administrative.comments: A 1-year mandate from vacancy to appointment is extremely difficult due to common delays in certificate verification, medical tests, and frequent litigation/stay orders on recruitment processes.

timeline.comments: The law can be passed quickly, but achieving the 1-year cycle across all departments within a 5-year term is highly ambitious.

political.comments: Strong public support as recruitment delays and lack of transparency are major grievances for youth.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is legally sound but administratively idealistic. Mandating a 1-year timeline by law often fails when faced with the reality of judicial interventions and bureaucratic bottlenecks.
key_risks: Judicial stays on recruitment
Administrative capacity of TNPSC/TRB
Quality of vetting vs. speed

points.text: All vacant posts in government departments will be filled swiftly.
points.title: 3 Filling Vacant Posts

evidence.title: TN Govt Jobs 2026 2027 - 31031 Vacancies
evidence.snippet: Current notifications show over 31,000 vacancies being processed across various departments in Tamil Nadu for 2026.

plan_existence.notes: Filling vacancies is a continuous administrative function of the state government. This promise is a commitment to accelerate an existing process.

legal.comments: Standard executive function; no legal barriers.

administrative.comments: Requires massive coordination across all departments to identify and notify vacancies to recruitment boards.

timeline.comments: Significant progress can be made in a 5-year term, though 'all' vacancies is a moving target.

political.comments: Highly popular; addresses the core demand of the educated unemployed.

feasibility.overall_comments: A standard but essential manifesto promise. Success depends entirely on the efficiency of the state's recruitment boards and the availability of fiscal space for the resulting salary bill.
key_risks: Increased wage bill
Recruitment board backlogs
Litigation delays

points.text: All students studying from UG to PhD will be given collateral-free loans of up to Rs. 20 lakh. Through this, it will be ensured that students can study the course of their choice at the colleges of their choice without any financial barriers.
points.title: 4 Education Guarantee Scheme

existing_scheme_names: Credit Guarantee Fund Scheme for Education Loans (CGFSEL)

evidence.title: Pradhan Mantri Vidyalaxmi (PM-Vidyalaxmi) Scheme
evidence.snippet: Provides collateral-free education loans up to Rs. 7.5 lakh with a 75% credit guarantee. Interest subvention is available for loans up to Rs. 10 lakh for families with income up to Rs. 8 lakh.

evidence.title: TAMCO Education Loan
evidence.snippet: Tamil Nadu Minorities Economic Development Corporation provides education loans up to Rs. 20 lakh for minority students.

plan_existence.notes: The promise expands the collateral-free limit from the current central limit of Rs. 7.5 lakh to Rs. 20 lakh and makes it universal for all students in TN, regardless of minority status.

fiscal.comments: Providing a state guarantee for loans up to Rs. 20 lakh for all students creates a massive contingent liability. If default rates are high, the state's credit rating and budget could be severely impacted.

legal.comments: The state can set up its own credit guarantee fund, similar to the central CGFSEL.

administrative.comments: Requires a robust partnership with commercial banks who may still be hesitant to lend without collateral despite a state guarantee.

timeline.comments: A state-level credit guarantee corporation can be established within 1-2 years.

political.comments: Extremely popular among middle-class and low-income families facing rising costs of professional education.

feasibility.overall_comments: A high-impact promise that addresses a genuine gap (the gap between Rs. 7.5L and Rs. 20L for professional courses). However, the fiscal risk of acting as a guarantor for such large unsecured amounts is significant.
key_risks: High NPA/default rates
Bank reluctance to participate
State's contingent liability risk

points.text: To improve the skills of young people, internship training will be provided in partnership with government and private industrial firms. It will be ensured that 5 lakh youth benefit from this initiative every year. During this training period, graduates will be paid Rs. 10,000 per month and those with vocational education will be paid Rs. 8,000 per month. Priority will be given under this scheme to young people from families without permanent employment.
points.title: 5 Vettri Trade Training

evidence.title: Naan Mudhalvan - Apprenticeship & Internship
evidence.snippet: The Naan Mudhalvan scheme provides skill training and facilitates apprenticeships/internships with stipends ranging from ₹5,000 to ₹9,000 per month.

evidence.title: PM Internship Scheme - Ministry of Corporate Affairs
evidence.snippet: Provides 12-month internship opportunities in top 500 companies with a monthly stipend of ₹5,000.

plan_existence.notes: The proposed scheme expands on the existing Naan Mudhalvan platform by increasing the stipend to ₹10,000 for graduates and ₹8,000 for vocational trainees, while setting a specific annual target of 5 lakh beneficiaries.

fiscal.comments: At an average stipend of ₹9,000 per month for 5 lakh youth, the annual cost is approximately ₹5,400 crore. This is a significant but manageable outlay for a state with Tamil Nadu's budget size, especially if shared with private partners.

legal.comments: No legal hurdles; apprenticeship and skill development are standard state subjects and well-supported by existing labor laws.

administrative.comments: The existing Naan Mudhalvan infrastructure and TNSDC (Tamil Nadu Skill Development Corporation) provide a strong foundation for scaling this initiative.

timeline.comments: Can be rolled out immediately by augmenting existing portals and industry partnerships.

political.comments: Highly popular among the youth demographic; aligns with the national focus on employability.

feasibility.overall_comments: A well-structured expansion of existing skill initiatives. The primary challenge will be ensuring the quality of internships in the private sector and maintaining the high volume of placements annually.
key_risks: Quality of training in private firms
Budgetary sustainability
Matching skills with industry demand
