points.text: Young people who wish to start businesses will be given collateral-free loans of up to Rs. 25 lakh by the government. Through this, the dream of becoming an entrepreneur will come true for many young people.
points.title: 6 Vettri Entrepreneur Scheme

existing_scheme_names: New Entrepreneur cum Enterprise Development Scheme (NEEDS)

evidence.title: UYEGP - MSME Department, Tamil Nadu
evidence.snippet: UYEGP provides loans up to ₹15 lakh for manufacturing and ₹5 lakh for service/business with a 25% subsidy. Loans up to ₹10 lakh are collateral-free.

plan_existence.notes: The promise increases the collateral-free loan limit to ₹25 lakh, which is a significant jump from the current ₹10 lakh limit under UYEGP.

fiscal.comments: Increasing the collateral-free limit to ₹25 lakh increases the state's contingent liability. The fiscal impact depends on the default rate and the corpus of the credit guarantee fund.

legal.comments: State governments have the authority to provide credit guarantees and interest subventions for MSMEs.

administrative.comments: Can be implemented through the District Industries Centres (DIC) and existing tie-ups with nationalized/cooperative banks.

timeline.comments: Feasible within the first year by notifying revised guidelines for UYEGP/NEEDS.

political.comments: Strong appeal for aspiring entrepreneurs; however, bank cooperation for higher collateral-free limits can be a bottleneck.

feasibility.overall_comments: A practical expansion of existing MSME support. Success depends on the state's ability to convince banks to lend without collateral at this higher threshold.
key_risks: High NPA risk for banks
Bank reluctance to lend without collateral
Ensuring genuine business viability

points.text: Private firms that employ Tamil Nadu youth up to 75 percent of their workforce will be given a 2.5 percent subsidy on state GST, a 5 percent subsidy on electricity charges, and priority in government procurement. Through this scheme, it will be ensured that Tamil Nadu youth receive extensive employment.
points.title: 7 Local Jobs for Local Youth Scheme

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 7 Local Jobs for Local Youth Scheme

evidence.title: TN parties promise job reservation for locals
evidence.snippet: While various parties have promised 75% reservation for locals in manifestos, no such law or specific subsidy scheme currently exists in Tamil Nadu.

plan_existence.notes: Though the concept of local reservation has been discussed politically, the specific incentive structure (SGST and electricity subsidies) is a new policy proposal.

fiscal.comments: A 2.5% SGST subsidy and 5% electricity subsidy for a large number of firms would result in significant revenue loss for the state treasury.

legal.comments: Domicile-based reservation in the private sector has been struck down or stayed by various High Courts (e.g., Haryana) for violating Articles 14, 15, and 19(1)(g) of the Constitution.

administrative.comments: Verifying the 'local' status of 75% of a workforce across thousands of private firms is an administrative nightmare prone to corruption.

timeline.comments: Policy can be announced quickly, but legal challenges would likely stall implementation indefinitely.

political.comments: Highly popular 'sons of the soil' rhetoric, but may face pushback from industrial bodies and investors.

feasibility.overall_comments: While politically attractive, the scheme faces near-certain legal defeat in courts. The fiscal cost of subsidies is also poorly defined and potentially massive.
key_risks: Unconstitutional (Article 16/19)
Industrial flight to other states
Administrative corruption in verification

points.text: Under the Chief Minister's People's Service Friend Scheme, 5 lakh young people will be appointed as permanent staff in every village panchayat and urban local body and paid a monthly salary of Rs. 18,000. These staff will ensure that the state government's services and schemes reach every home. This joint initiative between the government and youth will help the administration function better.
points.title: 8 Chief Minister's People's Service Friend Scheme

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 8 Chief Minister's People's Service Friend Scheme

evidence.title: TVK Election Manifesto Promises
evidence.snippet: The TVK manifesto includes the 'Chief Minister's People's Service Friend' scheme to appoint 5 lakh youth at a salary of ₹18,000.

plan_existence.notes: This is a new proposal for a massive grassroots-level government workforce, distinct from existing temporary or volunteer-based schemes.

fiscal.comments: The annual cost for 5 lakh staff at ₹18,000/month is ₹10,800 crore. This would be one of the largest single-item salary expenditures in the state budget, posing a severe fiscal risk.

legal.comments: The state has the power to create posts, but 'permanent' status requires adherence to recruitment rules and long-term pension/benefit liabilities.

administrative.comments: Recruiting, training, and managing 5 lakh new permanent employees across every village and ward is a monumental task that the current bureaucracy is not equipped for.

timeline.comments: Creating 5 lakh permanent positions and completing recruitment within 5 years is highly unlikely given typical government hiring speeds.

political.comments: Massive appeal as a direct job creation promise, though it may be criticized as fiscally irresponsible 'populism'.

feasibility.overall_comments: Fiscally reckless and administratively improbable. While it addresses service delivery gaps, the scale of permanent hiring is unsustainable for the state's finances.
key_risks: Fiscal bankruptcy
Recruitment scams
Long-term pension liability
Redundancy with existing local body staff

points.text: The following schemes will be implemented to encourage creators who take the greatness of Tamil land's history, art, culture, language, food and music to the world through digital platforms. 1.5 lakh creators will be selected, and their land-based creations will be funded for production costs. 500 creative skill training centres will be set up across Tamil Nadu. Training in video production, digital marketing, content strategy and similar fields will be given at these centres.
points.title: 9 Digital Creators Encouragement Scheme

evidence.title: Government unveils $1 billion fund for the creator economy ahead of WAVES 2025
evidence.snippet: The Indian government has initiated a $1 billion fund to support digital content creators, focusing on capital access, skill development, and global market integration.

evidence.title: 8 crore creators in India but only 1.5 lakh able to monetise: Report
evidence.snippet: India now has at least 8 crore creators but only 1.5 lakh professional content creators are able to monetise their services effectively.

plan_existence.notes: While the Central Government has announced a $1 billion fund for the creator economy, there is no specific Tamil Nadu state scheme that provides production cost funding for 1.5 lakh creators. The target number '1.5 lakh' appears to be based on industry reports regarding the number of currently monetizing creators in India.

legal.comments: No legal barriers to providing grants or training for digital skills.

administrative.comments: Setting up 500 specialized training centres and managing production grants for 1.5 lakh individuals is a massive logistical challenge for the state's skill development machinery.

timeline.comments: Establishing 500 centres and selecting 1.5 lakh beneficiaries would likely take the full 5-year term to reach scale.

political.comments: Highly popular among the youth demographic and aligns with the 'soft power' goals of promoting Tamil culture.

feasibility.overall_comments: The scheme is highly ambitious in scale, particularly the 500 centres and 1.5 lakh beneficiaries. While it aligns with global trends in the creator economy, the administrative burden of verifying and funding individual production costs for such a large group is significant.
key_risks: High administrative overhead in beneficiary selection
Potential for misuse of production grants
Difficulty in maintaining quality across 500 training centres
Sustainability of funding without clear ROI for the state

points.text: To ensure youth participation in Tamil Nadu government policy and administrative decisions, a Tamil Nadu Youth Advisory Council will be created. This council will directly convey their views and suggestions to the Chief Minister and other senior government officials. This council will serve as a bridge between government administration and the thinking of young people.
points.title: 10 Tamil Nadu Youth Advisory Council

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Assembly Elections: Campaigning in full swing
evidence.snippet: The Tamilaga Vetri Kazhagam led by actor Vijay released its manifesto... Tamilnadu Youth Advisory Council for youth input.

plan_existence.notes: There is currently no formal 'Youth Advisory Council' that provides a direct bridge to the Chief Minister in Tamil Nadu, although the CM's Fellowship Programme involves youth in policy implementation.

fiscal.comments: Low fiscal impact as it is primarily an administrative/advisory body.

legal.comments: Can be established via executive order or a simple government order (GO).

administrative.comments: Easy to set up, but requires consistent engagement from senior officials to be effective.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented within the first year of government.

political.comments: Strong appeal to first-time voters and youth; minimal political opposition.

feasibility.overall_comments: A highly feasible, low-cost governance initiative that enhances youth representation. Its success depends entirely on whether the council's advice is actually integrated into policy.
key_risks: Risk of becoming a purely symbolic body
Potential for political patronage in council appointments
Lack of statutory power to enforce recommendations

points.text: Micro tech parks will be set up in 25 towns including Pollachi, Karur, Kovilpatti, Krishnagiri, Tiruvannamalai, Vellore, Erode, Dindigul, Namakkal and Sivakasi. Each micro tech park will be equipped with infrastructure such as high-speed internet, modern meeting halls and co-working space. These micro tech parks will create opportunities for youth from those towns to work globally while working from their hometowns.
points.title: 11 Micro Tech Parks

evidence.title: CM MK Stalin Launches Neo Tidel Parks in Tamil Nadu to Boost IT Jobs
evidence.snippet: Tamil Nadu Chief Minister MK Stalin inaugurated two Neo Tidel parks in Thanjavur and Salem... part of the govt's effort to take IT jobs to tier-II and tier-III cities.

evidence.title: TIDEL Neo Rasipuram (Namakkal) – Foundation Stone Laid
evidence.snippet: On 5th November 2025, the Foundation Stone for TIDEL Neo Rasipuram (Namakkal) was laid... project cost of Rs. 45.5 Crores.

plan_existence.notes: The Tamil Nadu government is already implementing 'TIDEL Neo' (Mini IT Parks) in several tier-2 and tier-3 towns including Salem, Thanjavur, Vellore, and Namakkal. The promise to expand this to 25 towns including Karur and Pollachi is a direct continuation and scaling of this existing policy.

fiscal.comments: Current projects cost approximately ₹30-45 crore per park. Expanding to 25 towns would require an outlay of roughly ₹1,000 crore, which is manageable for the state's capital budget.

legal.comments: Standard industrial infrastructure development; no legal hurdles.

administrative.comments: TIDEL and ELCOT have established mechanisms for executing these projects.

timeline.comments: Construction of mini IT parks typically takes 18-24 months; 25 parks can be completed in a 5-year term.

political.comments: Highly popular as it addresses the 'brain drain' from smaller towns to Chennai/Bangalore.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a highly feasible and grounded promise that builds on a successful existing model. It directly addresses regional development and employment needs.
key_risks: Occupancy risk in smaller towns
Competition from established IT hubs like Bangalore and Coimbatore
Maintenance and high-speed connectivity reliability in remote towns

points.subsection_title: B. Tamil Nadu Rising in Sports
points.text: A Tamil Nadu Sports University will be set up with high-quality sports training, sports medicine, sports technology, and excellent administrative infrastructure so that Tamil Nadu athletes can win Olympic medals. Sports Excellence Centres will be established to provide training equivalent to that given to athletes in developed countries.
points.title: 1 Tamil Nadu Sports University

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: B. Tamil Nadu Rising in Sports

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu Physical Education and Sports University (TNPESU)

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Physical Education and Sports University - Wikipedia
evidence.snippet: Tamil Nadu Physical Education and Sports University (TNPESU) is a state-run university in Chennai, established in 2005. It is the first public sports university established in India.

plan_existence.notes: A dedicated state sports university (TNPESU) has existed in Chennai since 2005. The promise to 'set up' a university is redundant unless it refers to a second campus or a complete overhaul/rebranding of the existing institution.

legal.comments: Setting up a second state university for the same purpose may face scrutiny regarding duplication of resources.

administrative.comments: The state already has the administrative framework for sports education through TNPESU and SDAT.

timeline.comments: A new university or major campus expansion can be completed within 3-4 years.

political.comments: Sports infrastructure is generally well-received, especially with the focus on Olympic medals.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is largely redundant as the institution already exists. It lacks clarity on whether this is a new entity or an upgrade of the 20-year-old TNPESU.
key_risks: Redundancy with existing TNPESU
High capital expenditure for duplicate infrastructure
Difficulty in attracting world-class coaching staff to a new state institution

points.subsection_title: B. Tamil Nadu Rising in Sports
points.text: The Tamil Nadu Olympic Skill Development Scheme will be introduced to make Tamil Nadu the sports capital of India. Through this, talented athletes across Tamil Nadu will be identified and given international-standard training to enable them to win at the Olympics. Individual athletes from Tamil Nadu who qualify for the Olympics will be given Rs. 25 lakh as a grant, and team athletes will be given Rs. 10 lakh. Of these, Tamil Nadu athletes who win gold at the Olympics will be given Rs. 6 crore, silver medallists Rs. 4 crore, and bronze medallists Rs. 3 crore as cash prizes. Similarly, 500 athletes who excel at national and international sports competitions will be given financial assistance.
points.title: 2 Tamil Nadu Vettri Olympic Scheme

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: B. Tamil Nadu Rising in Sports

evidence.title: Rs 3 crore for Tamil Nadu athletes who win Olympic gold
evidence.snippet: Chief minister M K Stalin announced a cash reward of Rs 3 crore for sportspersons from Tamil Nadu who win gold medal, Rs 2 crore for silver and Rs 1 crore for bronze.

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu emerges as a Sports Hub
evidence.snippet: The Dravidian Model Government launched the 'Olympic Gold Hunt' scheme with a goal to transform Tamil Nadu's athletes into world-class competitors.

plan_existence.notes: The state already provides cash prizes (3cr/2cr/1cr) and training support through the Olympic Gold Hunt. This promise doubles the prize money and formalizes a qualification grant (25L/10L).

fiscal.comments: Doubling the gold medal prize to 6cr is a high per-capita cost, but given the historically low number of Olympic medalists (13 Olympians total in 2024), the total fiscal impact is manageable within the sports budget.

legal.comments: Sports is a State List subject (Entry 33), giving the state full authority to award grants and prizes.

administrative.comments: The Sports Development Authority of Tamil Nadu (SDAT) already has the mechanism to identify and disburse funds to elite athletes.

timeline.comments: Cash prizes and grants can be implemented immediately upon qualification or victory.

political.comments: High public support for rewarding sporting excellence; aligns with the state's 'Sports Capital' ambition.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible as it builds on an existing, successful framework. The primary change is a significant increase in the quantum of financial rewards.
key_risks: Budgetary volatility if medal counts increase significantly
Ensuring transparency in 'international-standard' training procurement

points.text: World-class multi-sport stadiums, sports complexes, gymkhanas, sports academies and training centres will be set up in the 10 zonal capitals of Tamil Nadu.

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: B. Tamil Nadu Rising in Sports

evidence.title: Olympic academies to be set up in Chennai, Madurai, Tiruchi and the Nilgiris
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu government will establish four Olympic Academies in Chennai, Madurai, Tiruchi and the Nilgiris to provide world-class training.

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Launches Olympic Training Academy in Trichy With ₹150 Crore Investment
evidence.snippet: The project will be executed in two phases, featuring world-class sports facilities including a swimming pool and athletics track.

plan_existence.notes: The state has already begun setting up Olympic Academies in 4 zones. This promise expands the scope to 10 zonal capitals.

fiscal.comments: A single academy in Trichy costs 150cr. Scaling this to 10 zonal capitals would require an investment of approximately 1,500cr, which is a significant portion of the state's capital expenditure.

legal.comments: No legal hurdles; land acquisition for sports infrastructure is a standard state function.

administrative.comments: SDAT has experience in managing large-scale infrastructure projects, though concurrent construction in 10 cities will test capacity.

timeline.comments: Large sports complexes typically take 3-5 years to complete. Delivering 10 world-class facilities within one term is ambitious.

political.comments: Strong political will exists to decentralize sports infrastructure from Chennai to Tier-II cities.

feasibility.overall_comments: Feasible but fiscally demanding. The state has already demonstrated the model in Trichy and Chennai, making the expansion a logical next step.
key_risks: Cost overruns
Land acquisition delays in urban zonal capitals
Maintenance and utilization rates post-construction

points.text: 500 athletes with interest and talent in sports will be identified and selected from schools. Athletes between 8 and 14 years of age thus selected will be given world-class training sufficient to win Olympic medals.

plan_existence.notes: Existing schemes target the 10-14 age group. This promise lowers the starting age to 8 and sets a specific target of 500 athletes for 'world-class' training.

fiscal.comments: Training 500 athletes is well within the current SDAT budget of ~485cr.

legal.comments: No legal constraints; falls under state educational and sports mandates.

administrative.comments: The school-based scouting mechanism is already in place through the CM Trophy and physical education departments.

timeline.comments: Identification and induction can be completed within the first year of the term.

political.comments: Focusing on children ('Catch Them Young' philosophy) is politically popular and aligns with national sports goals.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible. It refines existing talent-spotting programs with a more specific age bracket and beneficiary target.
key_risks: Ensuring scientific age-appropriate training for 8-year-olds
Long-term retention of talent in the sports pipeline

plan_existence.notes: Infrastructure for para-sports is already being developed in Trichy and planned for Madurai/Salem. The promise formalizes these as 'world-class training centres'.

points.text: (Surfing, Kayaking, Wind-Surfing, Snorkelling, Scuba Diving) 10 water sports centres will be set up. Priority will be given to young coastal fishermen to train at these sports centres.
points.title: 6 Water Sports

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: B. Tamil Nadu Rising in Sports

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu International Surf Open
evidence.snippet: The Sports Development Authority of Tamil Nadu (SDAT) has been actively promoting surfing and water sports, including hosting international competitions in Mahabalipuram.

plan_existence.notes: While the state promotes water sports through SDAT and events like the Mahabalipuram Surf Festival, a dedicated network of 10 integrated centers with a specific quota for coastal youth is an expansion of current ad-hoc initiatives.

fiscal.comments: Setting up 10 centers is a moderate capital expenditure well within the sports budget; recurring costs for training are manageable.

legal.comments: No legal hurdles; falls under state subjects (Sports and Fisheries).

administrative.comments: SDAT has experience in infrastructure; identifying coastal youth via Fisheries Department is straightforward.

timeline.comments: Infrastructure can be completed within 2-3 years; training programs can start immediately.

political.comments: High appeal in coastal constituencies; promotes tourism and local employment.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible and aligns with the state's 'Blue Economy' goals. The focus on coastal youth adds a strong social inclusion element.

Maintenance of high-end equipment

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.text: Crop loans taken from cooperative banks by farmers owning less than 5 acres will be fully waived. Fifty percent of crop loans taken from cooperative banks by farmers owning more than 5 acres will be waived.
points.title: 1 Agricultural Loan Waiver

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

evidence.title: TN Govt issues orders for Rs 12,110 cr crop loan waiver
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu government in 2021 waived Rs 12,110.74 crore of crop loans availed by 16.43 lakh farmers from cooperative banks.

plan_existence.notes: Loan waivers are a recurring policy in Tamil Nadu. The 2021 waiver covered all crop loans; this proposal introduces a tiered waiver (100% vs 50%) based on land size.

fiscal.comments: Previous waivers cost over Rs 12,000 crore. A tiered waiver for all farmers will significantly strain the state exchequer and increase the fiscal deficit.

legal.comments: State government has full authority over cooperative banks to implement waivers.

administrative.comments: Cooperative bank records are digitized; implementation is fast and efficient.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented via a single government order within months.

political.comments: Extremely popular among the rural electorate; standard electoral strategy in TN.

feasibility.overall_comments: Administratively and politically easy, but fiscally taxing. It follows a well-established precedent in the state.
key_risks: Fiscal deficit increase
Moral hazard for future repayments
Reduced lending capacity of cooperative banks

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.text: Every farmer family that owns agricultural land will be given Rs. 15,000 per year in two instalments (July-August season, January-February season) as seed-season financial aid. This fund will be a support for meeting the expenses of the sowing season.
points.title: 2 Vettri Farmer Entitlement

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

evidence.title: PM-Kisan Samman Nidhi
evidence.snippet: Central scheme providing Rs 6,000 per year in three installments to all landholding farmer families.

plan_existence.notes: This is a state-level top-up/alternative that is significantly higher (Rs 15,000) than the Central PM-KISAN (Rs 6,000). It mirrors schemes like Rythu Bandhu (Telangana) but at a higher fixed rate.

fiscal.comments: With ~7.9 million operational holdings in TN, a Rs 15,000 grant would cost approx. Rs 11,850 crore annually, a massive recurring expenditure.

legal.comments: No legal barriers to providing state-funded grants.

administrative.comments: Can leverage PM-KISAN database and DBT infrastructure for delivery.

timeline.comments: Can be rolled out in the first budget cycle.

political.comments: High impact 'cash-in-hand' promise likely to garner significant rural support.

feasibility.overall_comments: A high-stakes welfare promise that is administratively simple but fiscally very aggressive.
key_risks: Extreme fiscal pressure
Inflationary pressure on agricultural inputs
Exclusion of landless labourers (addressed in P4)

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.text: A scheme to issue a Farmer Identity Card to tenant farmers and agricultural labourers will be introduced. Farmers holding this identity card will be directly credited with Rs. 10,000 per year into their bank accounts as a grant.
points.title: 3 Uzhavar Thozhar Scheme

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Agricultural Labourers-Farmers Social Security and Welfare Scheme 2006
evidence.snippet: Provides social security/insurance but does not provide a direct annual cash grant of Rs 10,000 specifically for tenant farmers via an ID card system.

plan_existence.notes: While social security boards exist, a direct income support scheme specifically for tenant farmers and landless labourers is a major new policy shift, as they are usually excluded from land-ownership based schemes like PM-KISAN.

fiscal.comments: Agricultural labourers form a massive part of the rural workforce. If 5 million qualify, the cost is Rs 5,000 crore annually.

legal.comments: Requires a framework to legally recognize 'tenant' status without formal land registration, which can be complex.

administrative.comments: Identifying tenant farmers is notoriously difficult due to oral lease agreements and lack of documentation. High risk of leakage or exclusion.

timeline.comments: Issuing ID cards based on verification will take at least 1-2 years.

political.comments: Strongly pro-poor and addresses a long-standing gap in agricultural welfare.

feasibility.overall_comments: Socially progressive but administratively challenging. Success depends entirely on the integrity of the ID card issuance process.
key_risks: Identification fraud
Landowner resistance to formalizing tenancies
High administrative overhead for verification

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.text: Rs. 3,500 per quintal will be given as minimum support price for paddy, and Rs. 450 per tonne for sugarcane. When the market prices of turmeric, sugarcane, paddy and copra fall, the government will also bear that loss. All these funds, including the support price amount and market loss compensation, will be credited directly into farmers' bank accounts.
points.title: 4 Guaranteeing Minimum Support Price for Paddy and Sugarcane

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 4 Guaranteeing Minimum Support Price for Paddy and Sugarcane
Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

existing_scheme_names: Fair and Remunerative Price (FRP) for Sugarcane

evidence.title: Paddy MSP 2024-25
evidence.snippet: The Union Cabinet has increased the MSP for paddy (Common) to Rs 2,300 per quintal for the 2024-25 season.

evidence.title: TN Paddy Incentive
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu government provides an additional incentive of Rs 105 per quintal for Grade A paddy, bringing the total to approximately Rs 2,405.

plan_existence.notes: The paddy MSP promise of Rs 3,500 is a ~45% increase over the current total (Central MSP + State Incentive) of ~Rs 2,405. The sugarcane price of Rs 450 likely refers to an increased state incentive, as the current total price (FRP + Incentive) is already over Rs 3,600.

fiscal.comments: Paddy procurement in TN is ~4.5 million tonnes. A hike of ~Rs 1,100/quintal (Rs 11,000/tonne) would cost ~Rs 4,950 crore annually. Sugarcane incentive hike to Rs 450/tonne (from Rs 215) for ~15 million tonnes costs ~Rs 350 crore.

legal.comments: MSP is primarily a Central subject. States can only provide top-up incentives. A 'State MSP' of 3500 might face regulatory hurdles if not framed as an incentive.

administrative.comments: TNCSC already has a robust procurement and DBT system in place.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented in the first budget cycle of the new government.

political.comments: Highly popular among the large farming vote bank; low political risk.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly popular but fiscally demanding. The paddy MSP hike would cost ~Rs 5,000 crore annually. Legal implementation as a state-specific MSP may require careful structuring as an incentive to avoid conflict with Central norms.
key_risks: Fiscal strain on state budget
Conflict with Central procurement rules
Inflationary impact on rice prices

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.text: A Farmers' Income Enhancement Commission will be established to identify advice on increasing farmers' income, simplified marketing methods and pricing guidance. It will make annual recommendations to the government on these matters.
points.title: 5 Farmers' Income Enhancement Commission

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

evidence.title: TN Agriculture Budget 2024-25
evidence.snippet: The state has a State Agriculture Development Council, but no dedicated Income Enhancement Commission with an annual mandate.

plan_existence.notes: No dedicated 'Farmers Income Enhancement Commission' currently exists in Tamil Nadu, though the State Agriculture Development Council performs some advisory roles.

fiscal.comments: Setting up a commission involves minimal administrative costs relative to the state budget.

legal.comments: State government has full authority to establish advisory commissions via executive order.

administrative.comments: Easily implementable through existing bureaucratic structures.

timeline.comments: Can be established within 6 months.

political.comments: Low risk; signals a strong pro-farmer stance to the electorate.

feasibility.overall_comments: A low-cost, high-impact governance move. It provides a formal platform for farmer grievances and policy advice, though its effectiveness depends on whether its recommendations are binding.
key_risks: Recommendations may remain non-binding
Bureaucratic inertia in acting on advice

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.text: To benefit farmers, additional agro-meteorological centres will be established across Tamil Nadu. Through this, crop losses during the rainy seasons will be accurately calculated. Based on this, compensation will be quickly given to farmers. 100 percent of the cultivated land will be brought under crop insurance.
points.title: 6 100 Percent Crop Insurance

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

evidence.title: TN Agriculture Budget 2024-25
evidence.snippet: The state government allocates funds for PMFBY and focuses on expanding weather station networks for better loss assessment.

plan_existence.notes: Tamil Nadu already implements PMFBY with high coverage, but 100% coverage and expanded agro-met centres represent a significant scale-up of existing efforts.

fiscal.comments: TN already spends ~Rs 2,300 crore on crop insurance premiums. 100% coverage would likely increase this by 20-30%, requiring substantial budget allocation.

legal.comments: Aligns with existing national insurance frameworks; no legal barriers.

administrative.comments: 100% coverage is difficult due to land record gaps and the high number of tenant farmers who lack formal documentation.

timeline.comments: Full coverage and station network expansion would take 2-3 years to achieve.

political.comments: Strong public support for disaster protection given the state's vulnerability to cyclones and droughts.

feasibility.overall_comments: Technically feasible but administratively challenging. 100% coverage is difficult due to land record gaps. Expanding weather stations is a sound move for accurate loss assessment.
key_risks: High premium costs
Difficulty in verifying 100% land coverage
Reliance on Central PMFBY funding

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.text: To ensure that changes in coconut prices do not affect coconut farmers, a copra support fund will be allocated. Through the Coconut Producers' Cooperative Society, copra will be procured at places such as Coimbatore, Thanjavur, Pollachi and Kangeyam. Oil will be extracted from these at government mills and sold through the public distribution system. The Union Government will be strongly urged to introduce Minimum Support Price (MSP) for fresh coconut and, at the same time, to raise the support price for copra (both milling and ball varieties) to protect farmers from price fluctuations and ensure them profitable income. Further, a 'Tamil Nadu Coconut Processing Corporation' will be created to develop value-added products such as neera and coconut oil to increase farmers' income.
points.title: 7 Welfare of Copra Farmers

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

evidence.title: Centre increases minimum support price for copra
evidence.snippet: The Union Government increased MSP for milling copra and ball copra for the 2026 season to ₹12,027 and ₹12,500 per quintal respectively.

evidence.title: Coconut oil in ration shops: Tamil Nadu govt starts survey
evidence.snippet: The state civil supplies department has started a survey to find out if ration cardholders would be ready to buy coconut oil in place of palmolein oil.

plan_existence.notes: While copra procurement under the Price Support Scheme (PSS) already exists with Central MSP, the promise to distribute coconut oil through the PDS is currently only a proposal under survey. A dedicated 'Tamil Nadu Coconut Processing Corporation' and a specific 'copra support fund' are new institutional proposals not currently in place.

legal.comments: MSP for fresh coconut and revisions to copra MSP are under the jurisdiction of the Central Government (CACP); the state can only 'urge' or provide a state-funded bonus. Creating a state corporation is within state powers.

administrative.comments: Tamil Nadu has a robust network of Agricultural Producers' Cooperative Societies and regulated markets in the mentioned hubs (Pollachi, Kangeyam) to facilitate procurement and processing.

timeline.comments: Setting up a state corporation and integrating coconut oil into the PDS supply chain is feasible within a 5-year term, given existing infrastructure.

political.comments: Extremely high demand from the influential coconut-farming belts in Western and Delta Tamil Nadu; addresses long-standing grievances regarding price volatility.

feasibility.overall_comments: The plan is highly relevant to the state's agricultural economy and leverages existing cooperative strengths. While the MSP demands depend on the Union Government, the state-level interventions like the Processing Corporation and PDS integration are administratively viable and politically popular.
key_risks: Dependence on Union Government for MSP revisions
Logistical challenges in large-scale oil extraction and PDS shelf-life management
Market competition with cheaper imported palmolein oil

search_queries: MSP for fresh coconut vs copra India current status
Tamil Nadu government coconut oil PDS distribution status
Coconut Producers' Cooperative Society Tamil Nadu procurement centers
Tamil Nadu copra support fund existing
Tamil Nadu Coconut Processing Corporation existing or proposed

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.text: The following steps will be taken to make Tamil Nadu a leading state in organic farming. Farmers switching from synthetic-fertiliser farming to organic farming will be given a monthly grant of Rs. 2,000 for the first three years. A new brand called 'Tamil Iyal', based on geographical indication, will be created to market organically grown traditional rice varieties, spices, coconuts, turmeric and bananas. A Nammalvar College of Organic Agriculture will be established in Thanjavur.
points.title: 8 Tamil Nadu Organic Farming Scheme

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Organic Farming Policy 2023
evidence.snippet: The policy aims to convert Tamil Nadu into a chemical-free organic farming state by providing incentives for certification and inputs.

plan_existence.notes: While a policy exists, the specific monthly grant of Rs. 2,000 for three years and the 'Tamil Iyal' brand are new initiatives. There is a private Nammalvar College of Agriculture in Ramanathapuram, but not a government one in Thanjavur.

legal.comments: Agriculture is a state subject under the Seventh Schedule of the Constitution.

administrative.comments: Establishing a new brand and a specialized college requires significant inter-departmental coordination and long-term management.

timeline.comments: The grant can be rolled out quickly; the college and branding will take 3-5 years to fully materialize.

political.comments: Organic farming is highly popular among the public and environmental groups in Tamil Nadu.

feasibility.overall_comments: A well-rounded proposal that addresses financial transition, branding, and education, though administrative execution of a state-run brand is historically challenging.
key_risks: Certification delays for organic status
Market competition for the 'Tamil Iyal' brand
Sustainability of organic practices after the 3-year grant period

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.text: Farmers joining Farmer Producer Organisations will be given an incentive of Rs. 5,000.
points.title: 9 Farmer Producer Organisation

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

existing_scheme_names: Formation and Promotion of 10,000 Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs)

evidence.title: TNSFAC - Farmer Producer Organizations
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu Small Farmers Agribusiness Consortium supports FPOs through equity grants and credit guarantees.

plan_existence.notes: Current schemes provide financial support to the FPO entity (equity grants up to Rs. 15 lakh), but there is no existing scheme that pays a direct cash incentive of Rs. 5,000 to an individual farmer for joining.

legal.comments: No legal barriers to providing cash incentives for cooperative participation.

administrative.comments: Can be easily implemented via Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) using existing FPO registration databases.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented within the first year of the term.

political.comments: FPOs are a key focus of both central and state governments; this would be seen as a complementary welfare measure.

feasibility.overall_comments: A straightforward incentive to boost FPO membership, which is currently lower than desired in many districts.
key_risks: Ghost memberships created solely to claim the incentive
One-time incentive may not ensure long-term active participation in the FPO

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.text: Children of farmers holding less than two acres of agricultural land will be given free higher education.
points.title: 10 Free Higher Education for Farmers' Children

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 10 Free Higher Education for Farmers' Children
Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

evidence.title: Chief Minister's Uzhavar Pathukappu Thittam - Education Assistance
evidence.snippet: Provides educational assistance to children of registered farmers ranging from Rs. 2,250 to Rs. 50,000 per year depending on the course.

plan_existence.notes: The existing UPT provides fixed scholarships. The promise of 'free higher education' implies a full tuition waiver or 100% coverage, which would be a significant expansion of the current assistance levels.

legal.comments: State has the authority to provide full fee waivers in state-run and aided institutions.

administrative.comments: Verification of land ownership (< 2 acres) can be integrated with the Patta/Chitta database and the UPT registration system.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented from the start of the next academic year.

political.comments: Highly popular measure for the rural agrarian vote bank.

feasibility.overall_comments: A feasible expansion of an existing welfare framework, though the definition of 'free' needs to be clarified regarding private vs. government colleges.
key_risks: High budgetary burden if private college fees are reimbursed
Potential for fraudulent land division to meet the < 2-acre criteria

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.text: Paddy and other procured produce including vegetables, food grains and spices will be properly handled and safeguarded at all procurement centres, cluster hubs and silos. Through this, post-harvest losses will be reduced. Accurate weighing and storage data will be properly maintained. Similarly, it will be ensured that not a single paisa of commission is charged at procurement centres for procurement or for loading and unloading sacks. Whoever charges such commission will face strict action.
points.title: Y Reducing Produce Wastage at Procurement Centres

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: Y Reducing Produce Wastage at Procurement Centres
Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu Civil Supplies Corporation (TNCSC) Procurement

evidence.title: TNCSC - Procurement Operations
evidence.snippet: TNCSC procures paddy from farmers through Direct Purchase Centres (DPCs) and ensures storage in godowns and silos.

plan_existence.notes: While procurement and storage infrastructure exist, the promise focuses on eliminating illegal commissions and improving weighing accuracy, which are persistent operational grievances in the current system.

legal.comments: Charging commissions at DPCs is already illegal; stricter enforcement is a matter of executive action.

administrative.comments: Eliminating entrenched local corruption and 'bagging charges' at thousands of DPCs is a massive administrative challenge.

timeline.comments: Infrastructure improvements (silos) take years; behavioral change in the procurement staff is a continuous process.

political.comments: Strongly supported by farmers, but may face quiet resistance from local political intermediaries who benefit from the current system.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise addresses a critical pain point for farmers, but its success depends on enforcement rather than just policy announcement.
key_risks: Resistance from powerful local middlemen and staff unions
High capital expenditure required for modernizing all storage hubs into silos
Difficulty in monitoring thousands of decentralized procurement points

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.text: The fishing ban-period relief amount will be raised to Rs. 20,000. Fishermen engaged in traditional fishing will be given Rs. 7,000 as northeast monsoon relief.
points.title: 1 Fishermen Safety Entitlement Amount

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

evidence.title: Fishing ban cash aid distribution begins in Tamil Nadu
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu Fisheries Department has begun depositing ₹8,000 into bank accounts of 1.75 lakh fishermen in the State as ban period assistance.

plan_existence.notes: The current relief of Rs 8,000 was recently increased from Rs 5,000 in the 2024-25 budget. The proposed Rs 20,000 represents a 150% increase, targeting approximately 1.75 lakh registered fishermen.

fiscal.comments: Increasing ban relief from 8k to 20k for 1.75L fishermen adds ~Rs 210cr annually. Monsoon relief hike adds further cost.

legal.comments: No legal barriers; executive decision.

administrative.comments: Uses existing DBT and Fishermen Welfare Board databases.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented in the first budget cycle.

political.comments: High public reception among coastal communities.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible as it expands an existing DBT mechanism. The primary challenge is the significant fiscal outlay required to more than double the current relief amounts.
key_risks: Budgetary pressure from 150% hike
Inflationary impact on relief value
Verification of active traditional fishermen

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.text: Every registered fisherman will be given accident insurance worth Rs. 25 lakh. Further, to assist fishermen struggling for their lives in accidents at sea, satellite-technology-equipped sea ambulances will be set up at every major fishing harbour. Tow-boat facilities will also be put in place to rescue disabled boats at sea.
points.title: 2 Fishermen's Accident Insurance

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

existing_scheme_names: Group Accident Insurance Scheme for Fishermen

evidence.title: PMMSY Insurance Coverage
evidence.snippet: PMMSY supports insurance coverage for fishers which includes Rs. 5,00,000 against accidental death or permanent total disability.

plan_existence.notes: While insurance exists, the coverage is currently capped at Rs 5 lakh under PMMSY. Sea ambulances are currently a pilot project in Kasimedu; scaling to all major harbours is a significant infrastructure shift.

fiscal.comments: Insurance premium costs are manageable, but sea ambulance procurement and maintenance are high-capex items.

legal.comments: No legal barriers; insurance is contractual.

administrative.comments: Universal sea ambulance coverage requires specialized vessels and 24/7 medical staffing, which is currently lacking.

timeline.comments: Procuring and staffing sea ambulances for all harbours will take 3-5 years.

political.comments: Strong support from fishing unions.

feasibility.overall_comments: Insurance expansion is straightforward, but the sea ambulance and tow-boat components are infrastructure-heavy and lack a clear delivery roadmap.
key_risks: High maintenance cost of sea ambulances
Staffing specialized medical personnel at sea
Insurance claim settlement delays

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.text: The diesel subsidy for fishing boats will be raised from 1,800 litres to 3,600 litres. An additional subsidy of Rs. 15 per litre will be directly credited to the boat owner's bank account.
points.title: 3 Fishermen Fuel Security

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

existing_scheme_names: Sales Tax Exempted Diesel for Fishing Boats

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu CM Stalin announces hike in fishing ban assistance
evidence.snippet: The subsidised diesel will be increased from 18,000 litre to 19,000 litres for mechanized crafts.

plan_existence.notes: Current annual limits are approx 19,000 liters for mechanized boats. The promise to double this and add a Rs 15/L DBT subsidy would create a massive fiscal liability.

fiscal.comments: Doubling the diesel quota and adding a Rs 15/L subsidy would significantly increase the state's fuel subsidy bill.

legal.comments: No legal barriers; subsidy adjustment.

administrative.comments: Can be delivered via existing diesel card system.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented immediately via existing diesel cards.

political.comments: Extremely popular among boat owners.

feasibility.overall_comments: Fiscally aggressive; doubling the diesel quota and adding a per-liter subsidy creates a heavy recurring burden, though administrative delivery via existing diesel cards is simple.
key_risks: Fiscal deficit increase
Potential for fuel diversion/black marketing
Environmental concerns of increased diesel usage

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.text: For the first time in India, Minimum Support Prices will be fixed for prawns, sardines, mackerel and squid. To prevent fishermen from being affected by price crashes when these fish arrive in large quantities, the shortfall amount will be directly credited to their bank accounts.
points.title: 4 Minimum Support Price for Fish

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

evidence.title: Is MSP a Viable Proposition in Marine Fisheries? - EPW
evidence.snippet: The Kerala government introduced a minimum support price for fisherfolk's catch in April 2018, among the first of its kind in the country.

evidence.title: Himachal Pradesh sets MSP for reservoir fish
evidence.snippet: Himachal Pradesh government has fixed MSP of ₹100 per kg for reservoir fish with DBT for the shortfall.

plan_existence.notes: While Kerala (2018) and Himachal Pradesh (2026) have introduced versions of price support for fish, no such statutory MSP exists in Tamil Nadu. Current TN support is limited to fishing ban relief (₹8,000) and fuel subsidies.

fiscal.comments: Implementing MSP for highly perishable marine products is fiscally volatile. Unlike grains, the state cannot easily store fish to recover costs later. Kerala's 2018 attempt faced significant funding and implementation hurdles.

legal.comments: Fisheries is a State Subject (Entry 21, List II of the Seventh Schedule), giving the Tamil Nadu government full legal authority to enact price support legislation.

administrative.comments: Extremely difficult to monitor real-time auction prices across hundreds of dispersed landing centers. Verifying individual catch volumes for DBT to prevent fraud requires a massive digital and physical oversight infrastructure.

timeline.comments: A pilot project could be launched within 2 years, but a statewide statutory rollout for multiple species would likely take the full 5-year term.

political.comments: Highly popular promise among the influential coastal voting bloc (approx. 14 districts). It addresses a long-standing demand for parity with crop farmers.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is legally sound but administratively a nightmare. The lack of cold storage infrastructure and the perishability of fish make a 'shortfall' payment system prone to leakage and fiscal overruns. It mimics the Kerala model but aims for a more formal statutory status.
key_risks: High fiscal burden during peak harvest seasons
Difficulty in accurate price discovery at local landing centers
Potential for middleman exploitation of the DBT system
Lack of adequate cold chain to manage surplus catch

search_queries: Tamil Nadu government price support for fishermen
MSP for prawns sardines mackerel squid India
Kerala MSP for fish scheme details
Minimum Support Price for fish India Tamil Nadu existing schemes

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.text: To ensure that coasts are free of huts, a coastal housing scheme will be created. Under this scheme, houses will be built to a standard capable of withstanding cyclones, coastal erosion and bad weather. Priority will be given to fishing communities in Nagapattinam, Cuddalore, Ramanathapuram, Tuticorin and Kanyakumari districts — which are most vulnerable to cyclones.
points.title: 5 Sea Mother Home

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Budget 2024-25: Kalaignar Kanavu Illam
evidence.snippet: The government announced the 'Kalaignar Kanavu Illam' scheme to make Tamil Nadu hut-free by 2030, with an initial outlay for 1 lakh houses.

evidence.title: Fisheries and Fishermen Welfare Department - Housing Scheme
evidence.snippet: The department provides housing units for fishermen through various state and centrally sponsored schemes like PMAY.

plan_existence.notes: Tamil Nadu already has a 'Hut-free' mission (Kalaignar Kanavu Illam) and specific housing schemes for fishermen. This promise focuses on cyclone-resistant standards in specific vulnerable districts, which represents a specialized expansion of existing housing mandates.

fiscal.comments: The state has already committed to a hut-free TN by 2030. However, the cost of 'cyclone-resistant' housing is significantly higher than standard PMAY units (approx 3.5-5 lakh per unit vs 1.7-2 lakh). No specific budget is mentioned in the promise.

legal.comments: Housing is a state subject; no legal impediments exist for building specialized housing for specific communities.

administrative.comments: The Tamil Nadu Housing Board and the Fisheries Department have existing mechanisms for identification and delivery. Land acquisition in CRZ (Coastal Regulation Zone) areas remains the primary bottleneck.

timeline.comments: Construction of 1-2 lakh houses is feasible within a 5-year term if funding is prioritized.

political.comments: High political appeal in coastal belts; addresses a long-standing demand of the influential fishing community.

feasibility.overall_comments: The plan is highly feasible as it builds on existing state missions for a 'Hut-free Tamil Nadu'. The specific focus on cyclone-resilience in vulnerable districts is a logical policy evolution, though it requires higher per-unit fiscal allocation than current schemes.
key_risks: CRZ (Coastal Regulation Zone) clearance delays
High per-unit cost for cyclone-resistant specifications
Land availability in densely populated coastal hamlets

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.text: Under the rules currently in force, when a fisherman goes missing at sea, he is officially declared deceased only after 7 years. Only after that is compensation given to his family. Changing this, if a fisherman goes missing, appropriate relief will be given to his family within the next six months.
points.title: 6 Welfare of Missing Fishermen's Families

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

existing_scheme_names: Relief to the families of missing fishermen

evidence.title: TN Fisheries Dept - Relief for Missing Fishermen
evidence.snippet: Currently, daily allowances are provided to families of missing fishermen, but full compensation often follows the 7-year legal presumption of death under the Evidence Act.

plan_existence.notes: The 7-year rule is based on Section 108 of the Indian Evidence Act. While the TN government provides immediate daily relief (approx Rs. 250/day) and some ex-gratia, the official declaration of death for full settlement usually adheres to the 7-year legal standard.

fiscal.comments: The number of missing fishermen annually is relatively low (dozens, not thousands). Providing full compensation (typically 5-10 lakhs) within 6 months is fiscally negligible for the state budget.

legal.comments: The 7-year period is a statutory presumption under Section 108 of the Indian Evidence Act (a Central Act). A state cannot unilaterally change the legal definition of 'presumed dead'. However, the state can provide 'relief' or 'ex-gratia' equivalent to compensation without a legal death certificate.

administrative.comments: Requires a robust verification process by the Coast Guard and local police to prevent fraud, but otherwise simple to implement via the Fisheries Department.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented immediately via a Government Order (GO) for ex-gratia payments.

political.comments: Extremely popular among fishing communities who face immense hardship when the breadwinner goes missing.

feasibility.overall_comments: While the state cannot legally declare a person 'dead' in 6 months due to Central laws, it can certainly provide equivalent financial relief. The promise is highly feasible as an administrative ex-gratia measure rather than a legal amendment.
key_risks: Conflict with Central laws (Indian Evidence Act)
Potential for fraudulent claims without a 7-year verification window
Difficulty in recovering funds if the 'missing' person returns

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.text: Young fishermen who wish to become entrepreneurs in value-added marine trades such as boat-repair workshops, seafood-export enterprises, fish-processing businesses and seaweed production will be given an investment grant of Rs. 5 lakh.
points.title: 7 Fishermen Entrepreneur Welfare Scheme

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

evidence.title: PMMSY Guidelines
evidence.snippet: PMMSY provides 40% to 60% subsidies for various fisheries projects including processing and value addition, but not necessarily a flat 5 lakh grant for all youth.

plan_existence.notes: While PMMSY and state MSME schemes (like NEEDS) provide credit-linked subsidies for marine businesses, a direct 'investment grant' of Rs. 5 lakh specifically for young fishermen entrepreneurs is a more targeted, simplified version of existing complex subsidy models.

fiscal.comments: If 1,000 youth are targeted annually, the cost is Rs. 50 crore. This is well within the capacity of the TN Fisheries and MSME budgets.

legal.comments: No legal barriers to providing grants for entrepreneurship.

administrative.comments: Requires a rigorous selection process to ensure the grant is used for the intended business and not as a cash transfer. Monitoring 'seaweed production' or 'boat repair' startups requires technical expertise.

timeline.comments: Can be rolled out within the first year of a new government.

political.comments: Strongly aligns with the 'youth empowerment' and 'blue economy' narratives.

feasibility.overall_comments: A very feasible and targeted scheme. It simplifies the often cumbersome process of getting subsidies from PMMSY by offering a flat grant, which could significantly boost the local marine value chain.
key_risks: Misuse of funds as personal consumption rather than business investment
High failure rate of small-scale marine startups
Duplication with existing PMMSY subsidies

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.text: Fishermen who fish in rivers, tanks, ponds and reservoirs — other than the sea — currently do not receive any of the benefits of the Tamil Nadu Fishermen's Welfare Board. Changing this, all the benefits of the Tamil Nadu Fishermen's Welfare Board will be made available to all fishermen who fish for a living. Eligible prawn farmers will also benefit under this scheme. Further, a prawn disease insurance will be provided to benefit prawn farmers facing losses due to unexpected disease outbreaks.
points.title: 8 Welfare of Inland Fishermen

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

existing_scheme_names: Group Accident Insurance Scheme for Fishermen

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Fishermen Welfare Board - Membership
evidence.snippet: The board currently covers both marine and inland fishermen, but benefits like lean period allowances are often restricted to marine fishermen.

plan_existence.notes: Inland fishermen are technically eligible for the Welfare Board, but there is a disparity in the 'benefits' received compared to marine fishermen (e.g., the ban period relief). Prawn disease insurance is currently not a standard state-offered product in TN.

fiscal.comments: Expanding 'all benefits' (like the Rs. 5000-8000 lean period relief) to inland fishermen would be expensive, as the inland population is large. Prawn insurance would require state-subsidized premiums, the cost of which is unknown without actuarial data.

legal.comments: The Welfare Board is a state-controlled entity; its bylaws can be amended by the state government.

administrative.comments: Identifying 'all' inland fishermen in every pond and tank is harder than marine fishermen who operate from fixed landing centers. Prawn disease verification for insurance claims is technically demanding.

timeline.comments: Welfare board expansion can happen in 1-2 years; setting up a new insurance product usually takes 2-3 years for actuarial pricing.

political.comments: Inland fishermen are a significant vote bank in interior districts, often overlooked compared to coastal communities.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a significant equity-focused promise. While inland fishermen are already members of the board, parity in benefits is a major demand. The prawn insurance is a high-value addition for the aquaculture industry.
key_risks: High fiscal burden of extending lean-period relief to inland sectors
Difficulty in verifying 'disease' vs 'poor management' for prawn insurance
Administrative challenge in registering informal inland fishers

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.text: Just as there are Aavin outlets for selling dairy products, dedicated sales outlets will be set up across Tamil Nadu to sell marine food products, to improve the economic condition of fishermen. At these outlets, marine food products such as value-added dried fish and canned fish foods will be sold.
points.title: A Marine Products Sales Outlets

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

evidence.title: TNFDC Marketing Activities
evidence.snippet: TNFDC markets fresh fish and sea food through its chain of retail fish stalls and mobile units in Chennai, Madurai, Coimbatore, and other districts. It also operates the 'Kayal' franchisee model.

plan_existence.notes: The Tamil Nadu Fisheries Development Corporation (TNFDC) already operates retail outlets and mobile restaurants. The promise seeks to scale this to an 'Aavin-like' ubiquitous presence across the state.

fiscal.comments: TNFDC is a profit-making PSU (net profit of ₹12.86 crore in 2023-24). Expanding retail outlets via franchisee models (Kayal Scheme) reduces direct government capital expenditure.

legal.comments: No legal hurdles; falls within the state's power to promote trade and welfare.

administrative.comments: TNFDC has the existing infrastructure, supply chain, and procurement expertise to manage such an expansion.

timeline.comments: Setting up a statewide network of stalls is achievable within 5 years, especially using the existing franchisee model.

political.comments: High public and fisherfolk support; mimics the successful Aavin model which has high brand equity.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible as it builds on a profitable existing state PSU model. The transition from a few urban stalls to a statewide network is a logical scaling exercise.
key_risks: Supply chain logistics for perishable goods
Competition from private seafood retailers
Maintaining quality standards across a large network

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.text: A Seaweed Processing Industrial Park will be set up at Rameswaram. Here, seaweed will be turned into food, fertiliser and cosmetics. In this, priority will be given to women from women's self-help groups.
points.title: 10 Seaweed Park

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

evidence.title: Establishment of Multipurpose Seaweed Park in Tamil Nadu
evidence.snippet: A Multipurpose Seaweed Park has been sanctioned in Tamil Nadu at a cost of Rs. 127.71 crore, with Hub-I at Ramanathapuram for multiplication and Hub-II at Pudukottai for processing.

plan_existence.notes: The project is already under implementation with 50% of civil works completed as of late 2025. The focus on women SHGs is a core component of the existing PMMSY guidelines.

fiscal.comments: The project is already funded (₹127.71 crore) under the central PMMSY scheme with state participation.

legal.comments: Land has already been allotted and statutory clearances for the existing park are in place.

administrative.comments: Implementation is handled by the TN Fisheries Department; progress is already documented.

timeline.comments: Expected completion for the processing hub is July 2026, well within the next government's term.

political.comments: Non-controversial; aligns with both central and state goals for blue economy and women's empowerment.

feasibility.overall_comments: This promise is essentially a commitment to complete and operate a project that is already halfway finished and fully funded.
key_risks: Market linkage for seaweed products
Technical training for SHG members
Environmental impact of specific seaweed species

search_queries: Tamil Nadu marine products sales outlets TNFDC Meen Angadi expansion
Multipurpose Seaweed Park Rameswaram status PMMSY
Tamil Nadu fishing harbour infrastructure cold storage biofloc middleman free trade
Tamil Nadu Sea Shield Scheme coastal erosion barriers 200 km

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.text: Barriers will be built along 200 km in coastal districts most affected by coastal erosion — Nagapattinam, Cuddalore, Viluppuram, Ramanathapuram and Kanyakumari.
points.title: 11 Sea Shield Scheme

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu develops 2km coastal bioshield in Cuddalore
evidence.snippet: Tamil Nadu has established a 2 km long coastal bioshield in Cuddalore to combat erosion. Similar projects are planned for other districts under the Climate Change Mission.

plan_existence.notes: While 'Sea Shield Scheme' is a new name, the state is actively building bio-shields and hard structures. A 200 km target is a significant scale-up from current pilot projects.

fiscal.comments: Hard engineering (sea walls) costs roughly ₹10-15 crore per km. A 200 km project could cost ₹2,000-3,000 crore, requiring significant external funding (World Bank/ADB).

legal.comments: Requires CRZ (Coastal Regulation Zone) clearances from the MoEFCC, which can be difficult for hard structures.

administrative.comments: The PWD (WRO) and Environment Dept have experience in coastal works, but 200 km is a massive engineering undertaking.

timeline.comments: Environmental clearances and construction of 200 km of barriers typically exceed a 5-year window.

political.comments: Highly popular in coastal constituencies facing land loss, though environmental groups may oppose hard barriers.

feasibility.overall_comments: Technically and fiscally demanding. Success depends on whether 'barriers' refers to cost-effective bio-shields or expensive concrete sea walls.
key_risks: High capital cost
CRZ clearance delays
Erosion shifting to adjacent unprotected areas
Maintenance against high-tide damage

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.text: Infrastructure such as cold storage to preserve fish, facilities to convert toxic waste into useful fish feed (Bio Floc Technology), and cage fish farming will be set up at all fishing harbours across Tamil Nadu. A trade chain without middlemen between fishermen and fish traders will be created. Modern processing centres will be set up at all fish-landing sites.
points.title: 12 Development of Fishing Harbours

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

existing_scheme_names: Fisheries and Aquaculture Infrastructure Development Fund (FIDF)

evidence.title: FIDF Sanctions for Tamil Nadu
evidence.snippet: Under FIDF, 64 projects worth ₹1573.73 crore have been sanctioned for Tamil Nadu. PMMSY also funds cold storage and bio-floc units.

plan_existence.notes: Most components (cold storage, bio-floc, cage farming) are already covered under central flagship schemes. The 'middleman-free trade chain' is a recurring policy goal with limited structural implementation to date.

fiscal.comments: Modernizing 'all' harbours is capital intensive. While central funds (PMMSY) exist, the state's share for universal coverage would be substantial.

legal.comments: No major legal hurdles for infrastructure on existing government-owned harbour lands.

administrative.comments: The Fisheries Department has the capacity to execute these projects, though bio-floc for 'toxic waste' is technically misaligned (bio-floc is a farming system).

timeline.comments: Upgrading all harbours and landing sites is a long-term process; 5 years is sufficient for significant progress but not total completion.

political.comments: Eliminating middlemen is politically popular with fishermen but faces resistance from powerful local trading cartels.

feasibility.overall_comments: A solid infrastructure-led promise that aligns with existing national trends. The primary challenge is the fiscal outlay for universal coverage and the political will to disrupt traditional trade networks.
key_risks: Underutilization of cold storage due to electricity costs
Resistance from traditional middlemen
Technical failure of bio-floc systems if not managed properly

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.text: Fishermen will be provided interest-free loans to purchase deep-sea fishing boats equipped with modern processing facilities.
points.title: 13 Deep-Sea Boat Financial Loan

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu: Rs 300 crore allotted for deep sea fishing
evidence.snippet: Under a sub-component of the blue revolution scheme, Rs 300 crore has been released to provide deep sea fishing vessels to traditional fishermen.

evidence.title: Union Budget: Tamil Nadu fishers demand better subsidies
evidence.snippet: The Union and state governments now provide 50% of subsidy with an upper limit of Rs 30 lakh to build new gillnetters and longliners.

plan_existence.notes: Existing schemes provide significant capital subsidies (50-70%) for deep-sea boats, but the remaining cost is typically covered by bank loans with standard interest rates. This promise introduces 'interest-free' loans, which would be a new financial subvention on top of existing capital support.

fiscal.comments: A deep-sea vessel costs between Rs. 80 lakh and Rs. 1.5 crore. Providing interest-free loans for a target of 2,000 boats (as per existing state targets) would require the state to bear an interest burden of approximately Rs. 150-200 crore annually, assuming a 10% interest rate on a Rs. 1,500-2,000 crore loan portfolio.

legal.comments: State governments have the authority to provide interest subventions and credit support for primary sector activities.

administrative.comments: The Fisheries Department already has mechanisms for identifying beneficiaries and disbursing subsidies; adding an interest subvention component is administratively straightforward via tie-ups with cooperative or nationalized banks.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented within a 5-year term, though boat construction and delivery by yards often face backlogs.

political.comments: Highly popular among the fishing community, especially in the Palk Bay region where deep-sea fishing is promoted to prevent border transgressions.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is a logical extension of existing subsidy schemes. While the fiscal burden of interest subvention is manageable, the high unit cost of boats remains a barrier for individual small-scale fishermen even with interest-free loans.
key_risks: High default risk on large principal amounts
Limited capacity of boat-building yards
Rising costs of modern processing equipment

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.subsection_title: C. Water Management and Agricultural Innovations.
points.text: The Cauvery-Vaigai-Gundaru, South Pennaiyar-Palar, Pennaiyar-Cheyyar and Tamirabarani-Vaippar river linking projects have all been announced but have yet to be fully implemented. To implement these immediately, Rs. 5,000 crore will be allocated and works will be completed swiftly.
points.title: 1 River Interlinking

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: C. Water Management and Agricultural Innovations.
Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

evidence.title: Cauvery-Vaigai-Gundar river-linking project status
evidence.snippet: Between 2021-22 and 2024-2025, the government allocated Rs 373 crore for the project... DPR for building the canal at a cost of Rs 3,076 crore is being prepared.

plan_existence.notes: These projects are long-standing state priorities. The Cauvery-Vaigai-Gundaru project was officially launched in 2021. The promise to allocate Rs. 5,000 crore is a specific funding commitment to accelerate these ongoing works.

fiscal.comments: The total cost for the Cauvery-Vaigai-Gundaru project alone is estimated at over Rs. 16,000 crore. The promised Rs. 5,000 crore is only a partial amount and may not be sufficient to complete all four named projects simultaneously.

legal.comments: River interlinking often involves inter-state water sharing disputes (e.g., Karnataka's objections to Cauvery surplus diversion), which can lead to legal stays from the Supreme Court or Tribunals.

administrative.comments: Requires massive land acquisition across multiple districts, which is historically slow and prone to litigation.

timeline.comments: Large-scale river linking projects typically take 10-15 years; completing them 'swiftly' within a 5-year term is highly unlikely given the engineering and land acquisition scale.

political.comments: Extremely high political resonance in the water-starved southern districts of Tamil Nadu.

feasibility.overall_comments: While the projects are already in various stages of planning/execution, the promised Rs. 5,000 crore is a significant down payment but insufficient for full completion. The primary hurdles are inter-state legal disputes and land acquisition rather than just funding.
key_risks: Inter-state water disputes
Land acquisition delays
Cost overruns beyond the Rs. 5,000 crore allocation

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.subsection_title: C. Water Management and Agricultural Innovations.
points.text: More than 39,000 irrigation tanks across Tamil Nadu are in poor condition without proper maintenance. All of these will be restored under the Neerattchi scheme. Through this, 5 lakh new farm ponds will be created.
points.title: 2 Neerattchi Scheme

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: C. Water Management and Agricultural Innovations.
Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu Irrigated Agriculture Modernization Project (TNIAMP)

evidence.title: Restoration of 3,900 tanks under way in TN
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu Rural Development and Panchayat Raj Department has embarked on an initiative to rejuvenate nearly 5,000 minor irrigation tanks.

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Schemes for Farm Ponds
evidence.snippet: The Government of Tamil Nadu is providing 100% subsidy for construction of farm ponds.

plan_existence.notes: Tamil Nadu has approximately 39,000 tanks (PWD and Union tanks). Restoration is a continuous process under various names like Kudimaramathu. The target of 5 lakh farm ponds is an extremely large expansion compared to current annual achievements.

fiscal.comments: At an average cost of Rs. 1 lakh per farm pond, 5 lakh ponds would cost Rs. 5,000 crore. Restoring 39,000 tanks would cost several thousand crores more. This represents a massive fiscal commitment for a single department.

legal.comments: No major legal hurdles as these works are within the state's domain and often utilize MGNREGS funds.

administrative.comments: The scale of 5 lakh ponds requires massive field-level monitoring to prevent 'paper ponds' (fraudulent entries). Restoring 39,000 tanks simultaneously is an administrative challenge for the PWD and Rural Development departments.

timeline.comments: Tank restoration can be done in phases over 5 years. However, creating 1 lakh farm ponds per year is a very high-speed target.

political.comments: Water body restoration is a universally popular promise in rural Tamil Nadu.

feasibility.overall_comments: The scheme is essentially a rebranding and massive scaling up of existing water conservation efforts. The 5 lakh farm pond target is likely over-ambitious and carries high risks of fiscal strain and administrative leakage.
key_risks: Fiscal sustainability
Administrative corruption in desilting/pond digging
Encroachment issues in existing tanks

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.subsection_title: C. Water Management and Agricultural Innovations.
points.text: TVK will aim to achieve 100% irrigated area across Tamil Nadu by converting rainfed dry lands into irrigated cultivable lands. A Special Mission will be launched to fulfil this vision, ensure water security, reduce risk, and significantly raise farmers' incomes.
points.title: 3 Working Towards 100% Irrigated Area

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: C. Water Management and Agricultural Innovations.
Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

evidence.title: Rainfed Agriculture in Tamil Nadu
evidence.snippet: The percentage of total cultivation area in Tamil Nadu that is rainfed (Manavari) is approximately 57%.

plan_existence.notes: Currently, nearly 57% of TN's agriculture is rainfed. Reaching 100% irrigation is a long-term strategic goal rather than a simple scheme. Existing missions like MSDA focus on improving productivity in these areas but do not claim to achieve 100% irrigation.

legal.comments: The state has full authority to launch special missions for agricultural development.

administrative.comments: Converting 57% of the state's rainfed land to irrigated land requires a total overhaul of water resources, including massive new reservoirs or inter-state water transfers which are currently unavailable.

timeline.comments: Achieving 100% irrigation is physically and hydrologically impossible within a 5-year term given the state's current water deficit and the time required for major infrastructure.

political.comments: Aspirational and visionary, though farmers may view '100%' as unrealistic.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is an aspirational vision rather than a feasible 5-year plan. Given that over half of the state's farmland is currently rainfed and the state is water-stressed, the goal of 100% irrigation lacks a realistic hydrological basis.
key_risks: Severe water scarcity in the state
Climate change reducing predictable rainfall
Infeasibility of providing surface water to all drylands
