points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.subsection_title: C. Water Management and Agricultural Innovations.
points.text: The 75 percent subsidy currently provided to small and marginal farmers for installing drip and sprinkler irrigation will be raised to 90 percent. Through this, groundwater drawn for agriculture will be reduced by 40 percent. The shrinking cultivable area will expand by 30 lakh acres. Crop output will also increase.
points.title: 4 Drop-by-Drop Water Scheme

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: C. Water Management and Agricultural Innovations.
Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

existing_scheme_names: Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana (PMKSY) - Per Drop More Crop

evidence.title: Micro Irrigation - Department of Horticulture and Plantation Crops
evidence.snippet: Tamil Nadu is the only state in India providing 100% subsidy to Small and Marginal farmers and 75% subsidy to other farmers for installation of Micro Irrigation systems.

plan_existence.notes: The promise to raise the subsidy to 90% for small and marginal farmers is actually a reduction from the current 100% subsidy provided by the Government of Tamil Nadu for these categories. The 75% figure currently applies to 'other' (non-small/marginal) farmers.

fiscal.comments: The TN Budget 2024-25 allocated ₹450 crore for micro-irrigation. Since the state already provides 100% subsidy for small/marginal farmers, the fiscal promise of 90% would technically save money but contradicts the 'welfare' intent.

legal.comments: No legal hurdles; implemented through executive orders and existing departmental frameworks.

administrative.comments: The Department of Horticulture and Agriculture already has the mechanism (MIMIS portal) to process these subsidies.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented immediately as it modifies an existing active scheme.

political.comments: Reducing a 100% subsidy to 90% for small farmers would likely face severe political backlash from farmer unions.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise appears to be based on a misunderstanding of current state policy, which already offers a more generous 100% subsidy to the target group. Implementation is feasible but politically regressive.
key_risks: Political backlash due to subsidy reduction
Inaccurate targeting of small vs marginal farmers
Technical delays in MIMIS portal registration

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.subsection_title: C. Water Management and Agricultural Innovations.
points.text: To ensure irrigation security, groundwater recharge and drought mitigation throughout Tamil Nadu, TVK will implement zone-wise water infrastructure projects in mission mode. This will include the Anaimalayaaru-Nallaaru Link Project (Tiruppur/Coimbatore), the Erdhalavu-Pulikarai Link Project (Dharmapuri), the building of check dams and regulators in the Cauvery and Thirumanimuthaaru areas of Namakkal district, the revival of Upper Vaippaaru and Mudangiyaaru rivers (Virudhunagar/Rajapalayam), the Ramanadhi-Jambunadhi Link Canal Project (Tenkasi/Alangulam), the Tamirabarani-Karumeniyaaru-Nambiyaaru Link Project (Tirunelveli/Tuticorin), and other important projects.
points.title: 5 Accelerating Irrigation Projects

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: C. Water Management and Agricultural Innovations.
Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Water Resources Department - Major Projects
evidence.snippet: The Tamirabarani-Karumeniyaaru-Nambiyaaru river linking project is a major ongoing scheme in southern TN. The Anaimalayaaru-Nallaaru project is a long-standing demand involving inter-state water sharing.

plan_existence.notes: Most projects listed are either ongoing (Tamirabarani link is ~90% complete), in planning stages, or stalled due to inter-state disputes (Anaimalayaaru-Nallaaru requires agreement with Kerala).

fiscal.comments: These are high-CAPEX projects. The Tamirabarani-Karumeniyaaru-Nambiyaaru project alone had a revised estimate exceeding ₹1,000 crore. Funding multiple such projects simultaneously requires massive budgetary allocation.

legal.comments: The Anaimalayaaru-Nallaaru project is subject to the PAP (Parambikulam Aliyar Project) agreement and requires complex legal and diplomatic negotiations with the Kerala government.

administrative.comments: The Water Resources Department (WRD) has the technical capacity, but land acquisition in districts like Dharmapuri and Tiruppur remains a significant administrative bottleneck.

timeline.comments: Major river-linking and dam projects typically take 10-15 years; completing all in a 5-year term is highly unlikely.

political.comments: High local support in the mentioned districts as these address chronic water scarcity.

feasibility.overall_comments: While these projects are vital for irrigation security, they face significant inter-state legal hurdles and fiscal constraints that make a 5-year 'mission mode' completion unrealistic.
key_risks: Inter-state water sharing disputes (Kerala)
Land acquisition delays
Massive cost overruns
Environmental clearances

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.subsection_title: C. Water Management and Agricultural Innovations.
points.text: In the next 5 years, diesel and electric pump sets will be converted to solar pump sets. Surplus electricity from these will be sold to the electricity board, and the revenue will be given to farmers.
points.title: 6 Welfare of Copra Farmers

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: C. Water Management and Agricultural Innovations.
Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

existing_scheme_names: Chief Minister's Solar Powered Pump Sets Scheme

evidence.title: PM-KUSUM Scheme - TEDA
evidence.snippet: Component C of PM-KUSUM provides for solarization of grid-connected agriculture pumps, allowing farmers to use solar power and sell surplus to DISCOMs.

plan_existence.notes: The scheme to sell surplus power to the board (TANGEDCO) already exists under PM-KUSUM Component C. The promise expands the scale to all diesel/electric sets.

fiscal.comments: Converting all pump sets (approx. 23 lakh in TN) to solar is fiscally daunting. A single 5HP solar pump costs ~₹2.5 lakh; total cost would exceed ₹50,000 crore even with central subsidies.

legal.comments: Requires regulatory approval from TNERC for feed-in tariffs, which is already a standard procedure.

administrative.comments: TANGEDCO's financial health is a major risk for timely payments to farmers for surplus power. Grid stability with decentralized solar input is a technical challenge.

timeline.comments: Converting millions of pumps would take well over a decade given current manufacturing and installation capacities.

political.comments: Extremely popular among farmers as it provides free power and a secondary income stream.

feasibility.overall_comments: The plan aligns with national goals but the scale (converting all pumps in 5 years) is fiscally and logistically improbable given TANGEDCO's debt and the sheer volume of installations required.
key_risks: TANGEDCO financial insolvency
High upfront capital requirement
Grid synchronization issues
Maintenance of solar panels in rural areas

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.subsection_title: C. Water Management and Agricultural Innovations.
points.text: Every farmer will be provided with an AI technology facility on the smartphones they use, to give them information about their soil's characteristics, pesticide damage, nutrient deficiencies, the best day to sow, the best day to harvest and so on. Through this, every farmer can fully understand his own land through his smartphone. Drones will be provided at subsidised prices to agricultural cooperative societies for spraying pesticides.
points.title: 7 Uzhavan AI Scheme

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: C. Water Management and Agricultural Innovations.
Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

evidence.title: Uzhavan App - Tamil Nadu Government
evidence.snippet: The Uzhavan app provides information on subsidies, crop insurance, and weather. The central government provides up to 100% drone subsidy for KVKs and 40-50% for cooperatives.

plan_existence.notes: The Uzhavan app exists but lacks advanced integrated AI for real-time soil/pest diagnostics. Drone subsidies for cooperatives are already active under the SMAM scheme.

fiscal.comments: Software development for AI integration is relatively low-cost. Drone subsidies are already partially funded by the central government (SMAM).

legal.comments: Drone operations must comply with DGCA's Drone Rules 2021, which require pilot certification and 'Green Zone' mapping.

administrative.comments: Requires high-quality localized data for AI accuracy. Providing drones to all cooperatives requires a robust maintenance and training network.

timeline.comments: AI features can be rolled out within 1-2 years. Drone distribution is feasible within a 5-year term.

political.comments: Modernizes the image of agriculture; appeals to the youth and tech-savvy farmers.

feasibility.overall_comments: A highly feasible and modernizing promise that builds on existing digital infrastructure (Uzhavan app) and central schemes (SMAM), though data accuracy for AI remains a challenge.
key_risks: Low digital literacy among elderly farmers
Inaccurate AI diagnostics leading to crop loss
High maintenance cost of drones for cooperatives

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.subsection_title: C. Water Management and Agricultural Innovations.
points.text: Agri-business security centres will be set up in Coimbatore, Madurai, Trichy and elsewhere. Agri-business entrepreneurs who start start-ups in agri-business will be given an investment grant of Rs. 10 lakh. In particular, drone-based pesticide and fertiliser spraying, value-added small milling and processing industries will be encouraged through this.
points.title: 8 Agri-Business Centres

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: C. Water Management and Agricultural Innovations.
Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

existing_scheme_names: TANSEED (Tamil Nadu Startup Seed Grant Fund)

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Startup and Innovation Policy
evidence.snippet: TANSEED provides a seed grant of up to Rs 10 lakh to early-stage startups to bridge the gap in their financial requirements.

evidence.title: TNAU Directorate of Agri Business Development
evidence.snippet: The directorate facilitates agri-business incubation and supports startups in the agricultural sector through various centers across the state.

plan_existence.notes: The promise of a Rs 10 lakh grant aligns exactly with the existing TANSEED grant. The focus on drones and milling is an expansion of the current sectoral priorities under the Tamil Nadu Agricultural Budget.

fiscal.comments: A Rs 10 lakh grant per startup is a standard and manageable outlay within the existing Startup TN budget framework.

legal.comments: No legal hurdles; state governments have the mandate to promote industries and agriculture.

administrative.comments: The existing infrastructure of TNAU and Startup TN provides a strong delivery mechanism for these centers.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented within 1-2 years by leveraging existing incubation centers.

political.comments: High public reception as it targets youth employment and agricultural modernization.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a highly feasible promise that builds on existing successful state frameworks for startup promotion and agricultural technology.
key_risks: Low uptake in specific regions
Technical skill gap in drone operations
Sustainability of startups post-grant

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.subsection_title: C. Water Management and Agricultural Innovations.
points.text: Erode turmeric, Krishnagiri mango and copra produced in the Kongu region will be value-added so that Tamil Nadu farmers get higher prices. A turmeric extraction park will be set up in Erode to separate high-grade turmeric for pharmaceutical use from ordinary turmeric. Cargo transport facilities will be arranged to transport mangoes produced in Krishnagiri directly to Chennai or Bengaluru airports for export. A Coconut Value-Addition Centre will be set up in the Kongu region for value addition of coconuts and coconut products.
points.title: A Value-Addition Centres for Turmeric, Mango and Copra!

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: A Value-Addition Centres for Turmeric, Mango and Copra!
C. Water Management and Agricultural Innovations.
Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

existing_scheme_names: Centre of Excellence for Mango Krishnagiri

evidence.title: Turmeric Research Centre to be set up in Erode
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu government announced the establishment of a Turmeric Research Centre in Erode to help farmers improve yield and quality.

evidence.title: Centre of Excellence for Mango, Krishnagiri
evidence.snippet: The center focuses on high-density planting and productivity improvements for mangoes in the Krishnagiri region.

plan_existence.notes: While research centers exist, the specific 'Turmeric Extraction Park' for pharmaceutical use and dedicated 'Cargo transport facilities' for mangoes represent a shift from production to high-value logistics and processing.

fiscal.comments: Setting up specialized parks and cargo links requires moderate capital investment, often supported by central schemes like PMKSY.

legal.comments: No legal impediments; aligns with the Tamil Nadu Food Processing Policy.

administrative.comments: Requires coordination between Agriculture, MSME, and Transport departments, which is standard practice.

timeline.comments: Infrastructure projects like extraction parks typically take 2-3 years to become operational.

political.comments: Strongly supported by the influential farming lobbies in the Kongu and Krishnagiri belts.

feasibility.overall_comments: A well-targeted infrastructure promise that addresses specific regional agricultural strengths and market gaps.
key_risks: High operational costs for extraction technology
Competition from established hubs in other states
Fluctuating global demand for exports

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.subsection_title: C. Water Management and Agricultural Innovations.
points.text: The Cauvery delta region is the heart of Tamil Nadu agriculture. However, due to environmental degradation, the water resources, soil fertility and agricultural output of the Cauvery delta have suffered major damage. To correct this, the following steps will be taken. Rs. 5,000 crore will be allocated and special attention will be paid to correcting the salinisation of groundwater in the Cauvery delta. Further, steps to raise the depleted groundwater table and measures for soil restoration will be given focused attention. An agricultural ecotoxicology centre will be set up to restore the Cauvery delta; damage to the environment from pollution and chemicals will be investigated and remediated.
points.title: 10 Restoring the Cauvery Delta Ecosystem

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: C. Water Management and Agricultural Innovations.
Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu Protected Agricultural Zone Act, 2020

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Protected Agricultural Zone Authority
evidence.snippet: The act protects the delta from certain industrial projects but does not include a specific Rs 5,000 crore restoration package or an ecotoxicology center.

plan_existence.notes: While the delta is legally protected, the specific financial commitment of Rs 5,000 crore for salinisation and the establishment of an ecotoxicology center are new proposals.

fiscal.comments: Rs 5,000 crore is a significant outlay (approx. 1.5% of the state's annual budget). Funding would likely require external assistance from agencies like NABARD or the World Bank.

legal.comments: The state has full authority over land and water management in the delta region.

administrative.comments: Addressing groundwater salinisation is a long-term technical challenge requiring multi-departmental coordination (PWD, Agriculture, Environment).

timeline.comments: Ecological restoration and soil fertility improvement are decadal processes; visible results within 5 years may be limited.

political.comments: The Cauvery Delta is politically vital; such a large-scale welfare promise is likely to be very popular.

feasibility.overall_comments: A high-stakes, high-budget environmental promise that is technically complex but politically and legally sound.
key_risks: Ineffective implementation of groundwater recharge
High cost of soil remediation
Dependence on external funding

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.subsection_title: C. Water Management and Agricultural Innovations.
points.text: 'Blue-Green Horticulture Parks' powered by solar electricity will be set up in the coastal districts of Ramanathapuram, Nagapattinam and Tuticorin. Through these, rising salinity in the water in coastal districts will be removed. This water will be used for cash crops like chillies and coriander.
points.title: 11 Coastal Horticulture Scheme

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: C. Water Management and Agricultural Innovations.
Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Horticulture Department Schemes
evidence.snippet: Current schemes focus on micro-irrigation and subsidies for seeds but do not feature 'Blue-Green Horticulture Parks' or solar-powered desalination for crops.

plan_existence.notes: This is a novel concept combining renewable energy, desalination, and horticulture specifically for high-salinity coastal zones.

fiscal.comments: Solar-powered desalination for irrigation is extremely capital-intensive and often has a high cost-per-liter, making it fiscally challenging for large-scale agriculture.

legal.comments: Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ) clearances may be required for infrastructure near the shore.

administrative.comments: Requires high technical expertise in both solar energy and desalination technology management.

timeline.comments: Pilot parks could be established within 3 years, but state-wide coastal coverage would take longer.

political.comments: Addresses the long-standing water crisis in arid coastal districts like Ramanathapuram.

feasibility.overall_comments: An innovative but expensive proposal that faces significant economic viability hurdles due to the high cost of desalination for low-margin crops.
key_risks: Prohibitive cost of desalinated water
Maintenance of solar and desalination units in saline environments
Environmental impact of brine disposal

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.subsection_title: C. Water Management and Agricultural Innovations.
points.text: At every paddy harvest in Tamil Nadu, tonnes of straw are produced. However, much of this is not properly used and is either burnt or disposed of as agricultural waste. Changing this, a scheme to produce biogas from straw will be implemented. For this, 10 straw bio-refineries will be set up in Thanjavur and Tiruvarur districts, and ethanol and biogas will be produced at them. Through this, arrangements will be made for paddy farmers to get additional income. Further, these straw bio-refineries will be managed through local farmer groups.
points.title: 12 Delta Bio-Refinery Centre

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: C. Water Management and Agricultural Innovations.
Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Ethanol Blending Policy 2023
evidence.snippet: The policy aims to promote ethanol production from various feedstocks including agricultural residues to support the 20% blending target.

evidence.title: IOCL to set up CBG plants in Tamil Nadu
evidence.snippet: The SATAT initiative encourages entrepreneurs to set up Compressed Bio-Gas (CBG) plants and provide CBG to Oil Marketing Companies.

plan_existence.notes: While the Central Government's SATAT scheme and the State's Ethanol Policy provide a framework for bio-energy, a specific state-run project to establish 10 dedicated straw bio-refineries specifically in Thanjavur and Tiruvarur managed by farmer groups is a new, localized proposal.

legal.comments: State government has full authority to establish industrial centers and agricultural processing units under the State List.

administrative.comments: Managing high-tech bio-refineries through local farmer groups requires significant technical training and a robust cooperative governance framework which is currently underdeveloped for this scale of technology.

timeline.comments: Setting up 10 bio-refineries involving land acquisition, environmental clearances, and technology transfer typically takes 3-5 years.

political.comments: High political appeal in the Delta region as it addresses the waste management issue and provides additional income to the influential farming lobby.

feasibility.overall_comments: The proposal is technically sound and aligns with national green energy goals, but the administrative challenge of farmer-led management of complex chemical refineries is the primary hurdle.
key_risks: Technical failure of farmer-led management
Supply chain volatility in straw collection
High capital expenditure requirements
Environmental clearance delays

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.subsection_title: C. Water Management and Agricultural Innovations.
points.text: Ramanathapuram and Virudhunagar districts will be declared drought zones. In these drought zones, farmers who grow millets and oilseeds using drip irrigation and sprinkler irrigation methods that use less water will be given an incentive.
points.title: 13 Drought Zones!

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: C. Water Management and Agricultural Innovations.
Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Millet Mission 2023-2027
evidence.snippet: The government has launched a 5-year mission to increase millet production, providing subsidies for seeds and micro-irrigation in dryland areas.

evidence.title: Micro Irrigation Scheme - TNHN
evidence.snippet: Provides 100% subsidy to small/marginal farmers and 75% to other farmers for installing drip and sprinkler irrigation systems.

plan_existence.notes: Ramanathapuram and Virudhunagar are already classified as drought-prone districts in administrative records. Incentives for millets and micro-irrigation already exist under the TN Millet Mission and PMKSY; this promise would likely expand the quantum of incentive.

legal.comments: Declaration of drought zones and provision of agricultural incentives are standard executive functions of the state.

administrative.comments: The state already has the machinery to distribute micro-irrigation subsidies and crop-specific incentives through the Department of Agriculture.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented immediately through a Government Order (GO) and budgetary allocation.

political.comments: Strongly favorable in the southern districts which face chronic water scarcity.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible as it builds upon existing administrative frameworks and addresses a genuine, long-standing geographic grievance in Southern Tamil Nadu.
key_risks: Budgetary constraints for increased incentive amounts
Ensuring actual adoption of micro-irrigation by smallholders
Groundwater depletion despite efficient irrigation

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.subsection_title: C. Water Management and Agricultural Innovations.
points.text: Quality cold storage warehouses will be set up to prevent large-scale post-harvest wastage of agricultural produce. Further, to protect agricultural produce from rotting and spoilage due to rain and sun, appropriate packaging facilities will also be put in place.
points.title: 14 Cold Storage Warehouses

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: C. Water Management and Agricultural Innovations.
Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

existing_scheme_names: Supply Chain Management Project for Perishables
Tamil Nadu State Agricultural Marketing Board (TNSAMB) Infrastructure

evidence.title: TNSAMB - Cold Storage Facilities
evidence.snippet: TNSAMB operates several cold storage units and godowns across the state to help farmers store produce and prevent wastage.

evidence.title: Supply Chain Management Project in Tamil Nadu
evidence.snippet: A major project implemented in 10 districts to provide cold chain infrastructure, including primary processing centers and cold storage.

plan_existence.notes: Extensive infrastructure already exists under TNSAMB and the World Bank-funded Supply Chain Management project. The promise suggests a further scale-up and addition of packaging facilities.

legal.comments: No legal hurdles; agricultural marketing and infrastructure are state subjects.

administrative.comments: The state has existing agencies (TNSAMB, TANFED) capable of executing this, though maintenance of cold storage units has historically been a challenge.

timeline.comments: Construction of a network of warehouses and packaging units can be completed within a 5-year term.

political.comments: Universally popular among farmers as it reduces distress sales and post-harvest losses.

feasibility.overall_comments: A standard but essential infrastructure promise. Success depends on the operational efficiency and electricity supply reliability for the cold storage units.
key_risks: High operational and electricity costs
Underutilization by farmers due to transport costs
Poor maintenance of cooling equipment

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.subsection_title: D. Livestock and Dairy Products Development
points.text: To improve the livelihoods of two lakh families, milch cows and goats will be given to them free of charge.
points.title: 1. Free Goats and Cows

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: D. Livestock and Dairy Products Development
Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

existing_scheme_names: Scheme for Free Distribution of Milch Cows and Goats/Sheep

evidence.title: Animal Husbandry Dept - Free Distribution Scheme
evidence.snippet: The scheme provides free milch cows to poor widows, destitute, and disabled women, and goats/sheep to the poorest of the poor families.

plan_existence.notes: This is a long-standing flagship welfare scheme in Tamil Nadu, famously associated with the 2011-2021 administration. The promise specifies a target of 2 lakh families, which is a continuation/expansion of this existing model.

legal.comments: Well-established legal and administrative precedent for such welfare distributions in the state.

administrative.comments: The Animal Husbandry Department has a robust existing mechanism for procurement, veterinary verification, and distribution.

timeline.comments: Distribution to 2 lakh families is easily achievable over a 5-year period (40,000 families per year).

political.comments: Highly popular 'freebie' scheme with proven electoral impact and direct benefit to the rural poor.

feasibility.overall_comments: Extremely feasible as it uses a proven delivery model. The primary concern is the quality of livestock procured and the survival rate of the animals.
key_risks: Corruption in livestock procurement
High mortality rate of distributed animals
Inadequate veterinary support for beneficiaries

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.subsection_title: D. Livestock and Dairy Products Development
points.text: The procurement price paid by Aavin to milk producers will be raised to Rs. 44 per litre.
points.title: 2 Increase in Milk Procurement Price

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: D. Livestock and Dairy Products Development
Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

evidence.title: Aavin Milk Procurement Declines Amid Rising Heat and Incentive Delays
evidence.snippet: Presently, Aavin pays Rs 38 per litre for cow milk, including a Rs 3 incentive, whereas private brands offer Rs 40 per litre.

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu government raises Aavin milk procurement price by ₹3
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu government has increased the procurement prices of Aavin milk by ₹3 per litre, causing the per-litre procurement price to go from ₹35 to ₹38 for cow milk.

plan_existence.notes: Aavin currently procures milk from approximately 3.85 lakh producers. The current procurement price for cow milk is Rs. 38 per litre (as of late 2023/2024). The promise to raise it to Rs. 44 represents a Rs. 6 (approx. 15.8%) increase.

fiscal.comments: Aavin reportedly procures 35-36 lakh litres daily. A Rs. 6 hike per litre would cost approximately Rs. 2.16 crore per day, or roughly Rs. 788 crore annually. Given Aavin's reported losses of over Rs. 800 crore, this would require significant state budgetary support.

legal.comments: The state government has full authority to set procurement prices for its cooperative federation (Aavin).

administrative.comments: Aavin has an established network of 9,189 cooperative societies and 3.85 lakh pouring members; price adjustments are handled through existing digital payment systems.

timeline.comments: Procurement price hikes can be implemented immediately via government order.

political.comments: High public and farmer support as producers have been demanding hikes of up to Rs. 12-15 per litre to cover rising input costs.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible administratively and politically, but fiscally challenging without increasing retail prices or providing a large state subsidy to Aavin, which is already financially strained.
key_risks: Increased fiscal deficit for Aavin/State
Potential pressure to increase retail milk prices for consumers
Private dairies may further hike prices to maintain competitive edge

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.subsection_title: D. Livestock and Dairy Products Development
points.text: Measures will be taken to increase production of A2 milk — which is obtained only from our native cows — through Aavin, to increase production of food products such as ghee, paneer and ice cream made from it, and to market them in a quality manner.
points.title: 3 A2 Milk Encouragement Scheme

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: D. Livestock and Dairy Products Development
Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

evidence.title: Aavin expands dairy market share, aims for 65% share in 5 years
evidence.snippet: Aavin is setting up a new ice cream manufacturing plant in Tiruchy... to produce high-quality, value-added dairy products.

plan_existence.notes: Aavin already launched A2 milk in the Chennai market around 2022. The promise focuses on expanding this to value-added products like ghee, paneer, and ice cream specifically derived from A2 milk, which is currently a niche segment.

fiscal.comments: Investment in specialized processing lines for A2 products is moderate. Aavin is already investing Rs. 43.41 crore in a new ice cream plant in Trichy.

legal.comments: No legal hurdles; falls under the mandate of the Dairy Development Department.

administrative.comments: Requires separate procurement and processing streams to maintain A2 purity, which adds operational complexity to the existing Aavin infrastructure.

timeline.comments: New product lines and marketing campaigns can be rolled out within 2-3 years.

political.comments: Promoting native breeds (Jallikattu-related sentiment) is politically popular in Tamil Nadu.

feasibility.overall_comments: A logical expansion of Aavin's premium product line that leverages existing infrastructure and cultural sentiment toward native cattle.
key_risks: Supply chain segregation (keeping A2 milk separate from regular milk)
Higher retail price may limit market size to urban affluent consumers
Low availability of pure native cow milk in large volumes

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.subsection_title: D. Livestock and Dairy Products Development
points.text: Families below the poverty line engaged in livestock rearing will be provided full livestock insurance free of cost. Further, steps will also be taken to appropriately provide incentives to those families.
points.title: 4 Insurance for Livestock

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: D. Livestock and Dairy Products Development
Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

evidence.title: Crop Insurance :: Livestock Insurance Scheme - TNAU Agritech Portal
evidence.snippet: The premium of the insurance is subsidized to the tune of 50%. The entire cost of the subsidy is being borne by the Central Government.

evidence.title: Animal Husbandry, Insurance departments ink MoU for livestock insurance scheme
evidence.snippet: Under the scheme, a 50% government subsidy will be available in the general category... and 70% for SC/ST categories.

plan_existence.notes: Existing schemes (NLM) typically provide 50-70% subsidy on premiums. The promise to provide 'full' (100%) insurance free of cost for BPL families is an amendment to remove the beneficiary's share of the premium.

fiscal.comments: Annual premiums for cattle are roughly Rs. 1,500-2,000 per animal. Covering the remaining 30-50% premium for all BPL livestock owners would cost the state an estimated Rs. 100-200 crore annually, depending on uptake.

legal.comments: State has the power to provide additional subsidies over and above central schemes.

administrative.comments: Requires coordination with insurance companies and the Animal Husbandry department for tagging (RFID) and health certification.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented within a single budget cycle.

political.comments: Strongly pro-poor and protects rural livelihoods against climate/disease shocks.

feasibility.overall_comments: A feasible welfare expansion that builds on existing central frameworks but requires the state to absorb the full premium cost for the BPL segment.
key_risks: High claim ratios leading to insurance companies withdrawing
Administrative delays in ear-tagging and health certificates
Fraudulent claims or 'moral hazard' issues

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.subsection_title: D. Livestock and Dairy Products Development
points.text: To revive Tamil Nadu's traditional cattle breeds — Kangayam, Pulikulam, Umbalachery, Bargur and Alambadi — women in self-help groups rearing these breeds will be given Rs. 5,000 as a livestock grant. Livestock banks will be created to sell livestock products made by women's self-help groups to farmers at fair prices.
points.title: 5 Traditional Cattle Breed Revival

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: D. Livestock and Dairy Products Development
Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

evidence.title: TNLDC - Tamil Nadu LiveStock Development Corporation
evidence.snippet: The Agency primary objective is to consolidate on the progress made... and improve milk production and animal productivity.

plan_existence.notes: While Tamil Nadu has breed-specific conservation research centers (e.g., for Kangayam and Bargur), a specific Rs. 5,000 livestock grant for women SHGs rearing these breeds and the creation of 'Livestock Banks' for their products is a new proposal.

fiscal.comments: A one-time grant of Rs. 5,000 is relatively low-cost. If 100,000 women are covered, the outlay is only Rs. 50 crore.

legal.comments: No legal constraints; falls under state welfare and agricultural promotion.

administrative.comments: Identifying and verifying specific traditional breeds owned by SHG members requires technical expertise from the Animal Husbandry department to prevent misclassification.

timeline.comments: Grants can be disbursed quickly; setting up 'Livestock Banks' (marketing hubs) would take 2-3 years.

political.comments: Strongly aligns with Tamil cultural identity and women's empowerment.

feasibility.overall_comments: A highly targeted, culturally resonant scheme with low fiscal burden but moderate administrative verification needs.
key_risks: Difficulty in verifying pure-bred status of livestock
Sustainability of 'Livestock Banks' without professional marketing
Exclusion of non-SHG native breed rearers

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.subsection_title: D. Livestock and Dairy Products Development
points.text: Large-scale feed banks to store and sell livestock feed, and cold-storage facilities to preserve milk, will be set up at every village panchayat.
points.title: 6 Cattle Feed Bank

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: D. Livestock and Dairy Products Development
Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

evidence.title: Aavin to install 200 bulk milk coolers in villages across TN
evidence.snippet: Aavin plans to install Bulk Milk Coolers (BMC) in villages to increase chilling capacity and prevent spoilage, funded under NPDD and VBMPS.

evidence.title: Aavin - Tirunelveli District Cooperative Milk Producers Union
evidence.snippet: Milk is procured from village level societies and chilled at Bulk Milk Cooling centers (BMCC) to maintain quality.

plan_existence.notes: Tamil Nadu already has an extensive network of Bulk Milk Coolers (BMCs) managed by Aavin at the village society level. Cattle feed is also supplied through these cooperatives, often with subsidies. The promise seeks to expand this to every single village panchayat (approx. 12,525), which is a significant scale-up from the current coverage of ~9,300 active societies.

fiscal.comments: Setting up cold storage and feed banks in all 12,525 panchayats is capital intensive. A single 5000L BMC unit can cost significantly, and 12,500 units would require an outlay likely exceeding ₹2,000-3,000 crore based on current infrastructure costs.

legal.comments: No legal hurdles; falls within the state's mandate for agriculture and animal husbandry.

administrative.comments: Aavin has a robust existing cooperative structure that can be leveraged to manage these facilities.

timeline.comments: Constructing and equipping 12,500+ locations in 5 years is a massive logistical challenge, though feasible with decentralized execution.

political.comments: Highly popular among the rural dairy-farming vote bank, which is a critical constituency in TN.

feasibility.overall_comments: The plan is an ambitious expansion of the existing Aavin network. While administratively sound due to existing cooperative structures, the fiscal burden of 'every panchayat' coverage is high and may lead to underutilization in smaller villages.
key_risks: High capital and maintenance costs
Potential underutilization in low-milk-yield panchayats
Electricity supply consistency for cold storage

points.section_title: Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen
points.subsection_title: D. Livestock and Dairy Products Development
points.text: Veterinary hospitals with adequate staff will be set up across Tamil Nadu. Similarly, multi-speciality veterinary hospitals will be set up in suburban districts.
points.title: 7 Veterinary Hospitals

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: D. Livestock and Dairy Products Development
Welfare of Farmers, Agricultural Labourers and Fishermen

existing_scheme_names: Department of Animal Husbandry and Veterinary Services Infrastructure

evidence.title: Animal Husbandry - Tamil Nadu Government Portal
evidence.snippet: The department provides veterinary services through a network of polyclinics, hospitals, and dispensaries across the state.

evidence.title: Madras Veterinary College Teaching Hospital
evidence.snippet: Provides multi-speciality veterinary care including surgery, radiology, and emergency services in Chennai.

plan_existence.notes: Tamil Nadu has a well-established network of over 2,500 veterinary institutions. Multi-speciality care is currently concentrated in major cities (Chennai, Coimbatore) via TANUVAS. The promise targets the gap in 'suburban districts' (like those surrounding Chennai) for advanced care.

legal.comments: State government has full authority over animal husbandry and veterinary services.

administrative.comments: Staffing multi-speciality hospitals requires highly trained veterinary specialists, who are in shorter supply than general practitioners.

timeline.comments: Construction of hospitals and upgrading existing ones can be achieved within a 5-year term.

political.comments: Strong support from both rural livestock owners and the growing urban/suburban pet-owner demographic.

feasibility.overall_comments: A logical expansion of existing services. The focus on suburban districts addresses the rapid urbanization of areas around Chennai and Coimbatore where veterinary demand is high but advanced public facilities are lacking.
key_risks: Shortage of specialist veterinary doctors
High cost of advanced medical equipment
Maintenance of specialized facilities in suburban areas

points.text: Every government school in Tamil Nadu will be equipped with world-class science laboratories, computer labs with high-speed internet, STEM-based modern education (Science, Technology, Engineering, Maths), modern toilets, clean drinking water, quality furniture, and counselling centres. By 2031, all school classrooms in Tamil Nadu will be converted into smart classrooms.
points.title: 1 Modernising Government Schools

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu: Government schools to be modernised for Rs 7,000 crore
evidence.snippet: The Perasiriyar Anbazhagan School Development Scheme was launched to upgrade and remodel government schools with new classrooms and smart facilities.

evidence.title: Smart classrooms to be set up in 20,000 schools in Tamil Nadu
evidence.snippet: The government informed the court that works are underway to establish smart classrooms in 20,000 schools and hi-tech labs in nearly 7,000 schools.

plan_existence.notes: The promise is a direct continuation and expansion of the ongoing Perasiriyar Anbazhagan School Development Scheme, which already targets 18,000-26,000 classrooms. The 2031 deadline for 'all classrooms' provides a specific long-term target for the current trajectory.

fiscal.comments: The current PASDS has an outlay of over ₹7,000 crore. Expanding this to 'every' school and 'all' classrooms by 2031 would likely require doubling or tripling this investment over the next decade.

legal.comments: Education is a concurrent subject; the state has full legal capacity to modernize its own schools.

administrative.comments: The School Education Department has already demonstrated capacity by initiating smart classroom projects in 20,000 schools.

timeline.comments: The 2031 target (5 years beyond the 2026 election) is realistic for a total state-wide conversion.

political.comments: Education modernization is a core pillar of the 'Dravidian Model' and has broad public support.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a highly feasible continuation of an existing flagship program. The specific 2031 target for 100% smart classroom coverage is a measurable and realistic goal given current progress.
key_risks: Sustained funding over multiple budget cycles
Maintenance and theft of electronic equipment in rural schools
Teacher training for STEM/Smart tools

points.text: Special schemes will be designed and implemented in full force to eliminate gender and social-based disparities in students' learning abilities. Through this, Tamil Nadu will be made the top state in the country in Learning Outcomes, and on par with developed countries.
points.title: 2 Becoming the Top State in Learning Outcomes

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 2 Becoming the Top State in Learning Outcomes

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu unveils State Education Policy 2025
evidence.snippet: The SEP 2025 focuses on equity, inclusion, and bridging learning gaps through initiatives like Ennum Ezhuthum and Naan Mudhalvan.

evidence.title: Ennum Ezhuthum Mission - Addressing Learning Gaps
evidence.snippet: Launched in 2022 to ensure foundational literacy and numeracy for all students under age 8 by 2025.

plan_existence.notes: The promise aligns with the goals of the Ennum Ezhuthum mission and the newly unveiled State Education Policy 2025. These existing frameworks specifically target learning gaps and social disparities in education.

fiscal.comments: Improving learning outcomes is more about pedagogical reform and teacher training than massive infrastructure spend, making it fiscally manageable within the existing ₹48,000+ crore education budget.

legal.comments: The state has the right to design its own curriculum and learning outcome frameworks under the State Education Policy.

administrative.comments: Measuring and improving 'Learning Outcomes' is notoriously difficult and requires consistent, high-quality assessment and teacher accountability.

timeline.comments: Significant shifts in state-wide learning outcomes usually take a decade to manifest in national surveys like NAS or ASER.

political.comments: Focusing on 'Learning Outcomes' as a counter to the National Education Policy (NEP) is a key political strategy for the state government.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a policy-heavy promise that builds on existing missions. While the goal of being 'top in the country' is aspirational, the administrative framework (Ennum Ezhuthum) is already in place to pursue it.
key_risks: Difficulty in measuring qualitative learning improvements
Resistance to pedagogical changes among teaching staff
External factors like socio-economic conditions affecting student performance

points.text: 100 elite schools will be opened across Tamil Nadu for students from socially and economically disadvantaged families who excel in education. In these schools, education, food, accommodation, textbooks and uniforms will be provided free of cost to students from 6th to 12th standard. Of these 100 schools, 50 will be dedicated exclusively for girl students.
points.title: 3 Kamarajar Special Schools

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Model Schools Strengthening Competitive Education
evidence.snippet: The Government of Tamil Nadu has established 38 Model Schools, ensuring that each district has one such institution to improve the quality of education in government institutions.

plan_existence.notes: Tamil Nadu currently operates 38 Model Schools (one per district) focusing on students from government schools, primarily for classes 9-12. The promise expands this to 100 schools and broadens the age group to include classes 6-8.

fiscal.comments: Establishing and maintaining 100 residential schools with free food and accommodation involves high capital and recurring expenditure. While TN has a history of funding such schools, the scale-up from 38 to 100 is significant.

legal.comments: The state has full authority to establish and fund special schools for disadvantaged sections under the Right to Education and state policy.

administrative.comments: The existing Model School Cell provides a framework, but managing 100 residential units requires robust decentralized administration and staffing.

timeline.comments: Constructing or upgrading 100 schools with residential facilities is unlikely to be fully completed within 5 years, though a phased rollout is possible.

political.comments: Naming schools after Kamarajar and focusing on disadvantaged students is politically popular and aligns with the state's social justice narrative.

feasibility.overall_comments: The plan is a logical expansion of the existing Model Schools program. It is highly feasible administratively but requires substantial long-term fiscal commitment for residential maintenance.
key_risks: High recurring costs for residential facilities
Quality maintenance across 100 units
Teacher recruitment for elite standards

points.text: To curb the rising school student dropout rate, AI-powered monitoring systems will be established across Tamil Nadu. 1,000 education coordinators will be appointed to identify students dropping out of school due to social and economic reasons and re-admit them to school.
points.title: 4 0% Dropout

evidence.title: IIT-M & school education department team up to boost digital assessment
evidence.snippet: The state school education department has teamed up with IIT-Madras to turn the EMIS into an assessment-focussed system using AI and data science.

evidence.title: School Education Department to Introduce Programmes for 0% Dropouts
evidence.snippet: To identify potential dropouts, we flag students who are absent for more than two weeks and upload this to the EMIS.

plan_existence.notes: TN already uses the EMIS platform to track long-absent students and has collaborated with IIT-Madras for AI integration. The appointment of 1,000 dedicated coordinators is a new human resource addition to the existing digital tracking.

fiscal.comments: The cost of 1,000 coordinators is relatively low compared to the overall education budget. AI infrastructure is already being developed in partnership with academic institutions.

legal.comments: Data privacy concerns regarding AI monitoring of minors must be addressed, but there are no major legal hurdles to hiring coordinators.

administrative.comments: The EMIS system is already robust; adding a human layer of 1,000 coordinators for field verification is a standard administrative task.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented within 1-2 years as the digital foundation already exists.

political.comments: Zero dropout is a universally supported goal with no significant political opposition.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a highly feasible and technologically modern approach to a persistent problem, building effectively on existing digital infrastructure.
key_risks: Data privacy of students
Ensuring coordinators focus on re-admission rather than just data entry
Accuracy of AI predictive models

points.text: The Chief Minister's Breakfast Scheme will be extended to government schools and government-aided schools up to Class 8, and nutritious breakfast will be provided. Through this scheme, it will be ensured that all students receive balanced nutrition.
points.title: 5 Expansion of the Breakfast Scheme

evidence.title: TN CM Stalin inaugurates expansion of breakfast scheme to govt aided schools
evidence.snippet: The scheme now benefits 20.59 lakh children across the state... It will benefit students from Classes 1 to 5 studying in government-aided schools.

plan_existence.notes: As of August 2025, the scheme covers students from Classes 1 to 5 in both government and government-aided schools. The promise seeks to extend this coverage up to Class 8.

fiscal.comments: The current scheme for Classes 1-5 costs approximately ₹600 crore annually for 20 lakh students. Extending it to Class 8 would likely increase the budget by 60-80% (approx. ₹400-500 crore additional).

legal.comments: The state has full jurisdiction to expand its own welfare schemes.

administrative.comments: The logistics of centralized kitchens and transport are already in place; scaling up volume is feasible but requires more vehicles and staff.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented immediately in the next academic cycle as the delivery mechanism is proven.

political.comments: Extremely popular scheme with high social impact; expansion is a strong political move.

feasibility.overall_comments: A highly feasible expansion of a successful flagship program, though it requires a significant increase in the annual food subsidy budget.
key_risks: Logistical strain on centralized kitchens
Maintaining food quality at higher volumes
Budgetary pressure from rising food prices

points.text: The 7.5 percent reservation for government school students in undergraduate degree programmes will be raised to 10 percent. Further, a 10 percent reservation will be given to government school students in postgraduate programmes as well.
points.title: 6 10 Percent Reservation

existing_scheme_names: 7.5% Horizontal Reservation for Government School Students

evidence.title: TN sets apart 7.5% preferential quota for govt school students
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu government set apart 7.5 percent reservation on 'preferential basis' to state government school students for admissions to engineering, agriculture, veterinary, law and other professional degree courses.

plan_existence.notes: The 7.5% horizontal reservation currently applies to undergraduate professional courses. The promise proposes increasing this to 10% and introducing a new 10% quota for postgraduate programs.

fiscal.comments: Reservation changes have no direct fiscal cost, though the state currently pays the fees for 7.5% quota students, which would increase proportionally.

legal.comments: Increasing the quota to 10% and extending it to PG may face judicial scrutiny. The 7.5% was justified by the Justice Murugesan committee; a further increase would require fresh empirical data to avoid violating the 'reasonableness' test in reservation law.

administrative.comments: Implementing reservation changes in the admission portal is a simple administrative update.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented in a single admission cycle via legislation/GO.

political.comments: Popular among the rural and poor electorate, but may face pushback from private school parents and merit-based advocacy groups.

feasibility.overall_comments: Technically simple to implement but legally vulnerable. The extension to PG is a significant policy shift that will likely be challenged in court.
key_risks: Legal challenge in High Court/Supreme Court
Potential dilution of merit in specialized PG courses
Need for fresh commission data to justify the 10% figure

points.text: A Headmasters' Skill Development Scheme will be implemented to further sharpen the leadership qualities of school headmasters. A dedicated leadership training institute will be set up for this, and continuous training will be given to headmasters across the state. Under this scheme, schools performing well and achieving excellence in learning outcomes will be given Rs. 50 lakh as an incentive.
points.title: 7 Headmasters' Skill Development Scheme

evidence.title: Anna Leadership Award announced for school heads - DT Next
evidence.snippet: Under the scheme, the award and shield of appreciation had been announced to 100 school heads each year... the school head who bags the award is given Rs 10 lakh as an incentive.

plan_existence.notes: Tamil Nadu already has the Arignar Anna Leadership Award which provides a Rs. 10 lakh incentive to 100 headmasters annually. The manifesto promise proposes increasing this incentive to Rs. 50 lakh and establishing a dedicated leadership training institute, which represents a significant financial and infrastructural expansion.

legal.comments: The state government has full authority over school education and teacher training under the Concurrent List.

administrative.comments: The existing SCERT and DIET infrastructure can be leveraged to establish the leadership institute, though scaling incentives to 50L requires strict audit mechanisms.

timeline.comments: Setting up a training institute and disbursing awards can easily be achieved within a 5-year term.

political.comments: Highly popular among the teaching community; no significant opposition expected for rewarding school excellence.

feasibility.overall_comments: The plan is a logical expansion of the existing Arignar Anna Leadership Award. While the 5x increase in the incentive amount is steep, the focus on institutionalizing leadership training through a dedicated academy is a sound policy move for long-term educational quality.
key_risks: Subjectivity in defining 'excellence' and 'learning outcomes' for the 50L incentive
Potential for corruption in the selection process of awardees
Underutilization of the training institute if not integrated with career progression

points.text: To encourage entrepreneurship among students from their school days, under the Tamil Nadu Student Entrepreneur Scheme, students studying in Classes 11 and 12 in government schools will be given Rs. 5,100 as investment capital. This fund will help these students develop small-scale business models. Further, special entrepreneurship classes will be conducted as part of extracurricular activities in all schools.
points.title: 8 Tamil Nadu Student Entrepreneur Scheme

evidence.title: T.N. to offer grants up to ₹50 lakh each for innovative startups - The Hindu
evidence.snippet: The grants would also support student startups up to a maximum of ₹1 lakh. Students should be enrolled and studying in any of the colleges/universities within Tamil Nadu.

plan_existence.notes: While Tamil Nadu has the 'Tamil Nadu Startup Seed Grant Fund' for college and university students, there is currently no state-wide scheme providing direct investment capital (Rs. 5,100) specifically to Class 11 and 12 students in government schools.

legal.comments: No legal barriers to providing cash grants for educational or vocational purposes to minors through guardians/bank accounts.

administrative.comments: Distributing small cash amounts to hundreds of thousands of students and monitoring 'business models' at the school level is administratively intensive.

timeline.comments: Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) can be set up quickly, but the 'entrepreneurship classes' require curriculum revision and teacher training.

political.comments: Likely to be very popular among students and parents as a direct cash benefit.

feasibility.overall_comments: A novel approach to fostering a startup culture at the school level. The fiscal burden is manageable given the small per-student amount, but the real challenge lies in ensuring the funds are used for 'business models' rather than general consumption.
key_risks: Diversion of funds for non-entrepreneurial purposes
Lack of qualified mentors in government schools to guide business model development
High administrative cost of monitoring small-scale investments

points.text: To ensure the mental wellbeing and safety of government school students and teachers, 5,000 mental health counsellors will be appointed, and psychological first aid training will be given to students and teachers. A happiness curriculum will be introduced to reduce stress and boost concentration among students.
points.title: 9 Student Mental Wellbeing Protection

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu Mental Health Policy for Educational Institutions

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu's mental health policy for educational institutions - The Hindu
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu government issued a mental health policy for educational institutions earlier this month (March 2026). It provides for mental health and counselling services... and specifies the role of teachers.

plan_existence.notes: Tamil Nadu recently notified a comprehensive mental health policy for schools in March 2026. The manifesto promise expands on this by specifying a target of 5,000 counsellors and introducing a 'Happiness Curriculum' similar to those in Delhi and Tripura.

legal.comments: Aligned with the Mental Healthcare Act, 2017 and the state's own 2026 policy.

administrative.comments: Recruiting 5,000 qualified mental health professionals is extremely difficult given the current shortage of trained psychologists in India.

timeline.comments: Hiring 5,000 staff and rolling out a new curriculum across all schools will likely take the full 5-year term.

political.comments: Strong public support given rising concerns over student suicides and academic stress.

feasibility.overall_comments: While the policy intent is excellent and builds on existing frameworks, the target of 5,000 counsellors is highly ambitious and faces a severe supply-side constraint of qualified personnel. The 'Happiness Curriculum' is a proven model elsewhere but requires significant teacher buy-in.
key_risks: Shortage of 5,000 qualified mental health professionals for recruitment
Risk of 'Happiness Curriculum' becoming a rote-learning subject if not implemented with empathy
Sustainability of funding for a large new cadre of specialized staff

points.text: In districts without government professional colleges, five new engineering colleges and three new medical colleges will be opened. Efforts will be made to urge the Union Government to grant approval for the medical colleges. Thirty government arts and science colleges will be upgraded to autonomous colleges.
points.title: 1 New Government Professional Colleges

existing_scheme_names: Centrally Sponsored Scheme for Establishment of new Medical Colleges
Policy of one Government Medical College per District

evidence.title: TN seeks nod for medical colleges in six districts
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu government has sought approval from the Centre to establish medical colleges in six districts including Tenkasi, Perambalur, and Mayiladuthurai.

evidence.title: TN misses chance to apply for new govt med colleges
evidence.snippet: Perambalur, Mayiladuthurai, Tirupattur, Ranipet, Kancheepuram and Kallakurichi do not yet have a government medical college.

plan_existence.notes: The state already has a policy to establish one government medical college per district. Six districts currently lack one. The promise to open three new medical colleges is actually a subset of the existing plan for six. Upgrading 30 arts colleges to autonomous status is a new specific target.

fiscal.comments: Establishing a single medical college costs approximately Rs. 300-500 crore. Opening 8 professional colleges would require an outlay exceeding Rs. 2,500 crore, plus recurring operational costs.

legal.comments: National Medical Commission (NMC) guidelines now cap MBBS seats based on state population (100 seats per 10 lakh). Tamil Nadu already exceeds this ratio, making federal approval for new colleges legally difficult.

administrative.comments: Tamil Nadu has a strong track record of executing large-scale medical infrastructure projects, having opened 11 colleges in a single year (2021).

timeline.comments: Construction and licensing of medical colleges typically take 3-5 years; delivering all 8 within one term is ambitious but possible if started immediately.

political.comments: Highly popular promise as it addresses regional disparity in professional education access.

feasibility.overall_comments: While administratively feasible, the plan faces significant legal hurdles from the NMC's population-based seat capping and requires heavy fiscal investment.
key_risks: NMC rejection due to seat-to-population ratio caps
Delay in Union Government funding/approval
High recurring expenditure for faculty and maintenance

timeline.comments: Can be implemented in the first academic cycle of the new government.

points.text: The government itself will bear the education and living expenses of Tamil Nadu students who qualify to study at the world's top 100 educational institutions. In return, after completing their studies, these students will work for three years as government advisors, or as honorary teachers at Tamil Nadu educational institutions, or as entrepreneurs who invest in Tamil Nadu. This knowledge exchange will help guide successive generations of students.
points.title: 3 First Respect for Intellect

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu Overseas Scholarship for SC/ST Students

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Government Overseas Scholarship 2024-25
evidence.snippet: Provides up to Rs. 36 Lakhs per annum for SC/ST students admitted to top 500 QS ranked universities.

evidence.title: TN govt introduces overseas scholarship for Muslim students
evidence.snippet: A new scheme aids 10 students from the Muslim community to pursue higher studies abroad with assistance up to Rs. 36 lakh.

plan_existence.notes: Existing schemes are community-specific (SC/ST/Minority) and cover top 500/250 rankings. This promise expands eligibility to all students but tightens the ranking requirement to the top 100 and introduces a mandatory 3-year service bond.

fiscal.comments: Studying at a top 100 global university can cost Rs. 40-60 lakh per year. If 100 students are funded annually, the cost is ~Rs. 50 crore/year, which is manageable but requires strict budgeting.

legal.comments: Enforcing a 3-year service bond for students who have studied abroad is legally complex and often difficult to execute if students choose to stay overseas.

administrative.comments: Requires a specialized department to track global admissions, verify rankings, and monitor the 3-year return service compliance.

timeline.comments: Can be rolled out within a year, but the 'knowledge exchange' benefits will only manifest after 2-4 years when students return.

political.comments: Likely to be viewed as a 'merit-based' elite welfare scheme, which may face criticism for not prioritizing BPL students.

feasibility.overall_comments: A visionary but expensive scheme where the primary challenge lies in enforcing the return-service bond and managing the high per-capita cost.
key_risks: Brain drain: Students failing to return despite the bond
High fiscal burden per beneficiary
Legal challenges regarding bond enforcement

points.text: College fees will be fully waived for first-generation Adi Dravidar and Tribal students who studied in government schools and join government colleges, as well as for first-generation students from economically disadvantaged families.

evidence.snippet: The state government bears the tuition fee for students who are the first in their family to pursue a degree in professional courses.

plan_existence.notes: The existing First Graduate scheme primarily targets professional courses (Engineering/Medical). This promise expands it to all government college courses and specifically highlights Adi Dravidar/Tribal and economically disadvantaged students for a 'full' waiver (potentially including non-tuition fees).

fiscal.comments: Government college fees in Tamil Nadu are already heavily subsidized (often <Rs. 5,000/year). Waiving the remainder for first-gen students is fiscally low-impact and highly feasible.

administrative.comments: The 'First Graduate Certificate' system is already well-established through the Revenue Department (Tahsildars).

timeline.comments: Can be implemented immediately via a Government Order (G.O.).

political.comments: Strongly aligns with the state's long-standing social justice and affirmative action narrative.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible and effective expansion of an existing successful model with minimal fiscal strain.
key_risks: Bureaucratic delays in issuing First Graduate certificates
Ensuring 'full waiver' covers hidden costs like lab or exam fees

points.text: A dedicated university (Periyar Institute of Emerging Technologies - PIET) will be created for emerging technologies such as artificial intelligence, quantum computing, biotech, climate technology, space technology and cybersecurity. This university will work in partnership with leading universities and technology firms in the world.
points.title: 5 Dedicated University for Emerging Technologies

evidence.title: NIELIT to establish first Quantum and AI University Campus

plan_existence.notes: While Tamil Nadu has the iTNT Hub and various departments in existing universities (like Anna University) focusing on these technologies, there is currently no standalone, dedicated 'University for Emerging Technologies' in the state. fiscal.comments: Establishing a new specialized university requires significant capital expenditure (estimated >₹500-1000 crore) and recurring operational costs for high-end labs and global faculty.

legal.comments: Requires a state legislative act to establish a new university, which is standard procedure.

administrative.comments: Attracting global faculty and maintaining partnerships with top-tier international firms requires high administrative agility and competitive compensation models.

timeline.comments: Setting up a full university campus and starting academic programs typically takes 3-5 years; achieving 'world-class' status takes longer.

political.comments: Naming the institute after Periyar aligns with the state's ideological leanings and is likely to receive strong public and political support.

feasibility.overall_comments: The plan is ambitious and aligns with global trends, but the high fiscal requirement and the challenge of competing for global talent are significant hurdles.
key_risks: High initial and recurring financial burden
Difficulty in recruiting specialized global faculty
Potential overlap with existing technology departments in Anna University
