points.text: To reduce the steep property tax increases imposed by the DMK government, the TVK government will introduce a new and fair property tax framework. This will be implemented with the aim of reducing the tax burden on residential property owners.
points.title: A Property Tax Reduction

evidence.title: Chennai corporation raises property tax by 6%
evidence.snippet: The corporation invoked Rule 264(2) of the Tamil Nadu Urban Local Body Rules, 2023, which permits an automatic annual 6% increase.

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu govt hikes property tax, Palaniswami calls it 'gift by DMK'
evidence.snippet: Tamil Nadu government on Saturday increased the property tax in various corporations, municipalities and panchayats to the range of 25-150 per cent.

plan_existence.notes: The current government implemented a major hike in 2022 and an automatic 6% annual increase starting 2024. This promise seeks to reverse or modify those specific policies.

fiscal.comments: Reducing property tax will severely deplete the revenue of Urban Local Bodies (ULBs). Furthermore, the 6% annual hike was a condition set by the 15th Finance Commission for states to receive central grants.

legal.comments: The state government has the legal authority to amend the Tamil Nadu Urban Local Bodies Rules, 2023, to change tax structures.

administrative.comments: Implementing a new tax framework is administratively straightforward through the existing municipal revenue departments.

timeline.comments: Tax reductions can be implemented immediately via government order or legislative amendment.

political.comments: Highly popular among urban voters who have been vocal against recent steep hikes.

feasibility.overall_comments: Politically astute but fiscally risky. Reducing local revenue may lead to a decline in urban service quality or the loss of significant Central Finance Commission grants.
key_risks: Loss of Central Government grants tied to tax reforms
Bankruptcy or severe funding crunch for Municipal Corporations
Inability to fund the infrastructure promises (P1, P2, P3) due to lower revenue

points.section_title: People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration
points.subsection_title: A. A 21st Century Government: Our administration will be built on honesty, accountability, transparency and putting people's welfare first.
points.text: Every government scheme of ours will reach the people directly and certainly. In that vein, our government will be the first in India to deliver welfare schemes directly to the people without forcibly demanding documents (No-Application). Today, for people to receive government schemes, they must fulfil eligibility criteria, properly fill out applications, contact the relevant office, and even pay bribes to officials. This is an unfair system that deceives people, not welfare-oriented governance. In this context, within the next five to seven years, our TVK government will advance Tamil Nadu to be the first state in India 'to implement welfare schemes without forcibly demanding any documents from the people (No-Application)'. With the help of technology, through digital information including Aadhaar, ration card, family details and family activities, we will identify the persons eligible for welfare schemes. Eligible beneficiaries will receive the benefits of schemes without any application procedure. Through the 'Chief Minister's People's Service Friend', the benefits of schemes will be delivered to beneficiaries at their doorstep. [cite: 3068, 3069, 3070, 3071, 3072, 3073, 3074, 3075]
points.title: 1 No-Application Scheme

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: A. A 21st Century Government: Our administration will be built on honesty, accountability, transparency and putting people's welfare first.
People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration

evidence.title: Social Sector Service Delivery - NITI Aayog
evidence.snippet: Documents various proactive service delivery models across states, but none currently implement a universal 'no-application' system for all welfare schemes.

evidence.title: Haryana Parivar Pehchan Patra (PPP)
evidence.snippet: A family-id based system in Haryana that enables proactive delivery of some services like old-age pensions without separate applications.

plan_existence.notes: While specific states like Haryana use a Family ID (PPP) for proactive delivery of certain benefits, a universal 'No-Application' system for all government schemes is a novel governance concept in India. Current systems in Tamil Nadu like 'Makkaludan Mudhalvar' still rely on citizen-initiated applications at camps.

legal.comments: Automated identification using Aadhaar and family data must comply with the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act 2023 regarding purpose limitation and consent.

administrative.comments: Extremely high complexity in breaking down departmental data silos and ensuring real-time data accuracy across all welfare sectors.

timeline.comments: The 5-7 year timeline is realistic for the massive digital infrastructure and backend integration required.

political.comments: Highly popular concept as it eliminates middle-men and reduces the 'bribe culture' mentioned in the manifesto.

feasibility.overall_comments: A transformative but administratively daunting promise. While technically possible with advanced data integration, the primary risk lies in data privacy and the potential for exclusion errors in automated eligibility checks.
key_risks: Data privacy and security breaches
Exclusion errors due to outdated digital records
Resistance from bureaucracy due to reduced discretionary power
High cost of maintaining a 500,000-strong 'Service Friend' workforce

points.section_title: People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration
points.subsection_title: A. A 21st Century Government: Our administration will be built on honesty, accountability, transparency and putting people's welfare first.
points.text: It currently takes weeks for people to obtain basic government documents like birth certificates, land pattas and caste certificates. Within a hundred days of the TVK government assuming office, a law will be enacted to ensure that people receive the benefits of 500 government services within a specified time. [cite: 3077, 3078]
points.title: 2 Right to Service Act; Service to All at the Legal Time

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 2 Right to Service Act; Service to All at the Legal Time
A. A 21st Century Government: Our administration will be built on honesty, accountability, transparency and putting people's welfare first.
People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu Right to Public Services Act, 2011

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Right to Public Services Act, 2011
evidence.snippet: An Act to provide for the delivery of public services to the people of the State within the stipulated time limit.

evidence.title: TN planning service rights bill for public
evidence.snippet: The current DMK government also proposed a new Right to Services Act in 2021 to strengthen the existing 2011 framework.

plan_existence.notes: Tamil Nadu already has a Right to Public Services Act from 2011, but it is often criticized for poor implementation. The promise to enact a 'new' law for 500 services suggests a replacement or significant strengthening of the existing statutory framework.

fiscal.comments: Enacting legislation has negligible direct fiscal cost; the primary costs are administrative overheads for monitoring.

legal.comments: States have full constitutional authority to enact such laws under the State List (Entry 41: State Public Services).

administrative.comments: Guaranteeing 500 services is ambitious; currently, most states only effectively guarantee 50-100 core services.

timeline.comments: Enacting a law within 100 days is standard for a new government with a majority.

political.comments: Broad public support as it directly addresses common grievances regarding delays and bribery.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible and legally sound. The challenge is not in passing the law, but in the administrative enforcement of penalties on officials who fail to meet the timelines for 500 different services.
key_risks: Bureaucratic pushback against penalty clauses
Inadequate digital infrastructure to track all 500 services
Potential for 'token' compliance where applications are rejected to meet deadlines

points.text: To prevent people from having to run around government offices for government services, through the 'Vettri Tamil Nadu Super App', each individual can obtain 500 services — including birth certificates, ration cards and driving licence renewals — from their mobile phone. [cite: 3084]
points.title: 3 Vettri Tamil Nadu Super App; Government in Your Mobile

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 3 Vettri Tamil Nadu Super App; Government in Your Mobile
A. A 21st Century Government: Our administration will be built on honesty, accountability, transparency and putting people's welfare first.

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu e-Governance Agency (TNeGA)
evidence.snippet: TNeGA already provides hundreds of services online through the e-Sevai portal and various mobile applications.

evidence.title: UMANG App - Government of India
evidence.snippet: A central 'Super App' that provides access to over 1,200 central and state government services.

plan_existence.notes: Tamil Nadu already has a robust e-governance infrastructure (TNeGA). The 'Vettri Tamil Nadu Super App' would be an expansion and consolidation of these existing disparate services into a single, user-friendly mobile interface.

fiscal.comments: Development of a Super App is a one-time tech investment (estimated ₹50-100 crore) which is well within the state's IT budget.

legal.comments: No major legal hurdles; requires standard data sharing agreements between departments.

administrative.comments: TNeGA has the technical capacity; the challenge is the backend digitisation of older records (e.g., land pattas).

timeline.comments: A functional version can be launched within 1-2 years, with services added incrementally.

political.comments: Aligns with the 'Digital India' trend and is highly appealing to the tech-savvy youth demographic.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a highly feasible technological upgrade. The success depends entirely on the 'last-mile' digitisation of department records rather than the app development itself.
key_risks: Cybersecurity and data privacy risks
Digital divide (accessibility for rural/elderly users)
Inter-departmental coordination for API integrations

points.section_title: People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration
points.subsection_title: A. A 21st Century Government: Our administration will be built on honesty, accountability, transparency and putting people's welfare first.
points.text: Like the Aadhaar card, an Entitlement Card will be issued to every family. Through this, eligible families can easily receive the benefits of all their services. This will ensure that our government administration is fair to all, with no room for political interference. [cite: 3086, 3087]
points.title: 4 Service Without Exclusion Through Entitlement Cards

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 4 Service Without Exclusion Through Entitlement Cards
A. A 21st Century Government: Our administration will be built on honesty, accountability, transparency and putting people's welfare first.
People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Public Distribution System - Smart Card
evidence.snippet: Tamil Nadu already uses Aadhaar-linked Smart Ration Cards as the primary document for family-based welfare identification.

evidence.title: Haryana Parivar Pehchan Patra (PPP)
evidence.snippet: A dedicated Family ID system used to track and deliver all state welfare benefits.

plan_existence.notes: The 'Entitlement Card' is essentially a 'Family ID' system. While Tamil Nadu's Smart Ration Card currently serves this purpose for 90%+ of the population, this proposal suggests a more comprehensive, non-PDS specific card similar to Haryana's PPP.

fiscal.comments: The cost of issuing physical cards to ~2.2 crore households is manageable (approx ₹200-400 crore).

legal.comments: Requires a legal framework for a state-level 'Family Database' similar to the Haryana PPP Act.

administrative.comments: Significant effort required to verify family data and prevent duplication with existing Aadhaar/Ration card databases.

timeline.comments: Can be rolled out within 2-3 years based on existing PDS data.

political.comments: May face criticism for being redundant given the existence of Smart Ration Cards and Aadhaar.

feasibility.overall_comments: Technically feasible but potentially redundant. Its value depends on whether it replaces the Ration Card as the sole eligibility document or merely adds another layer of documentation.
key_risks: Public confusion over multiple cards (Aadhaar, Ration, Entitlement)
Data synchronization issues with the National Population Register
Potential for political misuse of family-linked data

points.section_title: People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration
points.subsection_title: A. A 21st Century Government: Our administration will be built on honesty, accountability, transparency and putting people's welfare first.
points.text: The TVK government will launch the 'Tamil Nadu Governance Monitoring Platform' (Real Time Governance Dashboard) as an online information system. Through this, the government's revenue and expenditure accounts, the amount spent per constituency and per district, public complaints and questions related to these, and the government's responses — all of these will be uploaded live in real time. [cite: 3089, 3090]
points.title: 5 Tamil Nadu Governance Monitoring Platform

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: A. A 21st Century Government: Our administration will be built on honesty, accountability, transparency and putting people's welfare first.
People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration

evidence.title: CM Dashboard Tamil Nadu 360
evidence.snippet: The CM Dashboard monitors various department schemes, water levels, and rainfall in real-time for administrative oversight.

evidence.title: TNeGA - Tamil Nadu e-Governance Agency
evidence.snippet: TNeGA implements various IT projects for transparency and efficient delivery of government services.

plan_existence.notes: The current CM Dashboard is primarily for internal administrative monitoring. The promise expands this to a public-facing portal with granular constituency-level expenditure and real-time revenue tracking.

legal.comments: No legal barriers; aligns with the Right to Information Act and proactive disclosure norms.

administrative.comments: TNeGA already has the infrastructure; integrating real-time financial data from the treasury (IFHRMS) is technically feasible.

timeline.comments: Can be developed and launched within 12-18 months given existing digital foundations.

political.comments: Generally popular as it promotes transparency, though it may face internal resistance from departments wary of public scrutiny.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible and builds on existing digital infrastructure. The main challenge is the accuracy and 'real-time' nature of financial reporting at the constituency level.
key_risks: Data accuracy and lag in manual entry points
Security risks associated with live financial data
Political sensitivity of constituency-wise spending comparisons

points.section_title: People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration
points.subsection_title: B. Citizens as the Government's Guides
points.text: To enable people to convey their demands directly to the government, bypassing their legislator, a digital platform called 'Makkal Arangam' will be created. Through this, people can directly raise their complaints. *Demands signed and uploaded jointly by 10,000 people will be taken up for government consideration. *Demands signed and uploaded by 5 lakh people will be debated in the Legislative Assembly as a one-day 'Makkal Arangam' discussion. [cite: 3093, 3094, 3095, 3096]
points.title: 1 Makkal Arangam; Direct Public Complaints to be Debated for a Full Day in the Legislative Assembly

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 1 Makkal Arangam; Direct Public Complaints to be Debated for a Full Day in the Legislative Assembly
B. Citizens as the Government's Guides
People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration

evidence.title: Mudhalvarin Mugavari (CM's Helpline)
evidence.snippet: A unified portal for grievance redressal where citizens can track their complaints.

plan_existence.notes: While grievance portals exist, there is currently no mechanism in Tamil Nadu or India where a specific number of signatures triggers a mandatory debate in the Legislative Assembly.

legal.comments: Requires significant amendments to the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly Rules of Procedure. Constitutional validity of bypassing elected MLAs for direct petitions may be debated.

administrative.comments: Verifying 5 lakh unique, authentic signatures digitally to prevent bot-spamming or fraud is a major technical and administrative hurdle.

timeline.comments: Platform can be built quickly, but legislative rule changes and verification protocols will take time.

political.comments: Likely to face opposition from MLAs who may feel their role as intermediaries is being undermined.

feasibility.overall_comments: A bold direct-democracy initiative that faces severe legal and administrative hurdles, particularly regarding the verification of signatures and the disruption of established legislative business.
key_risks: Identity fraud and mass-bot signing of petitions
Legislative gridlock if too many petitions reach the 5-lakh threshold
Conflict between direct public demands and executive policy/budgetary constraints

points.section_title: People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration
points.subsection_title: B. Citizens as the Government's Guides
points.text: Through the 'Vettri Tamil Nadu Platform', people will be able to share their ideas with the government, express approval or disapproval of government schemes, and periodically rate government services. [cite: 3102]
points.title: 2 Vettri Tamil Nadu Platform

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: B. Citizens as the Government's Guides
People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration

evidence.title: MyGov Tamil Nadu
evidence.snippet: A platform for citizen engagement that allows people to share ideas and participate in governance.

plan_existence.notes: The existing MyGov TN platform already allows for sharing ideas and feedback. The 'rating' of government services is a specific feature that would be an expansion of current capabilities.

fiscal.comments: Low cost; involves software development and maintenance which is well within the state's IT budget.

legal.comments: No legal impediments to seeking public feedback.

administrative.comments: Requires a dedicated team to process feedback and ensure it reaches relevant departments for action.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented within 6-12 months.

political.comments: Low risk; generally viewed as a positive step toward modern governance.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible as it largely rebrands or slightly expands existing citizen engagement portals. Success depends on whether the government actually acts on the feedback received.
key_risks: Feedback fatigue if citizens see no tangible changes
Potential for organized groups to skew ratings/approval
Data privacy of users sharing ideas

points.section_title: People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration
points.subsection_title: B. Citizens as the Government's Guides
points.text: In all districts, people will decide which schemes 10% of the development funds allocated to the district should be used for. During the budget, this will be separately categorised as a 'Participatory Budget' and schemes will be implemented. In each district, a committee of 10 persons — consisting of women, youth, Scheduled Caste and Scheduled Tribe people, and domain experts — will be formed. Their job will be to monitor and evaluate district expenditure and to convey people's demands to the government. [cite: 3104, 3105, 3106, 3107]
points.title: 3 People's Right to Choose; 10% of District-Level Expenditure Allocated to People-Chosen Schemes

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 3 People's Right to Choose; 10% of District-Level Expenditure Allocated to People-Chosen Schemes
B. Citizens as the Government's Guides
People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu State Planning Commission - District Planning
evidence.snippet: The commission facilitates district-level planning but does not have a mandatory 10% participatory budget for the general public.

plan_existence.notes: While Kerala has implemented similar participatory budgeting (People's Plan), Tamil Nadu currently relies on top-down district planning and MLA/MP local area development funds.

fiscal.comments: The promise specifies a 10% allocation of existing district funds rather than new spending, but the total quantum of 'district development funds' is not cited.

legal.comments: Can be implemented through executive orders or amendments to the District Planning Committee rules under the 74th Constitutional Amendment.

administrative.comments: Setting up and managing committees in every district with specific demographic representation requires significant coordination and oversight.

timeline.comments: Can be rolled out in phases over 2-3 years.

political.comments: May face resistance from district administrations and local politicians who lose control over 10% of their discretionary spending.

feasibility.overall_comments: A significant shift toward decentralization. While legally possible, the administrative challenge of ensuring the 10-person committees are truly representative and not politically hijacked is high.
key_risks: Political capture of the 10-person monitoring committees
Bureaucratic delays in approving 'people-chosen' schemes
Potential for funds to be used for populist rather than sustainable infrastructure

points.section_title: People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration
points.text: Because people are unable to complain boldly against bribery, corruption continues to flourish. Against this, under TVK rule, complaint numbers will be displayed in the central area of every government office. If people report the practices of officials demanding bribes to this number — which is linked to the Chief Minister's office — whistleblowers will not only be given appropriate recognition, they will also be protected under the State Whistleblower Protection Act. [cite: 3111, 3112]
points.title: 1 Anti-Corruption People's Helpline

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration

evidence.title: Directorate of Vigilance and Anti-corruption Tamil Nadu Toll free No.
evidence.snippet: The official toll-free numbers designated for the Directorate of Vigilance and Anti-Corruption in Tamil Nadu are 1064 and 1965.

evidence.title: Whistle Blowers Protection Act, 2014
evidence.snippet: The Whistle Blowers Protection Act, 2014 remains unenacted nearly a decade after parliament passed it, as amendments are still pending.

plan_existence.notes: While anti-corruption helplines exist via DVAC and the CM's general helpline (1100), the promise to mandate displays in every office and enact a specific State Whistleblower Protection Act (as the central 2014 Act is largely unimplemented) represents a legal and administrative expansion.

fiscal.comments: Low cost; primarily involves signage and integrating existing telecom infrastructure with the CM's office.

legal.comments: Enacting a State Whistleblower Protection Act is within the state's legislative competence, though it must align with central frameworks.

administrative.comments: Highly feasible; requires a simple mandate for government offices to display contact information.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented within the first year of governance.

political.comments: Likely to receive strong public support; low risk of opposition.

feasibility.overall_comments: A highly feasible and low-cost governance reform that builds on existing infrastructure while adding necessary legal protections for whistleblowers.
key_risks: Ensuring the anonymity of callers
Preventing misuse of the helpline for personal vendettas
Capacity of the CM office to handle high volumes of complaints

points.section_title: People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration
points.text: The Lokayukta will be strengthened through a transparent appointment process and by granting the power to prosecute erring officials and elected people's representatives. [cite: 3114]
points.title: 2 Strengthening the Lokayukta

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Lokayukta - Wikipedia
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu Lokayukta was established under the Tamil Nadu Lokayukta Act, 2018. It has powers to inquire but is often criticized for lacking independent prosecution wings.

plan_existence.notes: The TN Lokayukta exists but currently requires government sanction for many investigations and lacks a fully independent prosecution wing. The promise aims to amend the 2018 Act to grant direct prosecution powers.

fiscal.comments: Minimal fiscal impact; involves legislative changes and potentially a small increase in staff for a prosecution wing.

legal.comments: Granting prosecution powers without government sanction may face legal challenges regarding the 'sanction to prosecute' clauses in the CrPC and Prevention of Corruption Act.

administrative.comments: Requires setting up a dedicated legal/prosecution cell within the Lokayukta.

timeline.comments: Legislative amendments can be passed quickly, but institutionalizing an independent wing takes time.

political.comments: High political risk; incumbents and legislators often resist a body that can prosecute them without executive control.

feasibility.overall_comments: Technically feasible but politically difficult. The core challenge is the legal conflict with existing central laws regarding the protection of public servants.
key_risks: Judicial challenges to the amendment
Political resistance from within the ruling coalition
Potential for the body to be used for political targeting

points.section_title: People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration
points.text: Beyond taking action after government malpractice happens, under TVK rule, the technology-assisted Anti-Corruption Monitoring Wing will continuously monitor procurement, welfare-scheme distribution records and financial transactions, and will prevent irregularities before the offence takes place. [cite: 3122]
points.title: 3 Tamil Nadu Anti-Corruption Monitoring Wing; Corruption Prevented Before It Happens

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 3 Tamil Nadu Anti-Corruption Monitoring Wing; Corruption Prevented Before It Happens
People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu: DVAC to probe all police telecom tenders since 2008
evidence.snippet: DVAC currently probes irregularities after they occur, such as the police modernization procurement scam.

plan_existence.notes: Existing bodies like DVAC are primarily reactive. While systems like IFHRMS track transactions, a dedicated 'Anti-Corruption Monitoring Wing' for real-time, technology-assisted prevention of irregularities is a new institutional concept.

legal.comments: Establishing a new wing under the executive or DVAC is legally straightforward.

administrative.comments: Extremely complex to implement real-time monitoring across all departments, procurement, and welfare schemes; requires massive data integration.

timeline.comments: Building the tech infrastructure and integrating it with all state departments would likely take the full 5-year term.

political.comments: Generally popular as a 'modern' solution to corruption, though may face internal bureaucratic pushback.

feasibility.overall_comments: A technologically ambitious plan that shifts from reactive to proactive monitoring. Success depends entirely on the quality of data integration and the independence of the monitoring wing.
key_risks: Data privacy concerns
Bureaucratic resistance to real-time oversight
High technical failure risk in cross-departmental integration

points.section_title: People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration
points.text: Mineral resource and sand extraction will be permitted only through transparent auction, in a fair and legal manner. [cite: 3124]
points.title: 4 Ending Sand Plunder

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu Minor Mineral Concession Rules, 1959 (Amended 2024)

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu government introduces e-auction for minor mineral quarry leases
evidence.snippet: In August 2024, TN amended rules to mandate e-auctions for minor minerals like granite and rough stone to improve transparency.

evidence.title: Sand mining in Tamil Nadu - Wikipedia
evidence.snippet: Since 2019, river sand mining has been largely a government monopoly via the Water Resources Department.

plan_existence.notes: Tamil Nadu recently introduced e-auctions for minor minerals (granite, stone) in 2024. However, river sand is currently managed through a government-run depot system. The promise suggests extending transparent auctions to all mineral/sand extraction, potentially ending the government monopoly or the 'depot' model.

fiscal.comments: Transparent auctions typically increase state revenue; the 2024 e-auction move was specifically aimed at optimizing revenue.

legal.comments: Fully within state powers under the Mines and Minerals (Development and Regulation) Act, 1957.

administrative.comments: The state already has an e-auction platform; scaling it to all minerals is administratively feasible.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented immediately by amending the Minor Mineral Concession Rules.

political.comments: Extremely high risk; sand mining is controlled by powerful local interests (often termed the 'sand mafia') with deep political ties.

feasibility.overall_comments: Technically and fiscally sound, but the primary barrier is the entrenched political-criminal nexus in the sand mining sector. Success requires immense political will.
key_risks: Violent opposition from the sand mafia
Cartelization of bidders in 'transparent' auctions
Environmental degradation if monitoring is not strictly enforced alongside auctions

points.section_title: People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration
points.text: An Anti-Narcotics Force will be created under the direct supervision of the Chief Minister. Through this, by operating on a war footing, drug use will be completely eradicated. Those selling narcotics will not only be arrested but will also be booked under the Goondas Act. Anti-Drug Clubs will be set up in every school. The TVK government will create a 'Rehabilitation Fund' to help people recovering from addiction. Further, villages that eradicate drug abuse will be given a special fund of Rs. 10 lakh for infrastructure projects, along with a government evaluation certificate. [cite: 3127, 3128, 3129, 3130]
points.title: 1 Drug-Free Tamil Nadu

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu CM launches 'Drug-free Tamil Nadu' drive
evidence.snippet: The CM launched a dedicated drive and created an Anti-Drug Coordination Committee. Anti-drug clubs were mandated in schools and colleges.

evidence.title: Goondas Act for Drug Peddlers in TN
evidence.snippet: Tamil Nadu police already invoke the Goondas Act against habitual drug offenders and sellers.

plan_existence.notes: Most components (Anti-Drug Clubs, Goondas Act usage, CM-level supervision) already exist under the current 'Drug-Free Tamil Nadu' initiative. The specific 'Rehabilitation Fund' and the 'Rs. 10 lakh reward for villages' are new additions to the existing framework.

fiscal.comments: The Rs. 10 lakh reward for drug-free villages is the primary cost. With ~12,500 village panchayats, if 10% qualify annually, the cost is Rs. 125 crore, which is manageable within the state budget.

legal.comments: The Goondas Act (Act 14 of 1982) already allows for the detention of 'Drug-offenders'. No new legislation is required for the enforcement aspect.

administrative.comments: The Enforcement Bureau CID is already operational. Setting up a dedicated fund and evaluation mechanism for villages is administratively feasible using existing rural development channels.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented immediately as it builds on existing police and school infrastructure.

political.comments: Anti-drug measures enjoy universal public support and face no significant political opposition.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible as it largely rebrands and expands existing enforcement and awareness programs, adding a specific fiscal incentive for local governance units.
key_risks: Corruption in 'drug-free' certification for villages
Sustainability of rehabilitation funds
Over-reliance on punitive measures (Goondas Act)

points.section_title: People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration
points.text: The TVK government will follow a steady and graduated approach to reduce alcohol consumption, with the primary aim of protecting the welfare of women, families and homes. The TVK government will work to reduce the social and financial burdens caused by alcohol through a controlled reduction in alcohol availability, strong de-addiction programmes, and awareness led by women's groups. In particular, it will be ensured that TASMAC shops located near schools and temples are immediately closed or relocated, and a safe environment will be restored. [cite: 3132, 3133, 3134]
points.title: 2 Healthy, Drug-Free Tamil Nadu

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu Liquor Retail Vending (In Shops and Bars) Rules, 2003

evidence.title: TASMAC shop distance rules
evidence.snippet: Existing rules mandate distance requirements from educational institutions and places of worship, though enforcement is frequently challenged in court.

evidence.title: Phased Prohibition in TN
evidence.snippet: The current government has previously closed 500 TASMAC shops as part of a phased reduction strategy.

plan_existence.notes: The promise of 'graduated reduction' and relocation away from schools/temples aligns with existing policy goals and court mandates, though the TVK promise emphasizes a more aggressive timeline for relocation.

fiscal.comments: TASMAC revenue is a cornerstone of TN's budget (approx. Rs. 45,000+ crore annually). Any significant reduction in availability without alternative revenue sources poses a major fiscal risk.

legal.comments: The state has the absolute right to regulate liquor trade. Relocation is legally sound but often faces litigation regarding specific site definitions.

administrative.comments: Closing or relocating shops is administratively simple, but preventing the rise of illicit liquor (hooch) in areas with reduced TASMAC access is a significant challenge.

timeline.comments: Relocation of specific shops can be done within months; graduated reduction is a multi-year process.

political.comments: Strongly supported by women voters and religious groups, but opposed by revenue-focused departments and the liquor lobby.

feasibility.overall_comments: Socially desirable and politically popular, but fiscally challenging due to the state's heavy reliance on liquor revenue for welfare schemes.
key_risks: Revenue loss for state welfare schemes
Increase in illicit liquor/hooch tragedies
Legal disputes over relocation sites

points.section_title: People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration
points.text: Every custodial killing that happens in a state is proof of the failure of the government administering that state. Under our TVK rule, independent inquiries will be conducted to secure justice for the families affected by custodial deaths that took place during DMK rule. Strict action will be taken against all those involved in such brutal violence, without fear or favour. [cite: 3140, 3141, 3142]
points.title: 3 Justice for Those Killed in Custodial Deaths

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 3 Justice for Those Killed in Custodial Deaths
People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration

evidence.title: Mandatory Judicial Inquiry for Custodial Deaths
evidence.snippet: Section 176(1A) of the CrPC makes a Judicial Magistrate inquiry mandatory for any death in police custody.

plan_existence.notes: While judicial inquiries are mandatory for all custodial deaths under existing law, the promise to specifically reopen or conduct 'independent inquiries' into cases from a previous administration (DMK rule) is a political/administrative pledge rather than a new legal framework.

fiscal.comments: The cost of setting up inquiry commissions or special investigation teams is negligible in the context of the state budget.

legal.comments: Re-opening closed cases or overriding previous judicial inquiries may face legal challenges in the High Court unless new evidence is presented.

administrative.comments: The state has the power to appoint Commissions of Inquiry under the Commissions of Inquiry Act, 1952.

timeline.comments: Inquiries can be initiated within the first year, though judicial processes and final actions often take much longer.

political.comments: Highly contentious; likely to be viewed as political vendetta, potentially leading to friction with the police force and opposition parties.

feasibility.overall_comments: Legally possible through Commissions of Inquiry, but primarily a political tool targeting the previous administration rather than a systemic reform.
key_risks: Accusations of political vendetta
Demoralization of the police force
Legal stay orders from the High Court

points.section_title: People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration
points.text: To reduce the number of pending cases in courts and to make justice more accessible to the general public, new local courts will be established. [cite: 3144]
points.title: 4 Judicial Infrastructure Improvement & Reduction of Pending Cases

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 4 Judicial Infrastructure Improvement & Reduction of Pending Cases
People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration

evidence.title: Gram Nyayalayas in Tamil Nadu
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu government has previously issued orders to establish Gram Nyayalayas (village courts) to provide justice at the doorstep.

plan_existence.notes: The establishment of local courts is an ongoing process under both central acts (Gram Nyayalayas) and state-level judicial infrastructure planning. The promise aligns with existing efforts to decentralize the judiciary.

fiscal.comments: Requires significant capital expenditure for buildings and recurring costs for judicial salaries. Funding is typically shared between the Centre and State.

legal.comments: Requires concurrence and coordination with the Madras High Court. The state cannot unilaterally establish courts without judicial approval.

administrative.comments: The bottleneck is often the recruitment of judges and staff through the TNPSC/High Court, which is a slow process.

timeline.comments: Setting up new courts, from land acquisition to inauguration, typically takes 3-5 years.

political.comments: Generally supported by the public and the legal fraternity as it reduces travel and backlog.

feasibility.overall_comments: A standard administrative goal that depends heavily on cooperation with the High Court and consistent fiscal allocation.
key_risks: Delays in judicial appointments
Lack of coordination with the High Court
Inadequate infrastructure maintenance

points.section_title: People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration
points.text: The TVK government is committed to completely eliminating the production and sale of counterfeit, illegal and black-market liquor. Considering this as a most serious offence, strict legal action will be taken against the vendors, suppliers and abettors involved. [cite: 3146, 3147]
points.title: 5 Zero Tolerance for Illegal Liquor Sales

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 5 Zero Tolerance for Illegal Liquor Sales
People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Enforces Stricter Penalties for Illicit Liquor
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu Prohibition Amendment Act, 2024, introduces harsher punishments, including life imprisonment and substantial fines, for offenses related to illicit liquor manufacture, possession, and sale.

plan_existence.notes: The state already has a robust legal framework under the 1937 Act, which was significantly strengthened in July 2024 following the Kallakurichi hooch tragedy. The current law already mandates life imprisonment and a Rs 10 lakh fine in cases of death due to illicit liquor.

legal.comments: The state has full constitutional authority over 'Liquor' under the State List (Entry 8).

administrative.comments: Existing enforcement wings (Prohibition Enforcement Wing) are already operational; success depends on curbing local-level corruption.

timeline.comments: Enforcement is an ongoing process that can be intensified immediately upon taking office.

political.comments: High public support due to recent hooch tragedies, though it may face resistance from local illicit networks.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a standard law-and-order commitment that aligns with recent legislative amendments. It is highly feasible as it utilizes existing legal and administrative machinery.
key_risks: Corruption in local police/enforcement
Inter-state smuggling from neighboring states
Economic incentive for bootlegging in rural areas

points.section_title: People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration
points.subsection_title: E. Five Special Scheme Budget Statements
points.text: Under this budget, the announcement of funds for the schemes, their reach to beneficiaries, and the resulting social impact will be subjected to intense monitoring and review. They are: *Social Justice Budget: Funds allocated for the equitable advancement of women, Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes, and all marginalised people will be spent entirely without any diversion. *Balanced Regional Development Budget: Impartial development schemes will be drawn up for all backward districts including the 12 most backward ones. *Tamil Nadu Art and Heritage Budget: Funds for recovering and preserving excavation sites, Keezhadi excavations, and taking Tamil culture to the world stage. *Education and Healthcare Equal Opportunity Budget: Funds to close the quality gap between educational institutions and hospitals, to fill vacancies, and to provide world-class education and healthcare. *Ecological Budget: Protection of marine resources, greening of dry districts, flood-prevention management in cities, new agricultural practices, and addressing climate change. [cite: 3151, 3152, 3157, 3158, 3159, 3160, 3161, 3162]
points.title: 1 India's First Five Special Scheme Budget Statements

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 1 India's First Five Special Scheme Budget Statements
E. Five Special Scheme Budget Statements
People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu to present separate Budget for agriculture
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu government introduced a separate annual Budget for agriculture in 2021 to increase productivity and protect farmers' welfare.

plan_existence.notes: While TN already presents a separate Agriculture Budget and includes Gender/Child budget statements as annexures, the proposal to create five distinct 'Special Scheme Budget Statements' for these specific themes is a procedural expansion of the current model.

legal.comments: The state legislature has the power to determine the format and presentation of its budget under Article 202.

administrative.comments: Creating five separate budget statements requires massive inter-departmental coordination and risks fragmenting fiscal oversight.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented in the first full budget cycle (2027-28).

political.comments: Likely to be popular as it signals targeted focus on marginalized groups and regional backwardness.

feasibility.overall_comments: Procedurally ambitious but administratively taxing. It rebrands existing allocations into thematic 'budgets' rather than necessarily creating new funds.
key_risks: Administrative silos
Double-counting of funds across multiple budget statements
Complexity in auditing and legislative review

points.section_title: People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration
points.subsection_title: F. Economic Sovereignty: We will grow revenue, prevent losses, and build new resources.
points.text: In the 2026-27 financial year, Tamil Nadu's debt exceeds Rs. 10.71 lakh crore. The TVK government's functioning will not waste money meant to be spent on the people. Without imposing new taxes on the people of Tamil Nadu, it will prevent losses, grow revenue and build economic infrastructure in new ways. The TVK government will pursue revenue growth through modern methods — eradicating corruption, mobilising Non-Resident Tamils to attract new investments, reviving micro and small industries, and employing technology, ecology and new-knowledge efficiency. [cite: 3166, 3169, 3170, 3175, 3176]
points.title: 1 We Will Transform Tamil Nadu into an Economically Sovereign State.

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu's debt to touch Rs 10.71 lakh crore by March 2027
evidence.snippet: The outstanding debt of the Tamil Nadu govt for 2026-27 is estimated at Rs 10.71 lakh crore by March, 2027.

plan_existence.notes: The debt figure cited in the promise (Rs 10.71 lakh crore) matches the official projection for March 2027. Debt management and revenue mobilization are core, ongoing functions of the state government.

fiscal.comments: Reducing debt while promising no new taxes and high spending is fiscally contradictory. Interest payments already consume 21% of revenue.

legal.comments: The state has full authority over its fiscal policy within FRBM limits.

administrative.comments: Eradicating corruption and using 'modern methods' for revenue are standard goals but difficult to quantify or execute rapidly.

timeline.comments: Significant debt reduction is unlikely within a 5-year term given the current committed liabilities and interest burden.

political.comments: The 'no new taxes' promise is politically popular but limits fiscal room.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise correctly identifies the state's debt challenge but offers generic solutions (eradicating corruption, NRT investment) that lack specific, grounded revenue-generation mechanisms.
key_risks: Breaching FRBM fiscal deficit targets
Inability to fund welfare schemes without new revenue
Global economic shifts affecting NRT investment

points.section_title: People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration
points.subsection_title: F. Economic Sovereignty: We will grow revenue, prevent losses, and build new resources.
points.text: Moving away from relying only on taxes and subsidies, the TVK government will take Tamil Nadu towards a new wealth-creating economic model. The TVK government will set up a Strategic Investment Authority with a valuation of Rs. 50,000 crore. Through this, the government will invest directly as a stakeholder in high-growth sectors such as semiconductors, electric vehicles (EVs), aerospace, artificial intelligence (AI) and advanced manufacturing. Rather than merely facilitating investments, under TVK rule the Tamil Nadu government will jointly invest with industry, create new values, and share their ownership. This will create lakhs of high-quality jobs and generate new non-tax revenue sources for the state. [cite: 3178, 3179, 3180, 3181, 3182]
points.title: 2 Tamil Nadu Strategic Investment Authority (TNSIA)

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: F. Economic Sovereignty: We will grow revenue, prevent losses, and build new resources.
People's Government; Honest and Upright Administration

evidence.title: TIDCO to Invest ₹25 Crore Each in Agnikul and Raptee
evidence.snippet: TIDCO will invest ₹25 crore each in Agnikul Cosmos and Raptee Energy, marking the first set of investments under the state's Startup Investment Policy 2025.

plan_existence.notes: TIDCO currently acts as a venture catalyst with smaller equity stakes (e.g., Rs 25-50 crore). A dedicated 'Strategic Investment Authority' with a Rs 50,000 crore valuation for direct stakeholder participation is a significantly larger and more structured institutional proposal.

fiscal.comments: An outlay of Rs 50,000 crore represents nearly 1.4% of the state's projected GSDP, which is extremely high for a single equity-investment authority given the current debt levels.

legal.comments: The state can form statutory authorities for investment, though direct ownership in private sectors may face regulatory scrutiny.

administrative.comments: Requires high-level fund management expertise (Sovereign Wealth Fund style) which the current state bureaucracy is not equipped for.

timeline.comments: Setting up the authority is possible, but deploying Rs 50,000 crore effectively in high-tech sectors takes a decade, not 5 years.

political.comments: Direct government ownership in industry can be seen as a return to older economic models, potentially worrying private investors.

feasibility.overall_comments: While it targets the right sectors (EV, AI), the Rs 50,000 crore valuation is fiscally unrealistic for a debt-stressed state. It shifts the government's role from 'facilitator' to 'speculative investor'.
key_risks: High risk of capital loss in tech sectors
Crowding out private venture capital
Fiscal strain from the massive initial corpus requirement

existing_scheme_names: White Paper on Tamil Nadu Government's Finances (2021)

evidence.title: White Paper on Tamil Nadu Government's Finances (August 2021)
evidence.snippet: The DMK government released a comprehensive White Paper in August 2021 detailing the state's fiscal situation, debt, and the status of major Public Sector Enterprises.

plan_existence.notes: The practice of publishing a White Paper upon assuming office is a standard transparency measure in Tamil Nadu, previously executed by the DMK government in 2021 to highlight the fiscal legacy of the preceding AIADMK administration.

fiscal.comments: Publishing a report is a low-cost administrative task with negligible impact on the state budget.

legal.comments: The state executive has full authority to compile and publish financial data.

administrative.comments: The Finance Department and Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG) data provide the necessary infrastructure for this task.

timeline.comments: Such papers are typically released within the first 100 days of a new government.

political.comments: Generally popular as it promotes transparency and allows a new government to set a baseline for its performance.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a highly feasible administrative promise that follows established political precedent in Tamil Nadu. It serves more as a diagnostic tool than a direct welfare or infrastructure project.
key_risks: Political bias in data interpretation
Potential market reaction to high debt disclosures

Further, to strengthen the administration, it will appoint sector-specific experts and also introduce Professional Management Practices. [cite: 3187, 3188]

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu Public Service Commission (Additional Functions) Act, 2022

evidence.title: TN Assembly passes Bill to entrust TNPSC with PSU recruitment
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu Assembly passed a Bill in 2022 to move recruitment for State-owned PSUs and corporations to the TNPSC to ensure professionalism.

plan_existence.notes: While recruitment has been centralized to TNPSC to improve professionalism, the promise to appoint 'sector-specific experts' and implement 'Professional Management Practices' expands on current efforts to reform loss-making entities like TANGEDCO and TNSTC.

fiscal.comments: While the promise aims to reduce financial burden, the cost of hiring high-level experts and implementing structural reforms is not specified.

legal.comments: The state government has the legal authority to restructure its own undertakings and change management bylaws.

administrative.comments: Implementing professional management in traditionally bureaucratic and politically influenced PSUs is administratively challenging.

timeline.comments: Structural reforms are long-term processes but initial expert appointments can be made within 1-2 years.

political.comments: Likely to face resistance from labor unions and political appointees who currently hold influence in PSU boards.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is a necessary but difficult reform. While legally sound, the transition from bureaucratic to professional management often stalls due to political interference and union pressure in major utilities.
key_risks: Resistance from powerful labor unions
Political interference in expert selection
High debt levels in PSUs limiting the scope of structural changes
